Name: 19_volunteer_volunteers_training_donate
Representative_Docs: ['Volunteers make an incredible difference every single day at Orange County Animal Services. From walking dogs and socializing cats to helping at events and supporting adoptions, our volunteers help provide enrichment, comfort, and care to the animals who need it most. Whether you want to spend time with animals, support the community, or gain hands-on experience in animal welfare, there’s a place for you at OCAS. Getting Started Before applying, please review the information below about volunteer requirements, training, and opportunities. Once you’ve reviewed everything, you can complete the volunteer application. Please note that depending on the time of year and the species you are interested in volunteering with, there may be a wait before training spots become available. If you want your primary species to be DOGS , please fill out this form . If you want your primary species to be CATS , please fill out this form Already a volunteer? Login to our volunteer portal here . Volunteer Age Guidelines Ages 16 and Older May volunteer independently with no special supervision required. Ages 13–15 May volunteer alongside a parent or guardian who is at least 18 years old. The accompanying adult must also complete the volunteer application and training process. Ages 12 and Under Are not eligible to volunteer inside the shelter facility. However, there are still many ways to help animals outside the shelter environment. Volunteer Expectations & Hours Volunteer shifts are available Monday through Saturday. After completing 50 volunteer hours, eligible volunteers may gain access to Sunday shifts and additional opportunities. To remain an active volunteer, volunteers are expected to complete: At least 2 shifts per month Each shift is approximately 2 hours long All volunteers are expected to assist with basic shelter tasks, including: Laundry Dishes Spot cleaning animal areas General tidying and organization Animal care is teamwork. Sometimes you’re walking a happy dog down the trail, and sometimes you’re battling a mountain of towels that somehow reproduced overnight. Both are important. Volunteer Training Process Step 1: Submit an Application Volunteers begin by completing an online application. The application includes: A virtual orientation video A species-specific training video (cats or dogs) Short follow-up questionnaires after each video Step 2: Attend In-Person Training Once the virtual portion is complete, volunteers will gain access to the training calendar to register for an in-person group training session. During this hands-on session, volunteers will: Learn shelter procedures and safety protocols Work alongside a volunteer mentor Become familiar with volunteer roles and expectations Gain hands-on experience with animals Step 3: Complete Mentored Shifts After in-person training, volunteers are asked to complete two mentored shifts before volunteering independently. Once completed, volunteers may begin scheduling shifts that fit their availability while meeting minimum participation requirements. Choosing a Volunteer Track Volunteers are asked to begin by primarily focusing on one species. Interested in Cats? Fill out the Cat Volunteer Application. Interested in Dogs? Fill out the Dog Volunteer Application. Many volunteers eventually cross-train to work with both species after gaining experience. Dog Volunteering Cat Volunteering Commmunity & Support Fostering Dog Adventures Dog Volunteering Dog Volunteer Opportunities Dog volunteers help provide exercise, enrichment, socialization, and support for the dogs in our care. Opportunities may include: Dog Walking Enrichment Activities Meet & Greets / Adoption Support Grooming Assistance Training Support Photography & Social Media Content Foster and Rescue Support Adventure Programs & Special Events Some dog roles require additional training and hands-on experience before participation. If you want your primary species to be DOGS , please fill out this form . Cat Volunteering Cat volunteers help cats and kittens stay comfortable, enriched, and socialized while in our care. Opportunities may include: Cat Socialization Kitten Care Cat Kennel Support Enrichment Activities Photography & Social Media Content Pet Biography Writing Adoption Support Foster and Rescue Support Some cat roles require additional training and experience before participation. If you want your primary species to be CATS , please fill out this form Commmunity & Support Not every volunteer role involves directly handling animals. Community volunteers help support the overall operation of the shelter and outreach efforts throughout Orange County. Opportunities may include: Surgery Day Ambassador Clerical Assistance Laundry & Dishes Outreach & Special Events Rabies Clinic Assistance Animal Transport Emergency Shelter Support These roles are critical to keeping daily operations running smoothly and helping us connect with the community. Fostering Not ready to volunteer onsite? Fostering is another incredibly impactful way to help. Fosters provide temporary homes for animals who may need: A break from the shelter environment Recovery time for medical care Socialization Bottle feeding or neonatal care Extra support during kitten season You do not need to be an active volunteer to become a foster. Learn more about fostering at: www.orangecountync.gov/fosterpets Dog Adventures One of our most popular opportunities is participating in Dog Adventures. These programs give dogs a chance to get out of the shelter environment, explore the community, and spend quality one-on-one time with individuals who are 18 or older. Dog Adventures opportunities includes: Dog Day Out Hikes with Hounds Dog Sleepovers and more to come! These outings help dogs reduce stress, build confidence, and show off their personalities outside of the shelter. These individuals also help by taking photos, sharing information about the dogs, and providing valuable feedback about how the dogs do in different environments. Questions? Please contact volunteer@orangecountync.gov with any questions about volunteering or fostering opportunities.', 'Volunteers Volunteers make an incredible difference every single day at Orange County Animal Services. Opportunities may include: Dog Walking Enrichment Activities Meet & Greets / Adoption Support Grooming Assistance Training Support Photography & Social Media Content Foster and Rescue Support Adventure Programs & Special Events Some dog roles require additional training and hands-on experience before participation. If you want your primary species to be DOGS , please fill out this form . Cat Volunteering Cat volunteers help cats and kittens stay comfortable, enriched, and socialized while in our care. Opportunities may include: Cat Socialization Kitten Care Cat Kennel Support Enrichment Activities Photography & Social Media Content Pet Biography Writing Adoption Support Foster and Rescue Support Some cat roles require additional training and experience before participation. If you want your primary species to be CATS , please fill out this form Commmunity & Support Not every volunteer role involves directly handling animals. Community volunteers help support the overall operation of the shelter and outreach efforts throughout Orange County. Opportunities may include: Surgery Day Ambassador Clerical Assistance Laundry & Dishes Outreach & Special Events Rabies Clinic Assistance Animal Transport Emergency Shelter Support These roles are critical to keeping daily operations running smoothly and helping us connect with the community. Fostering Not ready to volunteer onsite? Fostering is another incredibly impactful way to help. Fosters provide temporary homes for animals who may need: A break from the shelter environment Recovery time for medical care Socialization Bottle feeding or neonatal care Extra support during kitten season You do not need to be an active volunteer to become a foster. Learn more about fostering at: www.orangecountync.gov/fosterpets Dog Adventures One of our most popular opportunities is participating in Dog Adventures. These programs give dogs a chance to get out of the shelter environment, explore the community, and spend quality one-on-one time with individuals who are 18 or older. Dog Adventures opportunities includes: Dog Day Out Hikes with Hounds Dog Sleepovers and more to come! These outings help dogs reduce stress, build confidence, and show off their personalities outside of the shelter. These individuals also help by taking photos, sharing information about the dogs, and providing valuable feedback about how the dogs do in different environments. Questions? Please contact volunteer@orangecountync.gov with any questions about volunteering or fostering opportunities.']

Name: 20_asab_animal_meeting_concern
Representative_Docs: ['ASAB Frequently Asked Questions Orange County Animal Services Advisory Board (ASAB) Frequently Asked Questions Purpose of this FAQ page: To inform the public about the ASAB, protocol and process of meetings, and communication with the ASAB. What is the ASAB? The ASAB is a volunteer, Board of County Commissioners (BOCC) appointed board that advises the BOCC on issues regarding animals and animal services in Orange County. What does the ASAB do? The ASAB hears matters of concern regarding animal issues from Orange County residents and advises the BOCC on matters of concern regarding animal issues and animal services in Orange County. The ASAB works with the Animal Services Director (or designee) on various policy concerns and issues regarding County animal services. The Board also hears Potentially Dangerous Dog Declaration appeals. What types of concerns will the ASAB consider? The ASAB considers various animal concerns in Orange County. In the past, the ASAB has considered animal tethering, animal nuisance noise, livestock and public safety issues, spay/neuter, feral and free roaming cats, keeping of exotics and wild animals, cat sheltering issues, etc. What types of concerns will the ASAB not consider? Specific decisions or actions by staff in the different operational areas (as opposed to general policies and guidelines) or personnel matters are not the subject of ASAB oversight. These matters should be referred to the Animal Services Director. The one exception is the potentially dangerous dog appeal process mentioned above. If I have a concern, idea, or feedback about an animal-related issue in Orange County, whom should I contact? You may contact Animal Services Director or the ASAB Chair. Can I just show up and speak at an ASAB monthly meeting? Yes. There are two separate sheets for sign-up. One is “Public comments for items on the agenda” and the other is “Public comments for items not on the agenda”. How long will I have to speak? The ASAB follows the same format for public input as the Orange County Board of Commissioners. A 3-minute limit is set for all public comments. If you feel that you cannot convey your thoughts in the 3-minute limit, you may present your ideas or concerns in writing to the Board. It is helpful to keep your comments short and to the point. May I bring printed material or handouts? You\xa0are\xa0also\xa0welcome\xa0to\xa0bring printed material or handouts that may support your comments for the ASAB, however, do not expect ASAB members to\xa0read them “on the spot”.\xa0You are asked to provide the printed materials or handouts to the ASAB Secretary. When will the ASAB consider my idea or concern? As a general rule, only those items appearing on the agenda will be discussed or voted on at that meeting. However, the ASAB may choose to discuss your idea or concern, time permitting, so long as no action is taken until a subsequent meeting. Following the meeting at which you have spoken, The ASAB Chair, Vice Chair, and the Animal Services Director will discuss your concern and determine if the ASAB should consider this as an agenda item at a future meeting. If you request action from the ASAB, either the ASAB Chair or the Animal Services Director will contact you with the decision and with a date if this will be a future agenda item. Please keep in mind that not all ideas and/or concerns brought to the ASAB result in an action or vote from the ASAB. What happens when my idea or concern is listed as an agenda item? The Animal Services Director will contact you with the date and time of the meeting at which the item will be considered. You may be invited to speak and possibly have a question and answer period with the ASAB members for a designated period of time. You will then be asked to allow the ASAB to discuss what has been presented without interruption. In some cases a subcommittee may be formed to look into the issue further and you would be welcome to provide input to that subcommittee as well. Where can I go to find more information about Orange County Animal Services and the ASAB? Please go to https://www.orangecountync.gov/1233/Advisory-Board-Hearing-Panel-Pool for more information, including contact information.', 'Create a Website Account - Manage notification subscriptions, save form progress and more. Name: 21_direct_care_worker_sharp
Representative_Docs: ['SHARP Direct Care Worker Awards The Orange County Department on Aging has partnered with the community group, Senior Health Advocacy and Resource Partners of Orange County (SHARP), to recognize Direct Care Workers serving clients in Orange County, NC. Direct care workers impact their client’s lives on a daily basis and make a difference in our community. Each year six awards will be presented one in each of five different categories and one person will be chosen as "Direct Care Worker of the Year." Visit the links to the left to see previous award winners. Scroll down to view the 2025 SHARP Awards Nomination Form. Categories & Special Criteria Awards will be given in five categories with special attention to the criteria as noted: Client Impact - Frequently recognized or praised for their efforts that make an impact on the quality of their clients lives. Going the Extra Mile - Passionate about providing extraordinary care to their clients in ways that exceed expectations. Leadership - Serves as a role model and demonstrates teamwork and willingness to help other direct care workers and staff. Longevity - Has performed work as a caregiver each year for the last 10 or more years and plans to continue working in the field. Rising Star - Has been a caregiver for less than three years and demonstrates enormous promise in the profession. If selected as the Award Recipient the individual agrees to be represented in photographs and videos, as well as included in any and all public relations ads and media campaigns. Eligibility To be eligible, direct care workers must have served clients in Orange County within the past year. Each person may be nominated in two categories. Each nominee must: Provide direct care to clients as a primary function of their job. Have at least six consecutive months of service in the past year. Maintain and project a professional demeanor and exhibit dependability. Actively demonstrate pride in being a direct care worker and selflessly serve others in the course of their duties. Selection Process The SHARP Award winners will be selected by representatives from Project EngAGE. Project EngAGE is a senior leadership program sponsored by Orange County Department on Aging. The SHARP Committee is comprised of professionals from senior care, healthcare and caregiving support organizations from the Greater Orange County community. Nomination Form The deadline to submit nominations is October 10, 2025. To submit the Direct Care Worker Nomination Form online, please click here . To download the Direct Care Worker Nomination Form, please click here . Please email completed nomination forms to kmcmillan@orangecountync.gov or return in-person at 2551 Homestead Road, Chapel Hill, NC 27516. Awards Ceremony A recognition ceremony will be held for award winners on Thursday, November 13 at the Seymour Center in Chapel Hill. For more information about the Direct Care Worker Awards, email Kendall McMillan or call (919) 245-4278. Name: 23_holiday_toy_toys_children
Representative_Docs: ['The Toy Chest attempts to provide new toys to each eligible child in Orange County. The number of eligible children continues to increase each year and the Department of Social Services relies on individuals, businesses, community groups, schools and churches to meet all these needs. Benefits of Donating Donating to the Toy Chest is a great way for young children, Girl Scout and Boy Scout Troops, classrooms and Sunday Schools to get involved. Through toy drives, toy donation box collection sites and “bring-a-gift” parties they can help children their age have a joyful holiday. Toy drives are also a great way for businesses to increase employee morale and give back to the community. Please consider donating toys to the Toy Chest so that each child in Orange County will have at least one new toy on Christmas morning. How to Donate Toys Step 1 Collect or purchase new, unopened toys or gift cards. Not sure what to get? Here is a list of Gift Ideas (PDF) . Step 2 Bring toys to either office location of the Department of Social Services during regular business hours by December 9th. Please deliver any gift cards directly to staff, or drop off toys at one of these Toys for Tots drop-off locations . Step 3 Be sure to leave your name and address for a receipt. Remember, all donations are tax deductible. Name: 25_rabies_testing_animal_bats
Representative_Docs: ['Rabies is a fatal virus that may affect animals and people. It is a requirement in North Carolina that all cats, dogs and ferrets over 4 months of age be vaccinated against rabies. It is also a requirement that these animals wear a valid rabies tag at all times. Rabies vaccinations are typically available from any licensed veterinarian. Please check this page to see more information about low-cost rabies clinics that may be offered by Orange County, NC. Should your pet come in contact with an animal that might be considered a rabies carrier, contact Animal Control immediately at 919-942-PETS (7387). If you have an emergency outside of regular business hours (such as exposure to rabies), call 9-1-1. Simply seeing wild animals out in daylight hours does NOT mean they are sick or rabid. Daylight sightings of healthy wild animals (such as foxes, skunks, raccoons, opossums, etc.) are common. As the human population continues to increase and expand into areas traditionally only inhabited by wildlife, it\'s only natural that people will see more wildlife going about their daily routines. Many wild animals are opportunistic, meaning that they will show themselves more if there are more food sources or other things that are attractive to them. Other reasons for being spotted in daylight hours include (but are not limited to): mating, socializing, dealing with or protecting their young, and adapting or moving to a new habitat. While it is important to know that seeing healthy wild animals in daylight hours is common, it is also important for humans to have respect for wildlife and keep their distance. While any mammal can carry and spread rabies, we most often see rabies in wildlife such as skunks, raccoons, foxes, and bats. Clinical signs of rabies in animals may include: severe aggression (often unprovoked) severe lethargy excess salivation paralysis or trouble walking tremors or convulsions fever excessive irritability loss of appetite unusual vocalization change in behavior restlessness If a pet is infected, a pet owner may first notice that their animal stops eating and just "doesn\'t seem right". The animal may then become restless and irritable, possibly behaving strangely and making funny sounding cries or barks. As the disease progresses, nervous system signs become more obvious with tremors appearing, difficult walking and swallowing, and even convulsions and paralysis developing. Affected animals may or may not try to bite or show other signs of aggression. Rabies shares symptoms with many other infectious diseases, so it\'s not always easy to identify. Did You Know?... Rabies virus can be transmitted through secondary saliva exposure when handling an animal, so do not touch your pet without gloves if it has had any possible exposure to rabies. If a rabies suspect animal is alive, do not attempt to capture the animal. Keep visual contact with the animal until an Animal Control Officer arrives. Bats are beneficial to our ecosystem and they deserve our respect. Some types of bats in North Carolina are considered federally endangered or federally threatened. However, bats can carry rabies so it is important for humans to keep their distance. A host species of rabies in our own region and others is the bat. Of the few cases of rabies in humans in our country in recent years, most have been traced to bats. If you discover a bat inside your house, be sure not to release it, but do remove yourself and any animals from the area. Always call Animal Control immediately if you find a bat in your home even if there is no evidence of a bite. NC Rabies Results And Testing Animals are only submitted for testing if there’s good reason to think that there could have been a rabies exposure. This means that Animal Services could impound but not submit for testing a sick fox even if this animal had neurological signs consistent with rabies. Why? Because there has not been any contact with a person or domestic animal and thus there is no need to decide whether a person needs shots (aka post-exposure prophylaxis) or a dog or cat needs a rabies booster shot or isolation for the rabies incubation period. Wild animals that can carry rabies and that have had contact with a dog or a cat are not always tested. Generally speaking, they are only tested if the dog or cat with which they had contact does not have a documented rabies vaccination history. In these situations, the test results are used to decide whether the dog or cat needs to be isolated for the rabies incubation period. \xa0On the other hand, a wild animal that can carry rabies is not submitted for testing if the dog or cat with which it had contact has a documented history of rabies vaccinations. The reason is that it is much less expensive for the dog or cat to get a rabies booster shot than it is to perform a fluorescent antibody test on the animal to determine if it could have been shedding rabies when it had contact with the dog or the cat. Test results do not tell us very much about the natural occurrence of rabies in a locale or larger area. This is so because only wild animals that have had contact with a person or domestic animal are considered for testing since the purpose of testing is to decide if post-exposure measures need to be taken for the health of the public and other domestic animals. Additional Information NC Department of Health and Human Services National Association of State Public Health Veterinarians World Health Organization rabies information', 'Home Government Departments A - F Animal Services Animal Control & Protection Rabies Rabies Rabies is a fatal virus that may affect animals and people. It is a requirement in North Carolina that all cats, dogs and ferrets over 4 months of age be vaccinated against rabies. It is also a requirement that these animals wear a valid rabies tag at all times. Rabies vaccinations are typically available from any licensed veterinarian. Please check this page to see more information about low-cost rabies clinics that may be offered by Orange County, NC. Should your pet come in contact with an animal that might be considered a rabies carrier, contact Animal Control immediately at 919-942-PETS (7387). If you have an emergency outside of regular business hours (such as exposure to rabies), call 9-1-1. Simply seeing wild animals out in daylight hours does NOT mean they are sick or rabid. Daylight sightings of healthy wild animals (such as foxes, skunks, raccoons, opossums, etc.) are common. As the human population continues to increase and expand into areas traditionally only inhabited by wildlife, it\'s only natural that people will see more wildlife going about their daily routines. Many wild animals are opportunistic, meaning that they will show themselves more if there are more food sources or other things that are attractive to them. Other reasons for being spotted in daylight hours include (but are not limited to): mating, socializing, dealing with or protecting their young, and adapting or moving to a new habitat. While it is important to know that seeing healthy wild animals in daylight hours is common, it is also important for humans to have respect for wildlife and keep their distance. While any mammal can carry and spread rabies, we most often see rabies in wildlife such as skunks, raccoons, foxes, and bats. Clinical signs of rabies in animals may include: severe aggression (often unprovoked) severe lethargy excess salivation paralysis or trouble walking tremors or convulsions fever excessive irritability loss of appetite unusual vocalization change in behavior restlessness If a pet is infected, a pet owner may first notice that their animal stops eating and just "doesn\'t seem right". The animal may then become restless and irritable, possibly behaving strangely and making funny sounding cries or barks. As the disease progresses, nervous system signs become more obvious with tremors appearing, difficult walking and swallowing, and even convulsions and paralysis developing. Affected animals may or may not try to bite or show other signs of aggression. Rabies shares symptoms with many other infectious diseases, so it\'s not always easy to identify. Did You Know?... Rabies virus can be transmitted through secondary saliva exposure when handling an animal, so do not touch your pet without gloves if it has had any possible exposure to rabies. If a rabies suspect animal is alive, do not attempt to capture the animal. Keep visual contact with the animal until an Animal Control Officer arrives. Bats are beneficial to our ecosystem and they deserve our respect. Some types of bats in North Carolina are considered federally endangered or federally threatened. However, bats can carry rabies so it is important for humans to keep their distance. A host species of rabies in our own region and others is the bat. Of the few cases of rabies in humans in our country in recent years, most have been traced to bats. If you discover a bat inside your house, be sure not to release it, but do remove yourself and any animals from the area. Always call Animal Control immediately if you find a bat in your home even if there is no evidence of a bite. NC Rabies Results And Testing Animals are only submitted for testing if there’s good reason to think that there could have been a rabies exposure. This means that Animal Services could impound but not submit for testing a sick fox even if this animal had neurological signs consistent with rabies. Why? Because there has not been any contact with a person or domestic animal and thus there is no need to decide whether a person needs shots (aka post-exposure prophylaxis) or a dog or cat needs a rabies booster shot or isolation for the rabies incubation period. Wild animals that can carry rabies and that have had contact with a dog or a cat are not always tested. Generally speaking, they are only tested if the dog or cat with which they had contact does not have a documented rabies vaccination history. In these situations, the test results are used to decide whether the dog or cat needs to be isolated for the rabies incubation period. \xa0On the other hand, a wild animal that can carry rabies is not submitted for testing if the dog or cat with which it had contact has a documented history of rabies vaccinations. The reason is that it is much less expensive for the dog or cat to get a rabies booster shot than it is to perform a fluorescent antibody test on the animal to determine if it could have been shedding rabies when it had contact with the dog or the cat. Test results do not tell us very much about the natural occurrence of rabies in a locale or larger area. This is so because only wild animals that have had contact with a person or domestic animal are considered for testing since the purpose of testing is to decide if post-exposure measures need to be taken for the health of the public and other domestic animals. Additional Information NC Department of Health and Human Services National Association of State Public Health Veterinarians World Health Organization rabies information', 'Rabies Rabies is a fatal virus that may affect animals and people. Name: 27_partner_ocas_dog_placement
Representative_Docs: ['Placement Partners / Rescues Orange County Animal Services (OCAS) recognizes and values the hard work and dedication of area rescue organizations, and we enjoy working with many groups in order to save lives and work together to place adoptable, healthy animals into loving, permanent homes. Benefits OCAS offers many cost saving benefits to rescues, including vaccines and some medical services, at no cost! In addition, we offer sterilization and microchipping services for very low fees for cats and dogs transferred to rescue groups. We invite your organization to join us in our efforts to improve the lives of companion animals in Orange County! More Information For information about becoming a Placement Partner and transferring animals to a nonprofit rescue organization, visit our Placement Partner resource page .', "WORKING WITH US Click on each section below for more information! Communication When a Placement Partner chooses to work with OCAS, we want to ensure that the partnership is one that lasts and that there are no surprises. Therefore, we partner under a specialized set of guidelines that have been developed to specifically address the needs and expectations of both OCAS and our Partner Organizations. We also ensure that every dog or cat a Partner Organizations receives comes with a full medical record, temperament record and memorandum of any special circumstances or additional treatments it may need in the future. Orange County Animal Services aims to provide a mutually beneficial program to all of our Placement Partners. We achieve this through our strict standards of care and by working with our partner organizations to place as many sound animals as possible. We strive to make it easier for our placement partners to continue to offer their services to the people and animals of Orange County and beyond. Disease Prevention & Control Disease prevention is one of our most serious concerns at OCAS. All animals that enter our Shelter Division are given a medical examination upon intake to screen for disease. Additionally, animals are given standard vaccinations. Guidance & Regulations Domestic companion animals must be moved to the state of destination in accordance with that state's laws. If you are taking a dog, cat or ferret from North Carolina to another state for any reason, you must comply with all importation requirements set forth by the state of destination, which often vary from state-to-state. View more information and important links on this page . No Cost Medical Services All cats and dogs receive the following standard services at no cost to Placement Partner Organizations: Bordatella vaccination (dogs and puppies three weeks and older) Deworming DHPP vaccination (dogs and puppies six weeks and older) Flea/Tick Treatment FVRCP vaccination (cats and kittens six weeks and older) Heartworm preventative Heartworm test (antigen test; dogs six months and older) Physical exam Rabies vaccination (if older than three months of age) In addition to these standard services, all animals are given a basic evaluation to screen for aggression, and most dogs are given a more in-depth behavioral evaluation during their stay with us. Sterilization & Microchips We offer discounted sterilization performed by licensed and experienced veterinarians. We are also able to provide microchip implantation to cats or dogs before they are transferred to a rescue organization. These optional services offer huge savings and convenience for our partnering organizations, and are available for the following rates: Microchipping - $35 Neuters (dogs and cats!) - $30 Spays (dogs and cats!) - $60", "WORKING WITH US Communication Disease Prevention & Control Guidance & Regulations No Cost Medical Services Sterilization & Microchips WORKING WITH US Click on each section below for more information! Name: 30_individuals_justice_criminal_cjrd
Representative_Docs: ['The Pretrial Services Program minimizes unnecessary pretrial detention by assessing and supervising individuals who are likely to appear for future court dates and do not pose a risk to the community as they await trial. This results in reduced jail costs for the County. In addition, individuals who are released from custody pretrial are able to maintain employment, housing, and family ties; access treatment; and aid in their own defense.', 'Mission The mission of the Orange County Criminal Justice Resource Department (CJRD) is to: safely reduce unnecessary pretrial incarceration increase jail alternatives and diversion opportunities reduce the number of individuals with behavioral health issues facing justice involvement and incarceration reduce recidivism reduce racial and economic disparities increase public safety by providing treatment and support for individuals involved in Orange County’s criminal legal system', "Programming CJRD is staffed by a multidisciplinary team of lawyers and criminal justice and behavioral health specialists. We provide services across the criminal justice system that seek to further our mission. See a summary below and the links or the left-hand side bar for details about our programs. Pretrial Services Program The Pretrial Services Program minimizes unnecessary pretrial detention by assessing and supervising individuals who are likely to appear for future court dates and do not pose a risk to the community as they await trial. This results in reduced jail costs for the County. In addition, individuals who are released from custody pretrial are able to maintain employment, housing, and family ties; access treatment; and aid in their own defense. Behavioral Health Services for Adults and Youth CJRD has mental health and substance use professionals that work directly with individuals in custody or at risk of incarceration. The Clinical Coordinator works directly with individuals incarcerated in the Orange County Detention Center who have mental health and substance use concerns. \xa0A primary goal of this position is to increase opportunities for pretrial release and connection to community resources. The Y outh Behavioral Health Liaison provides clinical services to youth ages 18 and under who are incarcerated, in detention, or at high risk in the criminal and juvenile justice systems with the goal of developing an appropriate treatment plan to support youth in returning to or remaining in the community. Diversion and Deflection Programs The goal of diversion and deflection is to increase community safety and well-being by diverting/deflecting individuals from the criminal legal system and offering earlier and direct access to necessary interventions. CJRD administers four different diversion programs that work with four different groups of people. The Orange County Adult Pre-Arrest Diversion (OC-PAD) program works with adults ages 18 and up who are first-time offenders of certain low-level misdemeanors. The Youth Deflection Program (YDP) works with youth ages 17 and under who have committed an offense, or are at risk of committing an offense, but may not need juvenile justice system involvement. The Lantern Project works with adults with a behavioral health diagnosis, history of substance use, and frequently prior justice involvement. The Community Care and Diversion Response (CCDR) team works with individuals who have severe or persistent mental health diagnoses and prior or pending justice system involvement. Successful participants in diversion programs may earn the benefit of not having a charge or a criminal conviction, which have collateral consequences for employment, housing, legal residency status, and eligibility for public benefits and licensing. Recovery Courts CJRD administers two distinct, therapeutic programs for court-involved adults diagnosed with substance use disorders. Adult Recovery Court is a diversionary court for individuals who are facing significant terms of incarceration. Participants are referred to the program by judges, defense attorneys, and probation officers. Successful participants will avoid incarceration and reduce their likelihood of recidivism. Family Recovery Court is a collaborative effort between the court system and the Department of Social Services (DSS) for parents who have a pending abuse/neglect/dependency case. Successful participants enhance their parenting skills and improve the chances of having their parental rights restored. The core components of both programs are formalized substance use treatment, random drug testing, frequent court appearances, peer support, and monitoring by a multidisciplinary team. Restoration Legal Counsel Restoration Legal Counsel provides pro bono legal assistance to individuals facing barriers due to a criminal record and/or driver's license suspension. The program works in collaboration with court and community stakeholders to provide restorative legal services to low-income, justice-involved individuals. This pro bono legal assistance to qualified individuals includes driver’s license restoration work, expunctions, certificates of relief, and criminal justice debt relief. Local Reentry Council The Local Reentry Council works with formerly incarcerated people to reduce barriers to reentry by providing case management, connecting people to services in the community, providing direct funding for needs, and building a network of individuals, agencies, and advocates within the local community that provide support and coordination of innovative responses to reentry. Street Outreach, Harm Reduction and Deflection (SOHRAD) Administered by the Housing Department in collaboration with CJRD , the SOHRAD program connects people experiencing homelessness in Orange County with housing and services. Peer support and clinical staff use a relationship-based model to provide ongoing engagement and case management for people living unsheltered. Partnership to End Homelessness CJRD collaborates with the Orange County Partnership to End Homelessness and participates in HOME Committee and Coordinated Entry Planning Committee meetings. Many unhoused individuals have encounters with law enforcement and are involved in the criminal justice system as a direct result of not having a place to call home. The collaboration between CJRD and the Partnership to End Homelessness provides an opportunity to serve this specific population, which includes some of the most vulnerable members of our community. To learn about housing and other resources, visit the OC Connect Database"]

Name: 32_tax_assistance_property_disabled
Representative_Docs: ['Home Government Departments G - Z Tax Administration Property Tax Assistance Programs State Provided Property Tax Assistance Programs The North Carolina General Assembly offers three programs: the Elderly and Disabled Exclusion, the Disabled Veteran Exclusion, and the Circuit Breaker Tax Deferment. Please contact us to discuss eligibility or for assistance in completing the application. Under certain circumstances, individuals may be eligible for reduced taxes or participation in other special programs if they meet the requirements specified in the North Carolina General Statues. Below is a list of the assistance programs available to Orange County residents: Elderly and Permanently Disabled Assistance Disabled American Veteran Assistance Solid Waste Programs Fee Financial Assistance Exempt Property Present Use Value Builder Property Tax Exclusion Please contact us for more information or click the Property Tax Assistance Evaluator button below to see if you might qualify for one or more of the tax assistance programs. You are not required to use the Property Tax Assistance Evaluator before submitting an application to the Orange County Tax Office. You may simply fill out, sign and submit a completed application. New County Provided Property Tax Assistance Program Longtime Homeowners Assistance Program Contact the Tax Office If you have questions or need help with your bill, your value, tax relief, or any other tax matter, please contact us. Orange County Tax Administration 228 S. Churton Street, Suite 200 P.O. Box 8181 Hillsborough, NC 27278 Phone: 919-245-2100 Email: tax@orangecountync.gov If you need an interpreter or a disability-related accommodation, please tell us when you call or visit. We want all County services to be accessible to everyone. Orange County Tax Administration 228 S. Churton Street, Suite 200 P.O. Box 8181 Hillsborough, NC 27278 Phone: 919-245-2100 Email: tax@orangecountync.gov If you need an interpreter or a disability-related accommodation, please tell us when you call or visit. We want all County services to be accessible to everyone. Name: 34_road_reserved_street_shall
Representative_Docs: ['North, South, East and West are intended to be directional features of the address system and will be used only when necessary, such as for distinguishing regions of a continuous road traversing several municipalities. \xa0A directional word should not be contained within the name of a new road and shall be placed ahead of the road name. \xa0A street or road may have no more than one directional prefix.', 'New Roads and Road Name Changes – Please review road naming guidelines carefully before submitting a Road Name Reservation Request. Your street name is not considered reserved until you receive a confirmation receipt.', 'Road names for new developments may be reserved upon written request for up to one year. \xa0If the requirements to finalize the road name are not satisfied within one year from the date of approval, upon written request an extension of one year may be granted. \xa0If such extension is not received, the road name may no longer be reserved.']

Name: 35_extension_tiana_ncacc_recognition
Representative_Docs: ['Home News Flash North Carolina Association of County Commissioners Annual Conference Wrap Up News Flash North Carolina Association of County Commissioners Annual Conference Wrap Up Home - News & Events Posted on August 27, 2025 The North Carolina Association of County Commissioners (NCACC) held its 118th Annual Conference August 21-23 in Pitt County . This year’s theme was “ Cultivate: Inspire County Growth.” Orange County was well represented throughout the event. Honoring Public Service Orange County Health Director Quintana Stewart was recognized through the Heroic Hands initiative , which celebrates public servants who go above and beyond in service to their communities. The initiative was launched by NCACC President and Warren County Commissioner Tare “T.” Davis to spotlight the critical work of local government employees and emphasize the importance of careers in public service. Cooperative Extension Award Recognition The Orange County Cooperative Extension Center was named runner-up for the North Central District Extension County Partnership Award . This recognition highlights the strong partnerships between Extension centers and county governments, which help sustain a robust Extension presence in 101 locations across North Carolina. Each year, one county is chosen from each of the five Extension districts for recognition. County Extension Director Jonathon Smith nominated Orange County, citing the outstanding support provided in resources, advocacy, program development, leadership and community engagement. Student Scholarship Recipient Congratulations are in order for Tiana Oxendine , a recent Cedar Ridge High School graduate who achieved a 4.0 GPA. Tiana received a $2,500 scholarship from the North Carolina Association of Black County Commissioners , after being nominated by Orange County Commissioner Phyllis Portie-Ascott , who serves on the NCACC Board of Directors. In addition to her academic achievements, Tiana earned certification as a nursing assistant this summer and has been actively engaged in community service. She is now pursuing a nursing degree at UNC Greensboro . Name: 37_pet_introducing_start_days
Representative_Docs: ['Caring For Your Newly Adopted Pet PLEASE SEE OUR RECOMMENDATIONS ON INTRODUCING A NEW PET TO OTHER PETS . Patience is your best friend after adopting a new pet and slowly introducing that pet into your home. Think of the "3-3-3 Rule." This rule is a guideline for having patience and giving your pet time to adjust: 3 DAYS - Three days is probably the minimum amount of time you pet will need to start to decompress. During the first 3 days in a new home, pets often feel overwhelmed, scared, cautious, and uncomfortable. The pet may not have a good appetite, and may try to hide or test the boundaries of the new environment in some way. 3 WEEKS - At the 3-week mark, your pet may be starting to learn the routine of the home. They may start settling in more and be more comfortable, with an understanding that this is probably their permanent home. The pet may be less cautious and start showing more personality. 3 MONTHS - The pet more clearly feels at home and seems to feel comfortable. Your pet begins to build trust and form a true bond. Your pet starts settling into routines and and gains a sense of security with you and the rest of the household. Name: 38_academy_government_participants_cohort
Representative_Docs: ['The Orange County Government Academy is a comprehensive, eight-week program designed to educate residents about the inner workings of Orange County government. The program begins in the late winter and concludes with a graduation ceremony at a Board of County Commissioners meeting. Sessions include in-person tours of key county facilities. Participants must attend five (of eight) classes to graduate. The course is open to all county residents aged 16 and older. View the 2026 curriculum and schedule . About the Academy The Government Academy is a dynamic program that fosters community engagement and understanding of county government operations. Participants gain firsthand insights into more than 19 departments, engage with county staff, and explore how they can contribute to their community’s growth and well-being. Key Highlights of the Academy: Interactive Learning : Faculty from the UNC School of Government will provide an overview of county government, while Orange County staff will present insights into their departments. Exclusive Tours : Participants will explore facilities such as the 911 Call Center, Animal Services, and the Drakeford Library Complex. Graduation Ceremony : The cohort will graduate in person, followed by recognition at the Board of County Commissioners (BOCC) meeting. County Departments Represented Staff from 19 departments will present and answer questions. They include: Aging Animal Services Arts Commission Child Support Services Clerk to the Board Community Relations Criminal Justice Resource Economic Development Elections Emergency Services Environment, Agriculture, Parks & Recreation Health Housing Library Office of Civil Rights & Civic Life Planning & Inspections Social Services Solid Waste Tax Applications to join the 2026 cohort have closed. Sign up for This Week in Orange County newsletter to find out about the next cohort. Name: 39_emergency_alerts_disaster_kit
Representative_Docs: ['Emergency Services IF YOU ARE EXPERIENCING AN EMERGENCY CALL 9-1-1 Orange County Emergency Services is dedicated to ensuring the appropriate and efficient response to emergencies throughout Orange County operating twenty-four hours each day, year round. Divisions Orange County Emergency Services consists of: Public Safety Communications Division Emergency Management Division Emergency Medical Services Division Fire & Life Safety Division Business Services Division Disaster Response In addition, Emergency Services also plans for the Orange County response to a disaster and other complex emergencies. Disaster planning integrates all of the critical Orange County departments with the local municipalities as well as state and federal agencies.', 'Welcome to Ready Orange Your "one stop shop" for preparedness information in Orange County! Sign up for Emergency Alerts OC Alerts is the public alert and warning system for Orange County. All Orange County residents are encouraged to sign up for OC Alerts to get important public safety messages! Visit ocalertsnc.com to sign up! General Preparedness Information Be Informed The National Weather Service - For up to date weather forecasts, storm tracks, and preparedness info ReadyNC.org - For much more information in North Carolina including evacuation info and more on specific hazards Red Cross Disaster Services - Your source for Red Cross information OC Alerts - Orange County\'s Public Alert and Warning System Build a Kit When an emergency strikes your family may be without power or access to grocery stores, running water or other day-to-day necessities. The best way to prepare for that is to build a Family Emergency Kit. The Kit should have some basic items, but should match you and your family - like a fingerprint, no two kits will look exactly alike. The Kit will be helpful whether you are staying at home or you have to leave on short notice. Buy a Red Cross Emergency Preparedness Kit FEMA\'s Recommend ed Kit ReadyNC\'s Recommended Kit Orange County Emergency Supply Kit Checklist (PDF) Orange County Older Adults Emergency Supply Kit Checklist (PDF) Make a Plan If a disaster strikes your family may be split up already, or be split up as a result. The best way to assure that you can make contact is to make a plan for how you all will check-in and get back together. FEMA\'s Guide to Making a Plan Get Involved There are more ways than ever to become better prepared and more connected to an emergency response than ever. Community Emergency Response Team (CERT) Public Health Reserve Corps The Red Cross Report Problems Power Outages Duke Power - 800-769-3766 Duke customers may also receive text notifications for power outages in their area. Text REG to 57801 for text alerts. With outage alerts, we\'ll notify you by text, voice message or email when you have an outage. We\'ll also send status updates and estimated restoration times. Piedmont Electric - Main Office 800-449-2667 or Report Outage 800-222-3107 ( PEMC outage map ) PSNC - 877-776-2427 ( Signs of a gas leak ) Downed Trees & Debris For downed trees and other debris in a roadway, assure that you are in a safe location and call 911 For downed trees on private property contact the primary insurer of the property and then contact a private debris removal company Flooding Report flooding by calling 911 Emergency Shelters Shelters Orange County will coordinate the opening of emergency shelters with support from the Department of Social Services, Health Department, Animal Services, and the American Red Cross when the need arises. There are 2 primary Orange County Emergency Shelters: Orange Middle School 308 Orange High School Road Hillsborough, NC 27278 Smith Middle School 9201 Seawell School Road Chapel Hill, NC 27516 Considerations Shelters require you to be as informed and prepared as possible: If you need special assistance in a disaster call 919-245-2884 to register for assistance before a disaster strikes. Shelter locations may change depending on the emergency Listen to the radio for further directions concerning shelter openings: WCHL-AM (1360), WCHL-FM (97.9), WDCG-FM (105.1), WRAL-FM (101.5), WYMY-FM (La Ley 101.1 in Spanish) Shelters will have food or water, but will not have easy access to medications or many other personal needs. If you must have transportation, emergency transport may be provided to you by calling 919-245-6100. Bring Picture ID Regularly taken medications Insurance information Bedding Towels Comfortable Clothing Personal radios - with headphones Games , reading material or other things to pass the time - especially for children Specialty items for your needs Do not bring weapons, illegal drugs, candles or valuables Smoking is not allowed inside the shelter Pets/Animals Animal Preparedness Orange County Animal Services provides emergency animal sheltering at all Orange County emergency services Service animals for the hearing and visually impaired will be allowed in the shelter All other animals are welcome at the shelter and Animal Services Staff will be on hand to board pets in the best manner possible Owners of large animals and livestock should make preparations for their animals as very limited support will be provided in a disaster Pets and Animal Preparedness Training FEMA Center for Domestic Preparedness FEMA Emergency Management Institute Calendars National Fire Academy North Carolina Emergency Management & Incident Command Training Calendar Login to WebEOC Orange County Road Closures To view the most up to date list, visit: https://drivenc.gov Active OC Alerts', 'Home Government Departments A - F Emergency Services Disaster / Emergency Preparedness Disaster / Emergency Preparedness Welcome to Ready Orange Your "one stop shop" for preparedness information in Orange County! Sign up for Emergency Alerts OC Alerts is the public alert and warning system for Orange County. All Orange County residents are encouraged to sign up for OC Alerts to get important public safety messages! Visit ocalertsnc.com to sign up! General Preparedness Information Be Informed The National Weather Service - For up to date weather forecasts, storm tracks, and preparedness info ReadyNC.org - For much more information in North Carolina including evacuation info and more on specific hazards Red Cross Disaster Services - Your source for Red Cross information OC Alerts - Orange County\'s Public Alert and Warning System Build a Kit When an emergency strikes your family may be without power or access to grocery stores, running water or other day-to-day necessities. The best way to prepare for that is to build a Family Emergency Kit. The Kit should have some basic items, but should match you and your family - like a fingerprint, no two kits will look exactly alike. The Kit will be helpful whether you are staying at home or you have to leave on short notice. Buy a Red Cross Emergency Preparedness Kit FEMA\'s Recommend ed Kit ReadyNC\'s Recommended Kit Orange County Emergency Supply Kit Checklist (PDF) Orange County Older Adults Emergency Supply Kit Checklist (PDF) Make a Plan If a disaster strikes your family may be split up already, or be split up as a result. The best way to assure that you can make contact is to make a plan for how you all will check-in and get back together. FEMA\'s Guide to Making a Plan Get Involved There are more ways than ever to become better prepared and more connected to an emergency response than ever. Community Emergency Response Team (CERT) Public Health Reserve Corps The Red Cross Report Problems Power Outages Duke Power - 800-769-3766 Duke customers may also receive text notifications for power outages in their area. Text REG to 57801 for text alerts. With outage alerts, we\'ll notify you by text, voice message or email when you have an outage. We\'ll also send status updates and estimated restoration times. Piedmont Electric - Main Office 800-449-2667 or Report Outage 800-222-3107 ( PEMC outage map ) PSNC - 877-776-2427 ( Signs of a gas leak ) Downed Trees & Debris For downed trees and other debris in a roadway, assure that you are in a safe location and call 911 For downed trees on private property contact the primary insurer of the property and then contact a private debris removal company Flooding Report flooding by calling 911 Emergency Shelters Shelters Orange County will coordinate the opening of emergency shelters with support from the Department of Social Services, Health Department, Animal Services, and the American Red Cross when the need arises. There are 2 primary Orange County Emergency Shelters: Orange Middle School 308 Orange High School Road Hillsborough, NC 27278 Smith Middle School 9201 Seawell School Road Chapel Hill, NC 27516 Considerations Shelters require you to be as informed and prepared as possible: If you need special assistance in a disaster call 919-245-2884 to register for assistance before a disaster strikes. Shelter locations may change depending on the emergency Listen to the radio for further directions concerning shelter openings: WCHL-AM (1360), WCHL-FM (97.9), WDCG-FM (105.1), WRAL-FM (101.5), WYMY-FM (La Ley 101.1 in Spanish) Shelters will have food or water, but will not have easy access to medications or many other personal needs. Name: 40_irs_file_refund_pays
Representative_Docs: ['If your return was electronically filed and you provided bank account information for a direct deposit, you will usually receive your federal refund within 21 days. You can check on the status of your refund at www.irs.gov/refunds or by downloading the IRS2Go app .', 'If you filed your return on paper or did not provide bank information, you may receive a CP53E\xa0notice in the mail from the IRS requesting that you provide your bank information. If you don’t respond to the notice, the IRS will issue a paper check after 6 weeks. For updates on your refund status, visit Where’s My Refund .', 'Taxpayers who missed the April 15 filing deadline should submit their federal tax return as soon as possible. Those who missed the deadline to file and owe taxes should file as soon as possible to avoid penalties and interest. Requesting an extension allows for additional time to file but not to pay taxes owed. Interest and penalties will continue to accrue on the owed taxes until the balance is paid in full. Taxpayers who still need to file can use electronic filing options such as IRS Free File , or VITA volunteer-assisted GetYourRefund by Code for America. These are available through Oct. 15, 2026.']

Name: 41_recycling_stars_cart_program
Representative_Docs: ["Home Government Departments G - Z Solid Waste Management Educational Resources How to Recycle Right Orange County Recycling Stars Program Orange County Recycling Stars Program The Orange County Solid Waste Department is launching a new Recycling Stars Program. The program goal is to improve the overall quality and quantity of recyclables collected from households around the County. Residents recycling properly will be rewarded with star stickers. Residents that need some additional information on recycling properly will receive ‘oops’ tags, personalized contamination letters, and other educational materials. Any recycling cart with contamination will be marked with an oops tag and will not be collected during that collection period. The Recycling Stars Program was awarded the Public Recycling Champion Award by the Southeast Recycling Development Council in October 2023. Star sticker pictured below: Interested in being an Orange County recycling star? First, make sure you recycle right! Visit the ' What Goes In My Blue Recycle Cart ? page for more information. Second, download the free Orange County NC Recycles app in the Apple or Google Play Stores and learn how to dispose of different items using the A-Z Waste & Recycle Guide . The Stars program will help create awareness of the need to recycle right and ultimately reduce the contamination rate in the County’s single-family curbside recycling. Based on the 2019 recycling composition study, the overall contamination in the recycling stream was about 15%. New Recycling Cart Stickers As a part of the recycling stars program, over the next several months Solid Waste employees will be applying updated stickers with current 'Recycle Right' information on residents' recycling carts. IMPORTANT: Until you have received a new cart sticker, please leave your cart out until the afternoon to allow staff to update the cart stickers. Stars Program Introductory Letter Solid Waste Department Honored for Recycling Stars Program The Southeast Recycling Development Council on Oct. 19 awarded Orange County Solid Waste the Public Recycling Champion Award for its Recycling Stars initiative. The Recycling Stars program is an individual education program designed to improve the quality of comingled recycling and reduce contamination in residential curbside recycling carts. The program educates residents on the materials acceptable in curbside carts and which materials should be otherwise disposed through an intensive cart inspection program. Residents recycling correctly receive recycling star stickers. To date, the program has resulted in improvement in 59 percent of inspected curbside recycling carts. The North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality, which had provided a $15,000 grant to support starting the Stars program, nominated Orange County for the award. In support of the nomination, the county made a presentation about the program at the Resource Recycling Conference in August. “This award recognizes the dedication and hard work Orange County staff places on engaging and educating the community on how to recycle right,” said Robert Williams, Solid Waste and Fleet Director. “Being nominated and winning this prestigious award is a testament of the emphasis Orange County staff places on improving the quality of recyclable material being processed. Name: 43_cover_fund_veterinary_veterinarians
Representative_Docs: ['Home Government Departments A - F Animal Services Pet Help Resources Veterinary Care Assistance Program Veterinary Care Assistance We are not currently accepting applications for this program. Thanks to the generosity of those who have donated, this program has helped many area animals since its inception at the beginning of the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020! More pets were able to stay in their homes with the people that love them. When active, this fund is available to Orange County residents based on their needs and ability to meet income criteria. Obtaining this assistance requires collaboration with local veterinarians or specialists, and may cover full or partial medical expenses for issues such as: skin infections and other skin issues, dental issues, minor surgeries, etc. Funds will be issued directly to veterinarians and veterinary clinics or hospitals that are willing to participate. This fund does not cover spay/neuter or routine veterinary visits and vaccinations. This fund does not cover urgent, emergency situations that require immediate attention at an emergency clinic.
