Name: 4_training_law_enforcement_basic
Representative_Docs: ['About the Training Becoming a police officer in North Carolina requires successfully completing a basic law enforcement training program.\xa0The full-time program takes about 16-20 weeks to complete and concludes with a comprehensive written exam and skills testing. The program\xa0prepares entry-level individuals with the cognitive and physical skills needed to become certified law enforcement officers. It includes: 36 blocks of instruction with topics including: Arrest, search and seizure Driver training Firearms Motor vehicle law Practical exercises Extensive ethics training woven throughout the course. Minimum requirements to attend the basic training include being: A United States citizen. At least 20 years old. A high school graduate or having passed the General Educational Development tests. Being of good moral character. Basic law enforcement training programs are offered through many community colleges across the state. More information is available on the North Carolina Justice Academy website .', 'Basic Law Enforcement Training Entry-level police officers must complete basic law enforcement training. The  course contains 640 hours of instruction and practical exercises and takes about 16 weeks to complete. It contains 36 blocks of instruction on such topics as: Arrest, search and seizure Driver training Firearms Motor vehicle law An extensive ethics section is woven throughout the training experience. Field Training Newly hired officers are assigned a field training officer and receive field training on: Community service Day-to-day activities of a police officer Practical aspects of law enforcement During field training, new officers ride with the field training officer, an experienced officer who is trained to teach, coach and evaluate the new officer. The new officer must meet performance standards and pass an examination provided orally through a panel interview.', 'The Police Department prioritizes developing and maintaining skills. It requires training beyond the\xa0minimum number of hours and topics required by the state each year. A number of Hillsborough officers are trained to provide instruction in house and to officers in other agencies. New Officers Basic Law Enforcement Training Entry-level police officers must complete basic law enforcement training. The  course contains 640 hours of instruction and practical exercises and takes about 16 weeks to complete. It contains 36 blocks of instruction on such topics as: Arrest, search and seizure Driver training Firearms Motor vehicle law An extensive ethics section is woven throughout the training experience. Field Training Newly hired officers are assigned a field training officer and receive field training on: Community service Day-to-day activities of a police officer Practical aspects of law enforcement During field training, new officers ride with the field training officer, an experienced officer who is trained to teach, coach and evaluate the new officer. The new officer must meet performance standards and pass an examination provided orally through a panel interview. Annual Training Canine Training Police dogs are certified annually by an independent trainer. Each month, the canine officers and their handlers train for at least 16 hours in a variety of scenarios and in different locations and weather conditions. They also train with handlers and dogs from surrounding agencies. Elective Training Officers have an annual minimum goal of 40 hours of elective training — external training that they can choose to attend beyond their required training. Hillsborough officers are allowed up to 120 hours of elective training per year. Mandatory In-Service Training Certified officers are required by the state to receive 24 hours of annual in-service training. The state requires training in: Domestic violence Ethics Firearms Juvenile minority sensitivity Legal updates Proactive wellness Officers may choose from other topics to complete their required training, including: Engaging with people with views more extreme than the majority Enhancing community engagement Learning from local leaders who have dealt with civil unrest Partnering with community professionals Reducing the intensity of situations through listening, talking and defensive techniques Driver Training Slower Is Faster Driver Training The town provides annual driver training to its officers. In addition, the town manager and police chief help provide driver training to other police departments in the state through a course modeled after the Hillsborough "Slower Is Faster" program and sponsored by the\xa0N.C. League of Municipalities Risk Management Services Division. Claims from police vehicle collisions are one of the largest liabilities and costs that the league\'s insurance pool must pay for each year. Vehicle-related accidents also are the leading cause of police line-of-duty deaths and cause many deaths within communities. Tactics to Reduce Collisions Town Manager Eric Peterson competes in motorsports and coordinates the annual training in Hillsborough, which emphasizes key tactics to perform while driving to reduce collisions: Looking ahead and scanning surroundings to avoid surprises. Braking early. Slowing down to leave a margin for error for the unexpected. Using self-talk to better perform under pressure. Breathing to remain calm. The tactics help officers to keep their vehicles and cognitive functions under control. Adrenaline can impair driving by causing tunnel vision, loss of hearing, slowed reaction time, decreased motor skills, and a slowing of forebrain functions and cognitive processing. Training includes classroom instruction and practice behind the wheel that is focused on improving decision-making through various scenarios, including the sudden appearance of objects in the roadway. An instructor rides along to provide coaching. Internal and External Training Options Internal Training The Police Department offers a variety of training in house, including the following. Jiu Jitsu Training The Police Department provides training in Brazilian jiu jitsu to increase officers\' confidence in their ability to safely bring calm to intense and dangerous situations. The intent  is to reduce the need for use of force. Jiu jitsu is a martial arts grappling technique that emphasizes taking an opponent to the ground, gaining a dominant position and using techniques to safely force the person into submission. The techniques allow dominance from any position and negate size and strength advantages. They allow officers to safely take someone into custody, including: Safely handcuffing a person. Safely stopping a person from hitting or hurting an officer. Safely extracting a person\'s arms if the person is face down possibly with a firearm or a weapon underneath. Virtual Training The Police Department owns a virtual reality training system that allows up to two officers to train together in a small space with a controlled environment.\xa0The training helps officers: Fine-tune work done on a daily basis, like vehicle stops. Learn how to safely respond to a variety of situations. Review where improvements are needed in their response. Officers put on a headset and trackers for their wrist and tools. They train in scenarios created by the department or provided through the system, such as an active shooter situation. Officers are told what their scenario will be and the expected outcome. Their responses are unscripted. A trainer monitors the training on a screen, where avatars for the training officers can be seen.\xa0A body camera view allows the trainer to see where the officer’s eyes are. The virtual training can: Be manipulated to interact with the officers and their responses. Provide training for more real-life situations than basic training can offer. External Training Hillsborough officers have achieved certification in a number of trainings offered externally. Crisis Intervention Crisis intervention training creates connections among law enforcement, mental health providers, hospital emergency services and individuals with mental illness and their families. The training improves communication, identifies mental health resources for those in crisis, and ensures officer and community safety. Law Enforcement Executive Program The Law Enforcement Executive Program at N.C. State University provides twenty days of training over six months to effectively manage\xa0critical challenges. Participants enter the program with\xa0good basic managerial skills and a strong working knowledge of North Carolina local government and police service. Through the training, they build on those foundations and: Explore applying public management ideas and techniques for police agencies. Sharpen personal skills to excel as police managers. Think creatively about law enforcement management in increasingly complex, uncertain and changing environments. Professional Certification Officers can earn intermediate and advanced law enforcement certificates through the\xa0Law Enforcement Officers’ Professional Certificate Program. The program was established by the Criminal Justice Education and Training Standards Commission to: Foster interest in college education and professional law enforcement training Attract highly qualified people into law enforcement West Point Leadership Training The West Point Leadership Course at Methodist University in Fayetteville is adapted from the military leadership course that is required of all cadets in their third year of study at the United States Military Academy at West Point. The academically rigorous course is adapted for use in non-military organizations and relies heavily on case-based learning. The training examines and integrates leadership from the perspective of the individual, group, leader and organization. Contact Information Police Station, 127 N. Churton St. 919-296-9500 919-732-2187 (fax) Office phones are answered 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. weekdays. Call 911 for emergencies or to have an officer dispatched. Contact Police Department Staff Title Phone Email Hooks, Richard Captain 919-296-9524 Email Chelenza, Nicholas Lieutenant 919-296-9523 Email']

Name: 5_historic_district_commission_application
Representative_Docs: ["Exterior improvements to a site or structure in the Historic District may: Require a Certificate of Appropriateness from the Historic District Commission. Be exempt from permitting. Be approvable as a minor works item by Planning and Economic Development Division staff. Before making any exterior changes to a site or structure, discuss the proposal with town staff to determine the process required for the project and to avoid potential after-the-fact permit penalties and enforcement. These standards and matrices guide the commission's decisions on Certificate of Appropriateness applications: Compatibility Matrix Historic District Design Standards (effective Sept. 3, 2021) Historic District Signage Matrix", "The Historic District Commission reviews applications for exterior changes within the Hillsborough Historic District. A Certificate of Appropriateness or minor works approval from staff may be required before any exterior work can begin. All meetings are open to the public. The Historic District Commission approves changes to the district, including the addition of interpretive signs along the Riverwalk greenway. Agendas and Minutes Certificate of Appropriateness About the Historic District The Hillsborough Historic District was created in 1973 through passage of a preservation ordinance. The district presents a visual history of Hillsborough's development and\xa0includes the town’s commercial core and surrounding residential neighborhoods. The district is\xa0listed on the National Register of Historic Places and\xa0contains more than 100 houses, churches and buildings from the 18th and 19th centuries. Included is development from Georgian- and Federal-style buildings dating to the late 1700s to contemporary construction. The town's historic district overlaps with the National Register of Historic Places district, but it is a separate designation. Historic District Inventory Historic District Map About the Historic District Commission Mission The commission’s mission is to identify, protect and preserve Hillsborough's architectural resources and to educate the public about those resources and preservation in general. It conducts an awards program for noteworthy historic preservation projects in town. Role The commission has a quasi-judicial role. The commission: Hears evidence. Determines relevant facts. Ensures the changes are\xa0not conflicting with the special character of the historic district or landmark. Regulation It is against the law to begin building, moving, demolishing, altering or restoring any structure or site within the Historic District before a\xa0Certificate of Appropriateness\xa0has been issued by the commission or approved by staff as a minor work or ordinary maintenance and repair.\xa0See the Design Standards section on this page. Design Standards Exterior improvements to a site or structure in the Historic District may: Require a Certificate of Appropriateness from the Historic District Commission. Be exempt from permitting. Be approvable as a minor works item by Planning and Economic Development Division staff. Before making any exterior changes to a site or structure, discuss the proposal with town staff to determine the process required for the project and to avoid potential after-the-fact permit penalties and enforcement. These standards and matrices guide the commission's decisions on Certificate of Appropriateness applications: Compatibility Matrix Historic District Design Standards (effective Sept. 3, 2021) Historic District Signage Matrix Membership Composition A majority of the commission’s members have specialized training or an interest in architecture, preservation, archaeology, history or building methods. Terms Members serve up to two consecutive three-year terms. One calendar year must pass before a member may be reappointed after serving two complete terms and any partial term. Members Membership Name Term Expires G Miller Dec. 31, 2028 Hannah Peele — Vice Chair March 31, 2028 Sara Riek Aug. 31, 2026 Will Senner — Chair Nov. 30, 2027 Daniel Widis Nov. 30, 2027 Vacant Vacant Appointed Boards Application Meeting Information Meetings are at 6:30 p.m. the first Wednesday of each month in the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. in Hillsborough.\xa0See agenda packets and minutes on the town's meetings site . The town  does not discriminate on the basis of disability in its services, programs or activities. See the Americans with Disabilities Act Policy page. Meeting Schedule Contact Information Town Hall, 101 E. Orange St. 919-296-9472 Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Contact Planning Staff Title Phone Email Hoffheimer, Joseph Planner 919-296-9472 Email", "Design Standards Exterior improvements to a site or structure in the Historic District may: Require a Certificate of Appropriateness from the Historic District Commission. Be exempt from permitting. Be approvable as a minor works item by Planning and Economic Development Division staff. Before making any exterior changes to a site or structure, discuss the proposal with town staff to determine the process required for the project and to avoid potential after-the-fact permit penalties and enforcement. These standards and matrices guide the commission's decisions on Certificate of Appropriateness applications: Compatibility Matrix Historic District Design Standards (effective Sept. 3, 2021) Historic District Signage Matrix"]

Name: 6_water_treatment_plant_river
Representative_Docs: ["Hillsborough's drinking water comes from the Eno River. Before the water is safe to drink, it must go through multiple stages of treatment, which can take several days. The Water Treatment Plant is staffed 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. Staff who maintain the pipes, pumps and tanks connected with supplying water to you must be available at all hours for emergencies. About Water Alerts Backflow Prevention Lead and Drinking Water Water Conservation Our Drinking Water Source Eno River, Lakes and Reservoir The Eno River is Hillsborough's water source. Water from the river is drawn from three sites located in the Upper Neuse River watershed. The Eno and these three sites are considered surface water\xa0— natural water that has not penetrated much below the ground. Lakes on the Eno River Lake Ben Johnston\xa0— Hillsborough takes water from this lake to make drinking water. Seven Mile Creek enters the Eno River upstream of this area. Lake Orange —\xa0The town uses this lake as a secondary source of water from the Eno. The lake is on the east fork of the river outside town limits. It is maintained by Orange County. West Fork Eno Reservoir The town owns this reservoir on the west fork of the Eno River in nearby Cedar Grove and uses it as a secondary source for water.\xa0It can store about 1.2 billion gallons of water —\xa0about two years of supply. The reservoir opened in 2000, and a second\xa0phase was completed in 2021.\xa0It includes an innovative spillway that provides more surface in a compact area for water to spill over. The dam resembles piano keys when in use. Emergency Water The town has pipe connections with neighboring utilities to receive or send water during emergencies. Receiving Water Hillsborough can receive water from Durham in an emergency. Additionally, the Hillsborough Utilities Department is working on a project that would enable the town to receive water from its southern utilities neighbor, Orange Water and Sewer Authority. Sending Water Hillsborough can send water to: City of Durham Water Management Orange-Alamance Water System, which serves some rural areas in Orange and Alamance counties Orange Water and Sewer Authority, which serves the Carrboro and Chapel Hill area History of the Reservoir In the 1980s and early 1990s, the Hillsborough community began exploring options for addressing the town’s water shortage due to a history of droughts and water restrictions and a desire to accommodate expected future growth. In 1993, Hillsborough voters approved general obligation bonds to help pay for building the reservoir’s first phase. The first phase was built with expansion in mind. The reservoir’s 20-year permit allowed for a second phase to be started by 2018. In 2000, the reservoir was made operational earlier than scheduled and before full to help provide immediate drought relief for customers under water restrictions. Severe Droughts Hillsborough experienced severe droughts in the 1920s, 1930s, 1950s, 1980s and 2000s. In 1954, schools closed at noon and the town sought federal aid because of drought conditions. That same year, drought conditions forced the town to disconnect the water of any customer found wasting it. In 1988, mandatory water restrictions were put in place. By the early 1990s, the town completed an interconnected pipeline with the City of Durham to allow regular purchases from Durham’s water system to withstand periods of droughts and water restrictions. The town could only purchase water between 10 p.m. and 6 a.m. due to demands on Durham’s system. Benefits Phase 1 of the reservoir has: Helped the town withstand several severe droughts in the 2000s. At one point, larger surrounding systems were near or below 50 days of remaining water, but Hillsborough still had enough water for 200 days. Allowed Hillsborough to accommodate new development. Many residents and businesses joining Hillsborough after 2000 would not be here today without the reservoir. The Phase 2 expansion of the reservoir: Is a long-term investment in Hillsborough’s water supply. Raised the reservoir about 10 feet, doubling its volume and providing a maximum yield of 6 million gallons of water per day. No Recreation on the Reservoir Recreation is not allowed at the West Fork Eno Reservoir or the surrounding town-owned property. This includes boating, hunting and fishing. Required Conservation Federal and state permits require a conservation easement to: Protect the town’s drinking water source. Reduce the effects of the reservoir on others. To provide\xa0a 100-foot buffer around the reservoir, the town must keep the land in a natural state with no development, recreation or access other than to maintain the reservoir. Risks The town does not have the resources to oversee public activities at the reservoir and cannot assume the risk or liability of allowing recreational activities. Recreation also could introduce invasive animals and plants. The town already funds treatment to remove a weed native to South Asia that can damage utility equipment and the environment. See the section on annual treatment for an invasive plant on this page. Seasonal Treatment for Hydrilla The state does not plan to treat the Eno River for hydrilla in 2026 due to the flooding from Tropical Storm Chantal in 2025. A survey will be conducted later this year to determine whether hydrilla treatment should resume. Typically, the state annually treats 16 miles of the Eno River with the herbicide fluridone to reduce hydrilla, an invasive aquatic plant.\xa0Applications typically start in mid-May and continue through August. The section of the river treated is\xa0below the Ben Johnston Lake dam in Orange County to Roxboro Road and U.S. 501 in Durham County. The herbicide is applied in a concentration that is safe for humans, fish and wildlife and\xa0well below limits set by the Environmental Protection Agency. Property owners next to the treated section of river should use caution in using river water for irrigation during this time. About Hydrilla Hydrilla is native to South Asia and grows rapidly, creating thick mats on the surfaces of lakes, rivers and other waterways. The weed: Negatively affects the health of fish and wildlife in the area. Can clog intakes for supplying drinking water and irrigation. Can harbor mosquitoes. Can interfere with recreation on waterways. The plant was discovered in the Eno River watershed in the early 1990s in Lake Orange, which is upstream of Hillsborough and one of the town's water sources. In 2009, biologists confirmed hydrilla in the West Fork Eno Reservoir. The town-owned reservoir is stocked with triploid grass carp to manage the invasive plant. More Information The North Carolina Division of Water Resources manages the annual treatment. Hillsborough helps fund it. For more information, contact Stormwater and Environmental Services Manager Terry Hackett by email or phone at 919-296-9621. Water Treatment Our Water Quality The town's water treatment must meet high standards set by the town, state and federal governments.\xa0Hillsborough routinely tests for more than 150 substances in the town's drinking water and meets or exceeds the standards.\xa0See the annual Water Quality Report . Water Plant Testing Staff monitor and evaluate water 24 hours a day.\xa0The plant has: A state-certified laboratory to test for bacteria daily. Two lab-certified operators who test raw and finished water at the plant every two hours\xa0— more than required by the state. Water System Testing Staff also test various points in the water system. The water system includes the pipes, pumps and tanks needed to transport and store water. Tests occur: Weekly\xa0— For bacteria at five of the farthest points in the town's water lines. Monthly — For bacteria at 15 areas. Quarterly — For disinfection byproducts. These are substances created by the process to clean water. Annually — For inorganic materials, pesticides, synthetic organic chemicals, nitrates, volatile organic compounds and secondary contaminants. Every five years — For unregulated contaminants that the Environmental Protection Agency lists for testing. Our Water Treatment Process The Hillsborough Water Treatment Plant is the processing plant for your water. The plant takes raw water from the Eno River. It then adds ingredients and applies processes to the water to make it usable. The plant focuses on: First\xa0— Cleanliness and compliance with standards. Second\xa0— Taste, odor and appearance. Water Treatment Converting raw water into drinking water includes injecting chemicals at multiple points to help remove organic\xa0material, disinfect the water and ensure longer freshness before releasing it for use by the community. The\xa0chemical process starts before the raw water reaches the Water Treatment Plant. Water released from the town's reservoir travels to Lake Ben Johnston, where the town removes water for use.\xa0At this intake location, a chemical is added to help particles in the water combine for easier removal. At the plant, more\xa0chemicals are added for the same reason and to adjust the water's color,\xa0acidity and alkalinity. Filtering Out Materials The water then goes through several filtration, aeration and clarification stages. In these stages, the water remains in large basins while being mixed at slower and slower speeds. This allows the combined large materials to drop to the bottom of the basins. Finally, the clear water\xa0moves through a fine filtration system to remove remaining particles.\xa0At this stage, the water’s turbidity is measured and filtration\xa0adjustments are made to remove any cloudiness from the water caused by large amounts of particles that\xa0are otherwise invisible to the eye. Adjusting Treatment The water is then disinfected and final adjustments to acidity and\xa0alkalinity are made before the water is pumped through pipes to homes and businesses. Throughout the\xa0treatment process, employees test the water for more than 150 contaminants, making adjustments to the\xa0treatment as needed. See: From River to Faucet: Hillsborough's Water Treatment Process video Water Treatment Plant and Tours The Water Treatment Plant employees are highly certified. They continually operate and maintain the plant 24 hours a day to ensure compliance with state and federal permits and regulations. The plant can treat up to 3 million gallons of drinking water per day. Awards In previous years, the Water Treatment Plant and its staff won an award for Outstanding Operator of the Year from the N.C. Waterworks Operators Association. Tours See what it takes to prepare water taken from the Eno River for drinking. Public tours are offered at 1 p.m. on the second Friday of the month in March and September. Private tours can be scheduled separately for special groups. All tours are open to a maximum of 10 people with participants at least 10 years old. All tours require complete a request form. Request a Tour Water Distribution  and System Maintenance Annual Chlorine Disinfection For one month each year, the town is required to disinfect drinking water with chlorine. The state requires this annual change in utilities that normally use chloramines,\xa0a compound of chlorine and\xa0ammonia. Chlorine is a more intense disinfectant,\xa0and\xa0the annual change helps ensure\xa0a high level of disinfection in water mains. Other utilities in the region conduct the disinfection at the same time. This includes Hillsborough’s\xa0interconnected neighboring utilities, Orange Water and Sewer Authority and the City of Durham. Effects on Customers During the annual disinfection, some customers may notice a chlorine taste or odor in Hillsborough’s drinking water. Your water remains safe to drink. To remove chlorine taste and odor from water, you can: Add a few lemon slices to a pitcher of water. The lemon’s ascorbic acid will neutralize the chlorine. Let water sit in an open container in the refrigerator or on a counter for a day or so. A wide or shallow container will allow more contact between the air and water. Filter water with activated carbon. Water pitchers with such filters are sold locally. Boil water for 15 minutes to evaporate the chlorine. If you use Hillsborough water for special purposes or for processes involving careful control of water\xa0characteristics, the town encourages you to seek advice from an appropriate technical source — such\xa0as a filter vendor or service company — about whether and how to make adjustments to your use of\xa0Hillsborough water during the month of chlorine disinfection. Annual and Periodic Water System Flushing During the annual disinfection, staff release water from fire hydrants to ensure chlorine flows through the entire system. They also perform maintenance on hydrants. The annual flushing helps remove sediment and other minerals that may have settled in the water system.\xa0This flushing can cause temporary cloudiness or discoloration of water and could discolor light-colored fabric.\xa0If discoloration or air bubbles appear in the water, customers should run cold water in a bathtub for a few minutes until the water and air bubbles clear. If the water does not clear within five minutes, contact the Water Treatment Plant at 919-732-3621. During flushing or hydrant maintenance: Town personnel may be required to trim plantings or remove other items to ensure that adequate access to the hydrants exists for emergencies and maintenance. Community members may be asked to relocate plants and other items in street rights-of-way where necessary to provide a clear, 3-foot-wide area around a hydrant. Water Line Breaks The responsibility for repairs of water lines depends on whether the water line is on the utility or the property owner's side of the water meter or service connection. Responsibilities The town owns and maintains the water main. The supply system's main line typically is located under roadways. The town also maintains water service connections in public rights of ways or easements. It will make repairs to the line in that area. Property owners are responsible for the water service line between their property's house or structure and the water meter or service connection. Water Meter A property's water meter typically indicates the ownership line. The meter is placed on the line between the property's edge and the public right of way or easement. If a water meter is not located in the public right of way or easement,\xa0the town will provide water service maintenance to the meter and may require relocation during redevelopment. Water Distribution Division The Water Distribution Division repairs water line breaks and maintains: 153 miles of water lines 3 water boosting pump stations 5 water tanks The division includes a backflow prevention program to prevent contaminants from entering the water supply. Awards In past years, the division has won\xa0Outstanding Water Distribution Operator of the Year from the N.C. American Water Works Association-Water Environment Association. More Information Black Residue Black residue around utility fixtures generally does not indicate a problem with water quality. It is likely due to one of the following: Bacteria feeding on oxidized iron and manganese in water. A reaction between ammonia that the town uses for disinfection and rubber in faucet rings or toilet flaps. Iron and manganese are naturally occurring minerals and are in most water across the United States. The bacteria that feed on iron and manganese are non-health threatening. As they feed on the minerals, they form red-brown or black-brown residue that can clog water systems and cause a foul odor. Hillsborough and neighboring utilities use chloramines — a compound of chlorine and ammonia — to disinfect and preserve water. Removal To remove the iron and manganese bacteria, use equal parts of white vinegar, peroxide and water to clean any residue seen. This solution can be used periodically as a preventative measure on utility fixtures. To resolve the issue of a reaction between ammonia and rubber, the rubber materials in faucets and toilets can be replaced with materials that can withstand ammonia. Check labels on replacement products. To request testing or to make utilities crews aware of the issue and location, call the Hillsborough Water Treatment Plant at 919-732-3621. Crews can take a water sample from the home to determine the cause and\xa0discuss solutions. Pink Residue A pink to reddish film or residue on bathroom and kitchen surfaces is likely the result of airborne bacteria in the home that produce a pinkish or dark gray film on routinely moist surfaces, such as toilet bowls, showerheads, sink drains and tiles. The bacterium is likely Serratia marcescens , which is found naturally in soil, food and animals. It produces a red pigment and thrives on moisture, dust and phosphates, such as soap and shampoo\xa0residue. The bacteria have been tied to urinary tract infections, wound infections and respiratory problems in some people. Removal To prevent the pink residue: Keep bathtubs and sinks wiped down and dry. Clean surfaces with a solution that contains chlorine. Periodically disinfect toilets with chlorine bleach. Note that abrasive cleaners may scratch fixtures and allow increased growth of bacteria. Stale Water Before resuming use of a building or during periods of underutilized use, remove stale and potentially unhealthy water in your building’s water system by\xa0flushing the water system and all water devices. For more information, contact the Water Treatment Plant. Background When buildings are vacant or operating at significantly reduced capacity for more than a few days, water sits in pipes and devices instead of flowing. The chlorine and ammonia that the\xa0Hillsborough Water Treatment Plant adds to disinfect water disappears over time.\xa0Without proper levels of disinfectant, disease-causing microorganisms can begin to grow. Corrosion control also can be affected. Flushing Water Systems and Devices Flushing in stages is best. Begin at the main service line and include all the plumbing, storage tanks, fixtures, fire suppression systems, and equipment such as water fountains and ice machines. The first flush pulls out stale water. Follow-up flushes ensure fresh water with disinfectant is drawn fully through the building. For larger buildings, a single flush isn’t enough to reestablish good water quality. The longer service is interrupted, the more flushing is needed. Depending on the complexity of a building’s water system, facility managers should develop a comprehensive water management program for this process, but it should not replace a facility-specific Water Management Plan. Flushing Steps to Consider In addition to the following steps, consider capturing and reusing flushed water for outdoor watering or cleaning purposes. Flush all interior and exterior faucets (remove faucet aerators, if possible) for 10 to 30 minutes. Open all outlets at once to flush the service line and then open them again, individually, starting near where the water enters the building. Flush cold water first. Then flush hot water until it reaches its maximum temperature. Follow manufacturer recommendations to flush water fountains, hot water tanks, hot water recirculating loops, ice makers, dishwashers, humidifiers, and cooling towers. Water Quality Concerns For information on the quality of your water, see\xa0the annual Water Quality Report . If you have concerns about your water quality or would like more information, contact the Water Treatment Plant at 919-732-3621. The town will test water for its customers at no charge. See the section on whole house filters on this page regarding testing. Private Testing Certified Laboratories — For private water testing, find a certified lab on the North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality website by searching for drinking water laboratories. Testing Kits — If you receive any water testing kits by mail, they are from private companies unaffiliated with the town. Whole House Filters Whole house water filter systems are devices that customers can buy privately to treat their water. Issues to Keep in Mind Bacteria —\xa0Most water filters must be changed every 30 days to prevent bacteria from growing, and many water filters eliminate chlorine. The town adds chlorine to its drinking water for disinfection. Without adequate disinfection, bacteria will start to grow. Poor Water Quality —\xa0Use of a filter that eliminates chlorine will cause free ammonia to be left in the filtered water. This could cause chemical substances in the water to change, resulting in poor water quality. Specifically, ammonium salts could change to nitrites and then nitrates in a process called nitrification. Nitrate contamination can be unhealthy, particularly for infants. Hillsborough adds ammonia to water as part of its disinfection process. The ammonia bonds with chlorine to produce chloramines to cleanse water. Water Testing —\xa0If a water quality issue occurs in a home with a whole house filter system, the town cannot test the water.\xa0Whole house filters provide secondary treatment to water. Test results at homes with these filters would not provide data on the water that Hillsborough delivers. Depending on the complexity of a building’s water system, facility managers should develop a comprehensive water management program for this process, but it should not replace a facility-specific Water Management Plan. Flushing Steps to Consider In addition to the following steps, consider capturing and reusing flushed water for outdoor watering or cleaning purposes. Flush all interior and exterior faucets (remove faucet aerators, if possible) for 10 to 30 minutes. Open all outlets at once to flush the service line and then open them again, individually, starting near where the water enters the building. Flush cold water first. Then flush hot water until it reaches its maximum temperature. Follow manufacturer recommendations to flush water fountains, hot water tanks, hot water recirculating loops, ice makers, dishwashers, humidifiers, and cooling towers. Water Quality Concerns For information on the quality of your water, see\xa0the annual Water Quality Report . If you have concerns about your water quality or would like more information, contact the Water Treatment Plant at 919-732-3621. The town will test water for its customers at no charge. See the section on whole house filters on this page regarding testing. Private Testing Certified Laboratories — For private water testing, find a certified lab on the North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality website by searching for drinking water laboratories. Testing Kits — If you receive any water testing kits by mail, they are from private companies unaffiliated with the town. Whole House Filters Whole house water filter systems are devices that customers can buy privately to treat their water. Issues to Keep in Mind Bacteria —\xa0Most water filters must be changed every 30 days to prevent bacteria from growing, and many water filters eliminate chlorine. The town adds chlorine to its drinking water for disinfection. Without adequate disinfection, bacteria will start to grow. Poor Water Quality —\xa0Use of a filter that eliminates chlorine will cause free ammonia to be left in the filtered water. This could cause chemical substances in the water to change, resulting in poor water quality. Specifically, ammonium salts could change to nitrites and then nitrates in a process called nitrification. Nitrate contamination can be unhealthy, particularly for infants. Hillsborough adds ammonia to water as part of its disinfection process. The ammonia bonds with chlorine to produce chloramines to cleanse water. Water Testing —\xa0If a water quality issue occurs in a home with a whole house filter system, the town cannot test the water.\xa0Whole house filters provide secondary treatment to water. Test results at homes with these filters would not provide data on the water that Hillsborough delivers. Contact Information Water Distribution Division Adron Thompson Water/Sewer Facility, 715 Dimmocks Mill Road 919-296-9650 Water Treatment Plant, 711 Dimmocks Mill Road 919-732-3621 Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Emergencies Tampering or Theft —\xa0Call 911 and then use the contact utilities form. Water and Sewer Emergencies — Report immediately by calling 919-732-3621. Contact Utilities Staff Title Phone Email Miller, Troy Utilities Infrastructure Protection Supervisor 919-296-9653 Email Lashley, Joel Utility System Superintendent 919-296-9651 Email Cates, Nathan Water Treatment Plant Superintendent 919-296-9641 Email", "Hillsborough's drinking water comes from the Eno River. Before the water is safe to drink, it must go through multiple stages of treatment, which can take several days. The Water Treatment Plant is staffed 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. Staff who maintain the pipes, pumps and tanks connected with supplying water to you must be available at all hours for emergencies. About Water Alerts Backflow Prevention Lead and Drinking Water Water Conservation Our Drinking Water Source Eno River, Lakes and Reservoir The Eno River is Hillsborough's water source. Water from the river is drawn from three sites located in the Upper Neuse River watershed. The Eno and these three sites are considered surface water\xa0— natural water that has not penetrated much below the ground. Lakes on the Eno River Lake Ben Johnston\xa0— Hillsborough takes water from this lake to make drinking water. Seven Mile Creek enters the Eno River upstream of this area. Lake Orange —\xa0The town uses this lake as a secondary source of water from the Eno. The lake is on the east fork of the river outside town limits. It is maintained by Orange County. West Fork Eno Reservoir The town owns this reservoir on the west fork of the Eno River in nearby Cedar Grove and uses it as a secondary source for water.\xa0It can store about 1.2 billion gallons of water —\xa0about two years of supply. The reservoir opened in 2000, and a second\xa0phase was completed in 2021.\xa0It includes an innovative spillway that provides more surface in a compact area for water to spill over. The dam resembles piano keys when in use. Emergency Water The town has pipe connections with neighboring utilities to receive or send water during emergencies. Receiving Water Hillsborough can receive water from Durham in an emergency. Additionally, the Hillsborough Utilities Department is working on a project that would enable the town to receive water from its southern utilities neighbor, Orange Water and Sewer Authority. Sending Water Hillsborough can send water to: City of Durham Water Management Orange-Alamance Water System, which serves some rural areas in Orange and Alamance counties Orange Water and Sewer Authority, which serves the Carrboro and Chapel Hill area History of the Reservoir In the 1980s and early 1990s, the Hillsborough community began exploring options for addressing the town’s water shortage due to a history of droughts and water restrictions and a desire to accommodate expected future growth. In 1993, Hillsborough voters approved general obligation bonds to help pay for building the reservoir’s first phase. The first phase was built with expansion in mind. The reservoir’s 20-year permit allowed for a second phase to be started by 2018. In 2000, the reservoir was made operational earlier than scheduled and before full to help provide immediate drought relief for customers under water restrictions. Severe Droughts Hillsborough experienced severe droughts in the 1920s, 1930s, 1950s, 1980s and 2000s. In 1954, schools closed at noon and the town sought federal aid because of drought conditions. That same year, drought conditions forced the town to disconnect the water of any customer found wasting it. In 1988, mandatory water restrictions were put in place. By the early 1990s, the town completed an interconnected pipeline with the City of Durham to allow regular purchases from Durham’s water system to withstand periods of droughts and water restrictions. The town could only purchase water between 10 p.m. and 6 a.m. due to demands on Durham’s system. Benefits Phase 1 of the reservoir has: Helped the town withstand several severe droughts in the 2000s. At one point, larger surrounding systems were near or below 50 days of remaining water, but Hillsborough still had enough water for 200 days. Allowed Hillsborough to accommodate new development. Many residents and businesses joining Hillsborough after 2000 would not be here today without the reservoir. The Phase 2 expansion of the reservoir: Is a long-term investment in Hillsborough’s water supply. Raised the reservoir about 10 feet, doubling its volume and providing a maximum yield of 6 million gallons of water per day. No Recreation on the Reservoir Recreation is not allowed at the West Fork Eno Reservoir or the surrounding town-owned property. This includes boating, hunting and fishing. Required Conservation Federal and state permits require a conservation easement to: Protect the town’s drinking water source. Reduce the effects of the reservoir on others. To provide\xa0a 100-foot buffer around the reservoir, the town must keep the land in a natural state with no development, recreation or access other than to maintain the reservoir. Risks The town does not have the resources to oversee public activities at the reservoir and cannot assume the risk or liability of allowing recreational activities. Recreation also could introduce invasive animals and plants. The town already funds treatment to remove a weed native to South Asia that can damage utility equipment and the environment. See the section on annual treatment for an invasive plant on this page. Seasonal Treatment for Hydrilla The state does not plan to treat the Eno River for hydrilla in 2026 due to the flooding from Tropical Storm Chantal in 2025. A survey will be conducted later this year to determine whether hydrilla treatment should resume. Typically, the state annually treats 16 miles of the Eno River with the herbicide fluridone to reduce hydrilla, an invasive aquatic plant.\xa0Applications typically start in mid-May and continue through August. The section of the river treated is\xa0below the Ben Johnston Lake dam in Orange County to Roxboro Road and U.S. 501 in Durham County. The herbicide is applied in a concentration that is safe for humans, fish and wildlife and\xa0well below limits set by the Environmental Protection Agency. Property owners next to the treated section of river should use caution in using river water for irrigation during this time. About Hydrilla Hydrilla is native to South Asia and grows rapidly, creating thick mats on the surfaces of lakes, rivers and other waterways. The weed: Negatively affects the health of fish and wildlife in the area. Can clog intakes for supplying drinking water and irrigation. Can harbor mosquitoes. Can interfere with recreation on waterways. The plant was discovered in the Eno River watershed in the early 1990s in Lake Orange, which is upstream of Hillsborough and one of the town's water sources. In 2009, biologists confirmed hydrilla in the West Fork Eno Reservoir. The town-owned reservoir is stocked with triploid grass carp to manage the invasive plant. More Information The North Carolina Division of Water Resources manages the annual treatment. Hillsborough helps fund it. For more information, contact Stormwater and Environmental Services Manager Terry Hackett by email or phone at 919-296-9621. Water Treatment Our Water Quality The town's water treatment must meet high standards set by the town, state and federal governments.\xa0Hillsborough routinely tests for more than 150 substances in the town's drinking water and meets or exceeds the standards.\xa0See the annual Water Quality Report . Water Plant Testing Staff monitor and evaluate water 24 hours a day.\xa0The plant has: A state-certified laboratory to test for bacteria daily. Two lab-certified operators who test raw and finished water at the plant every two hours\xa0— more than required by the state. Water System Testing Staff also test various points in the water system. The water system includes the pipes, pumps and tanks needed to transport and store water. Tests occur: Weekly\xa0— For bacteria at five of the farthest points in the town's water lines. Monthly — For bacteria at 15 areas. Quarterly — For disinfection byproducts. These are substances created by the process to clean water. Annually — For inorganic materials, pesticides, synthetic organic chemicals, nitrates, volatile organic compounds and secondary contaminants. Every five years — For unregulated contaminants that the Environmental Protection Agency lists for testing. Our Water Treatment Process The Hillsborough Water Treatment Plant is the processing plant for your water. The plant takes raw water from the Eno River. It then adds ingredients and applies processes to the water to make it usable. The plant focuses on: First\xa0— Cleanliness and compliance with standards. Second\xa0— Taste, odor and appearance. Water Treatment Converting raw water into drinking water includes injecting chemicals at multiple points to help remove organic\xa0material, disinfect the water and ensure longer freshness before releasing it for use by the community. The\xa0chemical process starts before the raw water reaches the Water Treatment Plant. Water released from the town's reservoir travels to Lake Ben Johnston, where the town removes water for use.\xa0At this intake location, a chemical is added to help particles in the water combine for easier removal. At the plant, more\xa0chemicals are added for the same reason and to adjust the water's color,\xa0acidity and alkalinity. Filtering Out Materials The water then goes through several filtration, aeration and clarification stages. In these stages, the water remains in large basins while being mixed at slower and slower speeds. This allows the combined large materials to drop to the bottom of the basins. Finally, the clear water\xa0moves through a fine filtration system to remove remaining particles.\xa0At this stage, the water’s turbidity is measured and filtration\xa0adjustments are made to remove any cloudiness from the water caused by large amounts of particles that\xa0are otherwise invisible to the eye. Adjusting Treatment The water is then disinfected and final adjustments to acidity and\xa0alkalinity are made before the water is pumped through pipes to homes and businesses. Throughout the\xa0treatment process, employees test the water for more than 150 contaminants, making adjustments to the\xa0treatment as needed. See: From River to Faucet: Hillsborough's Water Treatment Process video Water Treatment Plant and Tours The Water Treatment Plant employees are highly certified. They continually operate and maintain the plant 24 hours a day to ensure compliance with state and federal permits and regulations. The plant can treat up to 3 million gallons of drinking water per day. Awards In previous years, the Water Treatment Plant and its staff won an award for Outstanding Operator of the Year from the N.C. Waterworks Operators Association. Tours See what it takes to prepare water taken from the Eno River for drinking. Public tours are offered at 1 p.m. on the second Friday of the month in March and September. Private tours can be scheduled separately for special groups. All tours are open to a maximum of 10 people with participants at least 10 years old. All tours require complete a request form. Request a Tour Water Distribution  and System Maintenance Annual Chlorine Disinfection For one month each year, the town is required to disinfect drinking water with chlorine. The state requires this annual change in utilities that normally use chloramines,\xa0a compound of chlorine and\xa0ammonia. Chlorine is a more intense disinfectant,\xa0and\xa0the annual change helps ensure\xa0a high level of disinfection in water mains. Other utilities in the region conduct the disinfection at the same time. This includes Hillsborough’s\xa0interconnected neighboring utilities, Orange Water and Sewer Authority and the City of Durham. Effects on Customers During the annual disinfection, some customers may notice a chlorine taste or odor in Hillsborough’s drinking water. Your water remains safe to drink. To remove chlorine taste and odor from water, you can: Add a few lemon slices to a pitcher of water. The lemon’s ascorbic acid will neutralize the chlorine. Let water sit in an open container in the refrigerator or on a counter for a day or so. A wide or shallow container will allow more contact between the air and water. Filter water with activated carbon. Water pitchers with such filters are sold locally. Boil water for 15 minutes to evaporate the chlorine. If you use Hillsborough water for special purposes or for processes involving careful control of water\xa0characteristics, the town encourages you to seek advice from an appropriate technical source — such\xa0as a filter vendor or service company — about whether and how to make adjustments to your use of\xa0Hillsborough water during the month of chlorine disinfection. Annual and Periodic Water System Flushing During the annual disinfection, staff release water from fire hydrants to ensure chlorine flows through the entire system. They also perform maintenance on hydrants. The annual flushing helps remove sediment and other minerals that may have settled in the water system.\xa0This flushing can cause temporary cloudiness or discoloration of water and could discolor light-colored fabric.\xa0If discoloration or air bubbles appear in the water, customers should run cold water in a bathtub for a few minutes until the water and air bubbles clear. If the water does not clear within five minutes, contact the Water Treatment Plant at 919-732-3621. During flushing or hydrant maintenance: Town personnel may be required to trim plantings or remove other items to ensure that adequate access to the hydrants exists for emergencies and maintenance. Community members may be asked to relocate plants and other items in street rights-of-way where necessary to provide a clear, 3-foot-wide area around a hydrant. Water Line Breaks The responsibility for repairs of water lines depends on whether the water line is on the utility or the property owner's side of the water meter or service connection. Responsibilities The town owns and maintains the water main. The supply system's main line typically is located under roadways. The town also maintains water service connections in public rights of ways or easements. It will make repairs to the line in that area. Property owners are responsible for the water service line between their property's house or structure and the water meter or service connection. Water Meter A property's water meter typically indicates the ownership line. The meter is placed on the line between the property's edge and the public right of way or easement. If a water meter is not located in the public right of way or easement,\xa0the town will provide water service maintenance to the meter and may require relocation during redevelopment. Water Distribution Division The Water Distribution Division repairs water line breaks and maintains: 153 miles of water lines 3 water boosting pump stations 5 water tanks The division includes a backflow prevention program to prevent contaminants from entering the water supply. Awards In past years, the division has won\xa0Outstanding Water Distribution Operator of the Year from the N.C. American Water Works Association-Water Environment Association. More Information Black Residue Black residue around utility fixtures generally does not indicate a problem with water quality. It is likely due to one of the following: Bacteria feeding on oxidized iron and manganese in water. A reaction between ammonia that the town uses for disinfection and rubber in faucet rings or toilet flaps. Iron and manganese are naturally occurring minerals and are in most water across the United States. The bacteria that feed on iron and manganese are non-health threatening. As they feed on the minerals, they form red-brown or black-brown residue that can clog water systems and cause a foul odor. Hillsborough and neighboring utilities use chloramines — a compound of chlorine and ammonia — to disinfect and preserve water. Removal To remove the iron and manganese bacteria, use equal parts of white vinegar, peroxide and water to clean any residue seen. This solution can be used periodically as a preventative measure on utility fixtures. To resolve the issue of a reaction between ammonia and rubber, the rubber materials in faucets and toilets can be replaced with materials that can withstand ammonia. Check labels on replacement products. To request testing or to make utilities crews aware of the issue and location, call the Hillsborough Water Treatment Plant at 919-732-3621. Crews can take a water sample from the home to determine the cause and\xa0discuss solutions. Pink Residue A pink to reddish film or residue on bathroom and kitchen surfaces is likely the result of airborne bacteria in the home that produce a pinkish or dark gray film on routinely moist surfaces, such as toilet bowls, showerheads, sink drains and tiles. The bacterium is likely Serratia marcescens , which is found naturally in soil, food and animals. It produces a red pigment and thrives on moisture, dust and phosphates, such as soap and shampoo\xa0residue. The bacteria have been tied to urinary tract infections, wound infections and respiratory problems in some people. Removal To prevent the pink residue: Keep bathtubs and sinks wiped down and dry. Clean surfaces with a solution that contains chlorine. Periodically disinfect toilets with chlorine bleach. Note that abrasive cleaners may scratch fixtures and allow increased growth of bacteria. Stale Water Before resuming use of a building or during periods of underutilized use, remove stale and potentially unhealthy water in your building’s water system by\xa0flushing the water system and all water devices. For more information, contact the Water Treatment Plant. Background When buildings are vacant or operating at significantly reduced capacity for more than a few days, water sits in pipes and devices instead of flowing. The chlorine and ammonia that the\xa0Hillsborough Water Treatment Plant adds to disinfect water disappears over time.\xa0Without proper levels of disinfectant, disease-causing microorganisms can begin to grow. Corrosion control also can be affected. Flushing Water Systems and Devices Flushing in stages is best. Begin at the main service line and include all the plumbing, storage tanks, fixtures, fire suppression systems, and equipment such as water fountains and ice machines. The first flush pulls out stale water. Follow-up flushes ensure fresh water with disinfectant is drawn fully through the building. For larger buildings, a single flush isn’t enough to reestablish good water quality. The longer service is interrupted, the more flushing is needed. Depending on the complexity of a building’s water system, facility managers should develop a comprehensive water management program for this process, but it should not replace a facility-specific Water Management Plan. Flushing Steps to Consider In addition to the following steps, consider capturing and reusing flushed water for outdoor watering or cleaning purposes. Flush all interior and exterior faucets (remove faucet aerators, if possible) for 10 to 30 minutes. Open all outlets at once to flush the service line and then open them again, individually, starting near where the water enters the building. Flush cold water first. Then flush hot water until it reaches its maximum temperature. Follow manufacturer recommendations to flush water fountains, hot water tanks, hot water recirculating loops, ice makers, dishwashers, humidifiers, and cooling towers. If you have concerns about your water quality or would like more information, contact the Water Treatment Plant at 919-732-3621. The town will test water for its customers at no charge. See the section on whole house filters on this page regarding testing. Private Testing Certified Laboratories — For private water testing, find a certified lab on the North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality website by searching for drinking water laboratories. Testing Kits — If you receive any water testing kits by mail, they are from private companies unaffiliated with the town. Issues to Keep in Mind Bacteria —\xa0Most water filters must be changed every 30 days to prevent bacteria from growing, and many water filters eliminate chlorine. The town adds chlorine to its drinking water for disinfection. Without adequate disinfection, bacteria will start to grow. Poor Water Quality —\xa0Use of a filter that eliminates chlorine will cause free ammonia to be left in the filtered water. This could cause chemical substances in the water to change, resulting in poor water quality. Specifically, ammonium salts could change to nitrites and then nitrates in a process called nitrification. Nitrate contamination can be unhealthy, particularly for infants. Hillsborough adds ammonia to water as part of its disinfection process. The ammonia bonds with chlorine to produce chloramines to cleanse water. Water Testing —\xa0If a water quality issue occurs in a home with a whole house filter system, the town cannot test the water.\xa0Whole house filters provide secondary treatment to water. Test results at homes with these filters would not provide data on the water that Hillsborough delivers. Emergencies Tampering or Theft —\xa0Call 911 and then use the contact utilities form. Water and Sewer Emergencies — Report immediately by calling 919-732-3621. Name: 7_pollinator_garden_bee_native
Representative_Docs: ["Events Invasive Plant Removal Workday June 24, 2026 10:00 a.m. - 12:00 p.m. Invasive Plant Removal Workday July 01, 2026 10:00 a.m. - 12:00 p.m. Invasive Plant Removal Workday July 08, 2026 10:00 a.m. - 12:00 p.m. More Events Hillsborough became the 35th Bee City USA in 2016. As a designated Bee City, Hillsborough helps to: Expand pollinator-friendly practices and environments in town. Raise awareness about how pollinators sustain more than 75% of the world’s plant species. Sustainability Initiatives About Bee City USA The Hillsborough Garden Club encouraged the town to seek Bee City USA status. It helps develop the program and educational materials with the Hillsborough Public Space and Sustainability Division and Hillsborough Tree Board. Bee City USA primarily focuses on native species of bees. It gives communities a framework to help native pollinators by providing habitats with a variety of native plants and nest sites and with protection from pesticides. See: Hillsborough's Bee City USA resolution Bee City USA website Pollinator Garden The pollinator garden is maintained by the Orange County Master Gardener Program, a program of the N.C. Cooperative Extension Orange County Center . To help maintain the garden or any future bee hotel, contact the Public Space and Sustainability Division . Gold Park is located at 415 Dimmocks Mill Road. The pollinator garden is along the Riverwalk greenway, south of the railroad trestle. Bee Hotel In July 2025, floodwaters from Tropical Storm Chantal damaged the bee hotel that served as public art and a home in the Gold Park pollinator garden\xa0for 90 species of bees native to the area. Pollinator-friendly Activities Pollinator-friendly Activities Hillsborough has a number of initiatives to reverse pollinator declines. See the Sustainability Initiatives page for current initiatives. To volunteer, contact the Public Space and Sustainability Division . Pollinator Gardens — The town installed and maintains\xa0pollinator gardens at: Cates Creek and Gold parks Downtown planters and parking lot islands Orange County master gardeners helped install the garden at Gold Park and hold monthly workdays March-October at the garden. Resources to Help Pollinators The town, Tree Board and other partners offer a number of resources to help community members help pollinators. Designing a Rain Garden Growing Edible Landscaping Landscaping with Native Plants Growing a Pollinator Garden Top 25 Native Pollinator Plants for North Carolina Swarm Season Honeybee swarm season is during the spring and summer months. If you spot a swarm, immediately contact a local beekeeper or the Orange County Center of the North Carolina Cooperative Extension to help protect these bees, which are responsible for a third of the food we eat. Name: 8_property_owners_easement_right
Representative_Docs: ["A right of way or easement gives legal right for public uses or to perform work on infrastructure. They are documented to reserve property for the specific use, such as maintaining the town's water and sewer system. Easements do not remove property from owners, but they must be kept clear of any plantings or structures. Town Responsibilities The town periodically mows water and sewer easements to keep them accessible for routine maintenance and emergency response related to town utility assets. The mowing typically occurs in the spring and fall and includes the removal of brush and undergrowth. The town will not replace any plants, fences, sheds or other structures that are removed from a utilities easement when utilities workers need to access the easement. The town is responsible for any repairs to property damaged by heavy equipment used in the clearing. Customer Responsibilities Awareness Property owners with utilities easements need to be aware of the easement boundaries before building or planting. Contact the Engineering Services Division to: Learn if you have an easement on your property. Request permission before placing anything in an easement. Upkeep and Clearances Property owners are responsible for upkeep, such as mowing, of their property where easements are located and within the road right of way. Property owners also must maintain sufficient clearance to allow the town to access, operate and maintain its assets. C learance requirements are measured from the center point (radius) of the device: Fire hydrants\xa0— 3 feet Manholes and concrete vaults\xa0—\xa06 feet Water meters, valve boxes, sewer cleanouts and other utilities features not listed\xa0—\xa02 feet Additional information on the technical specifications for utilities and sanitary sewer use can be found in the Hillsborough Code of Ordinances , chapters 14 and 15. Contact Information Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. 919-296-9630 Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Contact Engineering Services Staff Title Phone Email Green, Bryant Engineering Services Manager 919-296-9630 Email", "Rights and Reasonable Use In North Carolina, common law governs stormwater runoff and drainage. Basically, a downhill property has to receive water from uphill. Water has a right to go where it wants to go ― downhill. Components of this common law: The easement of an uphill owner includes the right to accelerate and increase the natural flow of water. Natural flow means the uphill owner cannot divert or cause water to flow onto the lower land in a different manner or place than it would naturally. The downhill owner may not obstruct natural flow from above. Property owners have the legal right to make reasonable use of their property, even if development alters water flow and affects downstream owners. The uphill owner can incur liability when the change in runoff is unreasonable and causes substantial damage. Documents Stormwater Runoff Conveyances Water Flows Downhill Maintenance Responsibilities Property owners are responsible for the stormwater control measures on their private property. Owners include homeowners associations. Drainage Ditches Along Streets Property owners are required to mow drainage ditches along town streets. The town ensures water flow is not restricted in the drainage ditches along town streets. Contact the Public Works Division to request maintenance of drainage ditches. Wet and Dry Ponds Property owners are required to have  wet and dry ponds inspected annually by a private licensed inspector. The inspection report must be submitted to the Stormwater and Environmental Services Division. The third-party annual inspections may result in recommendations for maintenance or improvements. Before making a costly repair, contact town staff to check that the work is necessary. See the Ponds and Geese section if a recommendation involves addressing geese. Ponds and Geese The Stormwater and Environmental Services Division requires a 6-foot vegetated area around ponds used as stormwater control measures. In this area, non-woody vegetation must be allowed to grow to a natural height during the growing season. When this area is newly planted, Canada geese may eat or pull out the vegetation before it is fully established. Goose management can be used to ensure this vegetation is established and maintained. Once grown to a natural height, this vegetation can also help deter geese from accessing the pond. This deterrence method works best if the vegetated area is maintained consistently around the entire pond. Canada geese are protected under the Migratory Bird Treaty Act . They are attracted to ponds, including those used as stormwater control measures. Several geese living by a pond can hurt water quality. They eat young grass shoots that help keep pollution from reaching a pond, and they drop fecal matter that can run into the water. Lack of vegetation can cause shoreline erosion, and both erosion and excessive fecal matter can lead to odor problems, algal blooms and fish kills in the pond and downstream of the pond. To improve water quality, pond management companies may suggest ways to manage Canada geese. The Stormwater and Environmental Services Division does not require property owners to deter or chase away geese. Deterring Geese Hazing Canada geese is a legal option in North Carolina for deterring geese. See more information about hazing on the North Carolina Wildlife Resources Commission's website. Some additional methods for deterring geese include planting tall vegetation and installing temporary or permanent fencing. Often, pond owners with the most success are those who hire a management company to help them and who use multiple methods simultaneously to deter geese. Stream Identification and Riparian Buffers The town enforces a 50-foot buffer of plantings along streams and the Eno River. Prior approval is required to clear, build or make other impacts within a stream or river buffer, also called a riparian buffer. See the Public Infrastructure Permits page for additional information and to request stream identification services. See Riparian (Stream) Buffers . About Stream and River Buffers Buffers along water bodies are undisturbed, naturally forested areas that: Provide wildlife food and habitat. Slow stormwater runoff by allowing it to soak into the ground. Buffers help protect water quality by: Controlling erosion and keeping stream banks from collapsing. Reducing flooding and pollution of waterways. Moderating stream temperature through the amount and quality of sunlight allowed on a waterbody. Woody debris in waterways also protect water quality and provide habitat for aquatic organisms. See the document Woody Debris in Streams and Rivers . Resources The division offers materials to help property owners manage stormwater and protect the environment. Contact staff to request a site visit and advice. Documents Compost Blankets Designing A Rain Garden Don't Go Down the Drain — effect of stormwater runoff pollution on businesses Lawn Care For Water Quality Stormwater Bioswales Stormwater Runoff Conveyances Water Flows Downhill Woody Debris In Streams And Rivers Websites These sites were created in partnership with\xa0Elon University students: Enhance Your Yard with Green Infrastructure — Focusing on water management that works with the natural water cycle. Climate Impact in Residential Areas — Spotlighting the effects of climate change on the local area and actions community members can take. Videos Rain Garden", "Ditches, Rights of Way and Easements The town maintains drainage areas on town-maintained property and on property that it has the right to access. Areas with access rights are called rights of way or easements. Maintaining the drainage areas in ditches, rights of way and easements ensures water flow is not restricted. On some of these areas: Grass is mowed periodically. Trees are removed or trimmed to prevent roots from penetrating sewer lines and to keep the area clear. Riprap, grass seed and straw, or other devices may be installed to control erosion. Place Work Order Easement Work Advance notice — Before work is done on an easement, the property owner will be contacted about the purpose and approximate amount of time for the work. The advance notice allows property owners to ask questions and to remove personal items prior to clearing. During clearing\xa0— A town employee is on site at all times to monitor progress and to address community members' concerns."]

Name: 9_lake_falls_rules_wastewater
Representative_Docs: ['Falls Lake Rules Hillsborough is required to treat its wastewater at higher and more expensive standards than other sewer systems because it is in a watershed that drains into Falls Lake. The lake is a 12,000-acre reservoir in Durham, Granville and Wake counties. It is on the Environmental Protection Agency’s list of impaired waters and has been designated nutrient-sensitive by the North Carolina Division of Water Resources. To protect and restore the lake’s water quality, the state created the Falls Lake Rules to\xa0reduce the amount of nitrogen and phosphorous that enter Falls Lake through rivers and streams.\xa0The first stage of the wastewater discharge rules went into effect in 2016, and the second stage is expected in 2036. In 2014, Hillsborough completed upgrades to its wastewater treatment plant in part to meet the Stage 1 wastewater rule. Additional upgrades to the Wastewater Treatment Plant likely will be required to meet the Stage 2 wastewater rules. Read more about the rules from the Upper Neuse River Basin Association .', "Understanding the Cost of Providing Service Every community has different conditions that affect the ability to provide safe and reliable water and sewer service. Below are primary reasons that Hillsborough’s water and sewer rates are higher than those of some surrounding communities. Small System —\xa0Hillsborough operates a small water and sewer system. A larger system can spread the costs of operations among more customers. Small Water Supply —\xa0The Eno River does not provide a large volume of water. The town is  near the river's beginning, and the drainage basin that feeds into Hillsborough’s waterways is relatively small. Partnering with a neighboring system is likely not feasible. The Carrboro-Chapel Hill area\xa0is in a different river basin, and a past evaluation found\xa0costs for partnering with Durham to be too expensive. The town built a reservoir in the late 1990s and completed an expansion of it in 2021 to address water capacity needs because of expected growth and a history of droughts. Debt Management —\xa0A portion of  water and sewer bill payments helps pay the debt on projects to provide clean and reliable water and sewer service, including: Wastewater Treatment Plant upgrades —\xa0$17.8 million financed with payments until 2034. Reservoir expansion —\xa0$10.2 million financed with payments until 2042. Roads project for reservoir expansion —\xa0$3.3 million financed with payments until 2030. By law, the town must pass a balanced budget each year. Planned expenses cannot fall below expected revenue, and debt payments cannot create a deficit. State Rules —\xa0Hillsborough must treat its wastewater at higher and more expensive standards than other sewer systems because its watershed drains into Falls Lake. The lake has been deemed impaired by the Environmental Protection Agency and nutrient-sensitive by the North Carolina Division of Water Resources. The state's Falls Lake Rules to protect and restore the lake’s water quality\xa0are said to be the nation’s strictest limits on treated wastewater. In 2014, Hillsborough completed upgrades to its wastewater treatment plant in part to meet the Stage 1 wastewater rule that went into effect in 2016. A large portion of customers' sewer payments helps pay debt on the plant upgrades. Stage 2 rules, expected in 2036,\xa0likely will require more upgrades. Expensive and Older Infrastructure —\xa0Hillsborough has more sewage pump stations than a typical municipality because of its terrain and likely because of low upfront costs.\xa0Gravity is the most cost-effective way to transport water and wastewater. Pump stations are expensive to operate, require a large amount of staff time and can result in overflows and state fines when they fail.\xa0The town operates 25 stations in about 5 square miles. Most were built by developers, are old and were not built to today's standards. The town also must budget to replace other infrastructure. Some were installed well before the town's treatment plants were built. Maintenance Commitment —\xa0Water and sewer services are expensive to provide. The United States  poorly maintains its drinking and wastewater infrastructure, earning grades of C- and D+ in the 2025 Report Card for America's Infrastructure from the American Society of Civil Engineers. Across North Carolina , $20 billion is needed to maintain and improve drinking water infrastructure and $12.6 billion is needed for wastewater infrastructure. Hillsborough is committed to taking care of its operations. Your water and sewer bill reflects your contribution to the labor and the physical infrastructure required to have clean water available and to have wastewater removed and safely returned to the environment. The town does not profit from providing water and sewer service. It uses water and sewer revenue only for the operation, maintenance and improvement of the town’s water and sewer system.
