Name: 10_community_course_offers_government
Representative_Docs: ['Other ways to engage Community Police Academy The Police Department offers a multi-week interactive, informational program in May and June. The application period for 2026 has closed. Complete an interest form if you would like to be notified when the 2027 academy is scheduled. Government 101 The town offers a multiweek course in the fall to help community members increase: Their knowledge of town government. Their interest and ability in influencing and participating in town decisions. Registration typically opens in August. See the Government 101 page. Public Meetings Hillsborough Board of Commissioners meetings and appointed board meetings are open to the public. In addition, the town holds quarterly public hearings on planning items. See the Calendar for all public meetings. Surveys The town frequently requests input through surveys, including a community survey that is conducted every two years. Check back on this page for current surveys or subscribe to town news to receive them in your inbox.', "Course Basics The multiweek course is coordinated by the Communications Division and includes time with the Administrative Services, Community Services, Financial Services, Police and Utilities departments and  staff from the following divisions and operations: Budget Fleet Maintenance Planning and Economic Development Public Space and Sustainability Public Works Stormwater and Environmental Services Tours Visits are made to: A fire station in Hillsborough to learn about the Orange Rural Fire Department and the Hillsborough Police Department. The town contracts with Orange Rural for fire service. The town's water and sewer facilities, including the water and wastewater treatment plants. Program Goals The program is intended to: Increase community members' knowledge, interest and ability to influence and participate in town government. Reduce barriers to participating in and influencing town government decisions. This occurs through increased understanding of key processes and operations of the town. Assist in building community members' interest in serving the community through future service. Learn from community members attending the course.", "New to Town? See information on this page\xa0to help you quickly get settled and feeling at home in our small town. Want to Get More Involved? See information on this page to help you learn more about the town. Volunteering for pollinator garden workdays is a great way to meet other Hillsborough residents. Stormwater Fees Tax Rates Water and Sewer Rates Economic Development Around Town Arts and Events Hillsborough is home to a thriving arts and culture scene, as well as many events. View galleries, shops and upcoming events on the Hillsborough Visitors Center website. Development Private Development Private development is a project not undertaken by the town or any other government. The town does list large private development occurring or under review within town limits on the Development Projects page. Public Projects See what the town is developing on the Public Projects page. History Hillsborough is home to a thriving arts and culture scene, as well as many events. View galleries, shops and upcoming events on the Hillsborough Visitors Center website. Parks and Greenways Hillsborough has green spaces nestled within its trees, including the Riverwalk greenway, which follows the Eno River through downtown. See the town’s parks and greenways on the Recreation Facilities page. View recreation facilities available across the county on the Orange County website. Be Informed Emergency Alerts The town issues emergency notifications through OC Alerts , Orange County's mass notification system. The system is a partnership of Orange Water and Sewer Authority and the governments in Orange County. OWASA provides water service to the Carrboro and Chapel Hill community. See the Ready Hillsborough page for emergency resources. Town News The town provides regular updates through news releases, social media and a monthly newsletter . A weekly digest of town news releases and key reminders are emailed on Fridays. Time-sensitive emails are sent as needed. The newsletter is inserted into paper water and sewer bills and included in the weekly digest at the start of each month. Follow @HillsboroughGov on Facebook , Instagram and X . Follow Town of Hillsborough on Nextdoor . Subscribe to town emails. Get Involved Board Meetings Meetings of the elected and appointed boards are open to the public. They typically are in the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. Hillsborough Board of Commissioners — Meets the second and fourth Monday of each month, typically taking a break in July. Appointed Boards — See their individual\xa0pages for meeting schedules. Subscribe to meeting notices and see the agenda and minutes site for meeting information and materials. Civic Engagement Course The town offers a course over several weeks aimed at helping community members increase their knowledge of town government, as well as their interest and ability in influencing and participating in town decisions. See the Government 101 page. Engage Hillsborough The town offers periodic events for community members and staff to connect and discuss a topic. The town also seeks input at times through surveys. Check the Engage Hillsborough page for current or upcoming ways to engage. Volunteer Opportunities The town offers multiple ways to volunteer or help others in the community. Consider: Serving on appointed boards . Serving as a community liaison to help share information from the town and let staff know of concerns and questions in the community. Contact Communications . Improving the town’s public spaces\xa0by joining a team to remove invasive plants or attending various workdays, including to remove trash from roadsides and help with pollinator gardens. Contact Public Space . Donating to the Water Assistance Program to help neighbors pay their water bills. See a Quick Guide to Services Public Safety Emergency workers are dispatched through 911. Fire — Orange Rural Fire Department provides fire service to Hillsborough through a contract. Law Enforcement — Hillsborough Police Department provides police services within town limits. Orange County Sheriff’s Office provides services in unincorporated Orange County. Property improvements or uses Contact the Town Contact the Hillsborough Planning and Economic Development Division using the contact form or by calling 919-732-1270 before signing contracts or purchasing supplies for: Adding a deck or room or enclosing an open space. Making yard improvements such as outbuildings, fences and patios. Adding accessory dwelling units. Running a business from your home. Contact the County Call the Orange County Building Inspections Division at 919-245-2575 before signing contracts or purchasing supplies for: Renovating your home. Changing or improving electrical, heating, air conditioning or plumbing systems in your home. Adding any systems to an outbuilding. Street Maintenance The Hillsborough Public Works Division oversees maintenance of town-owned streets. Some roads in town are private or maintained by the state. See the Street Maintenance page to learn more. Solid Waste Bulk Items The town collects bulk items, such as large appliances, monthly on the second full week of the month, with the week starting on Sunday. See the Garbage Collection page for items that can be collected. Garbage The Public Works\xa0Division provides residential curbside garbage collection services within town limits as a service paid for by property taxes.\xa0To start residential service, call 919-296-9600 or use the Contact Public Works form. Multi-family dwellings receive commercial services through independent vendors. For routes and services, see the Curbside Collections Calendar . For information on special collections and requirements, see the Garbage Collection page. Rollout Carts Video Recycling Orange County provides recycling collection services on Wednesdays in Hillsborough.\xa0To set up new recycling service: Use the Orange County recycling contact form . Email recycling@orangecountync.gov . Call 919-968-2788. Rollout Carts Video Yard Waste Yard waste is collected weekly. Leaves can be placed in compostable bags year-round with a limit of 10 bags per household each week. Loose leaves are collected\xa0November through January. During the seasonal loose leaves collection, the town offers delivery of bulk leaves for compost or mulch. See the Yard Waste Collection page for requirements and additional information. Water and Sewer Accounts and Billing Hillsborough provides water and sewer services within town limits and to some properties outside town limits. Connection —\xa0Service should be requested at least three business days in advance. See the Start or Stop Service page. Electronic Bills and Online Payment —\xa0Water and sewer customers are encouraged to sign up for electronic bills and to pay online through the payments and donations site . Leaks —\xa0Customers may request bill adjustments if a water line break has resulted in extraordinary charges for a given billing period.\xa0Customers are allowed one leak adjustment in a12-month period. See the Water and Sewer Billing and Adjustments page. Rates —\xa0To view and understand the town's water and sewer rates, see the Water and Sewer Rates page. Water and Sewer Services Issues Emergencies —\xa0Report water and sewer emergencies immediately to the Hillsborough Water Treatment Plant at 919-732-3621. The plant is staffed 24 hours. Leave a message. Sewer Cleanout Pipes and Rings — Report broken sewer cleanout pipes and rings to utilities staff. The broken pipe should be repaired quickly to prevent stormwater from entering the sewer system. The pipe's protective ring must be kept in place. The capped pipes are located on the edge of rights of way and provide easy access to a property's main sewer line. Contact Utilities or call 919-296-9651. Water Tampering and Theft —\xa0If you see a person or business taking water from a water hydrant without an orange meter box, report it immediately to 911 and to the Hillsborough Water Distribution and Wastewater Collection Division at 919-296-9651. See the Tampering and Theft page. Water and Sewer Responsibilities Avoid Blocked Pipes Fats, Oils and Grease — Keep fats, oils and grease out of drains. They will stick to plumbing and sewer lines and eventually cause sewer backups or overflows. Prevent this by: Discarding used fats, oils and grease with other garbage. Minimizing use of garbage disposals. Scraping plates into trash cans. Using paper towels to wipe leftover oils and fats from pots and pans. The town provides fat trappers to water and sewer customers at the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. Contact Utilities or call 919-296-9653. Wipes — Flush the 3 P's only (pee, poo and toilet paper). Anything else can lead to overflows. Wipes labeled as flushable are not flushable and will clog the sewer system. No Wipes in the Pipes Video Be Aware of Easements and Town Assets Easements —\xa0Water and sewer easements give the Utilities Department the right to access, maintain, repair or replace a town asset, such as a water or sewer main. The town will not replace any plants, fences, sheds, fountains, patios, decks or other structures that impede access to the easement and maintenance of the infrastructure. Fences over easements require an encroachment agreement and other permissions. Contact Utilities or call 919-296-9630 before installing a fence. Private Service Lines —\xa0Homeowners are responsible for these private service lines: The water line between the meter and the home The sewer line between the cleanout pipe and the home. The cleanout pipe's protective ring must be kept in place. The capped pipe provides easy access to your home's main sewer line. Utility Lines and Devices —\xa0Know where utility lines are before digging, and leave access around fire hydrants and other utility devices. Call 811 to have public lines marked for free. Private service lines require marking from private companies. See the North Carolina 811 website. Follow Irrigation Rules Backflow Preventer — Customers with pools or separate irrigation systems must have a backflow preventer. See Backflow Prevention . Spray Irrigation — Spray irrigation requires a separate meter and has limited days and hours for use. Systems must have moisture sensors and should be checked annually for leaks. See: Irrigation Systems on the Water Conservation page Start or Stop Service page"]

Name: 11_backflow_prevention_device_devices
Representative_Docs: ['Testing The town contracts with Backflow Solutions to manage its backflow test data. Customers using backflow prevention assemblies must: Have the assemblies tested when installed. Maintain their assemblies and h ave them tested annually by qualified individuals. Submit results of testing and maintenance to Backflow Solutions. The company maintains a list of licensed, registered testing agencies on its website. Contact Backflow Solutions at 800-414-4990 or bsionline@backflow.com. Visit its website at BSI Online . Process Existing Assemblies — Licensed testers must submit test results through the Backflow Solutions website . Failed Test\xa0— T he assembly must be repaired within 10 days if it fails a test. A certified tester must submit retest results on the Backflow Solutions website . New Installations — A licensed tester who performed the work must sign a printed test form. A copy must be emailed to Backflow Solutions.', 'Protecting Devices in Winter Protection All backflow devices are required to be in a class 1 or 2 enclosure that meets the American Society of Sanitary Engineering’s 1060 standard. These enclosures help protect against frost and freezing. Steps to protect backflow devices in winter include: Adding pipe insulation, an insulated cover or a heat lamp and heat trace tape. The heat tape should be warm when touched if working properly. Wrapping the protective cover of the device with a blanket or other type of insulator. Dripping water from an interior faucet to have continuous movement of water through the assembly. Backflow devices for irrigation can be removed for winterization. They must be retested when reinstalled in the spring. Repair or Replacement For repair or replacement of a device that has frozen and burst, contact a business certified to repair or replace backflow preventers and then have the device tested. For a list of licensed, registered testing agencies, visit the Backflow Solutions website at BSI Online . If the preventer is replaced, contact Utilities Infrastructure Protection Supervisor Troy Miller to update your account information.', "The town regulates connections to its water supply to prevent contamination and to protect public safety. Backflow prevention devices ensure water flows only in one direction. A number of uses require installation of a backflow prevention device, including irrigation systems. A backflow prevention device with protection for the winter Tampering and Theft About Backflow What is it? Backflow is the unwanted reversal of the flow of water and substances.\xa0Typically, water will flow from the public water supply to the consumer’s plumbing system. When backflow occurs, the water flows from the non-drinking water source to the drinking water source and potentially contaminates it. What causes it? Backflow incidents are caused by one of the following: Back pressure — This is created when the pressure within the customer’s system becomes greater than the water supply pressure. Elevated tanks, heating systems and booster pumps are some of the main causes of back pressure. Back siphonage — This is created when the water supply main has negative or reduced pressure. Water main breaks or hydrant flushing are two of the main causes of back siphonage. About How the Town Protects Water The town has requirements to protect the public drinking water supply. Containment Protection — The town installs backflow\xa0prevention at the service meter to protect the public water supply from contamination. Isolation Protection — The town requires backflow prevention at the fixture\xa0where water is used. This protects the customer's internal drinking water plumbing from contamination. Uses Requiring Backflow Prevention Several types of facilities are required to install a backflow prevention device. If your property or business type is not included in the list below and you have a specific question about your legal requirements, contact the town's utilities infrastructure protection supervisor. Auto repair shops Auxiliary water supply (wells) Barber shops and beauty salons Breweries Buildings that reuse or recycle water Buildings with commercial or public kitchens, groundwater wells, multiple\xa0water service lines, roof tanks and elevated storage lines, or water-cooled equipment or chillers Canneries Commercial car washes Dye plants Exterminators Fire sprinkler system Food preparation facilities Food processing plants and meat or fish packers Funeral parlors Greenhouses Lawn irrigation systems Metal plating, cleaning, processing or fabricating facilities Hospitals, clinics and laboratories (including veterinary hospitals) Ice manufacturing facilities Large residential dwellings with water boilers that use rust-inhibitors or other\xa0water treatment chemicals (treated water boilers) Laundries and dry cleaners Medical and dental offices Morgues, mortuaries and autopsy facilities Nursing homes Paper processors Photo-processing facilities Printing facilities Schools and colleges Sewage treatment plants or handling facilities Slaughterhouses and live poultry processing facilities Swimming pools Supermarkets Tanneries Warehouses with toxic chemical storage Cross-Connection Control Requirements See Cross-Connection Control Policy . No Direct Connections The town prohibits direct connections between the water supply and any potential source of contamination.\xa0Potential sources of contamination must be isolated by an air gap or a reduced pressure zone backflow assembly. A direct connection would occur if a garden hose became submerged in a swimming pool, hot tub, soapy water bucket, pond or pet water dish. Water Use by Businesses Contracted businesses working in Hillsborough and needing water must do one of the following: Transport water into town. Purchase bulk water from the town. Rent a hydrant meter from the town. The orange box that is connected to some hydrants contains a meter and a backflow prevention device. See: Start or Stop Service page for bulk water purchases or hydrant meter rentals Tampering and Theft page Outdoor Faucet Requirements The town requires a hose bib vacuum breaker on every outside faucet. The vacuum breaker must be certified by the NSF 61 standard. The American Water Works Association recommends replacing vacuum breakers every five years or if they are failing. The device has failed if water is spraying out of the breaker's weep holes. Testing The town contracts with Backflow Solutions to manage its backflow test data. Customers using backflow prevention assemblies must: Have the assemblies tested when installed. Maintain their assemblies and h ave them tested annually by qualified individuals. Submit results of testing and maintenance to Backflow Solutions. The company maintains a list of licensed, registered testing agencies on its website. Contact Backflow Solutions at 800-414-4990 or bsionline@backflow.com. Visit its website at BSI Online . Process Existing Assemblies — Licensed testers must submit test results through the Backflow Solutions website . Failed Test\xa0— T he assembly must be repaired within 10 days if it fails a test. A certified tester must submit retest results on the Backflow Solutions website . New Installations — A licensed tester who performed the work must sign a printed test form. A copy must be emailed to Backflow Solutions. Preventing Backflow Backflow Prevention Methods Air Gap — An air gap is the vertical space between a water inlet and the rim of a receptacle receiving public water. Examples are a faucet or hose providing water to a bucket, sink or pool.\xa0The air gap must be at least twice the diameter of the water inlet and no smaller than 1 inch. Backflow Assembly — This mechanical unit prevents backflow. It does not have inlet and outlet shutoff valves. It has four test cocks, making inline testing possible. Public water systems, which regulate connections to the public water supply, generally require backflow assemblies rather than devices. Backflow Device — This mechanical unit prevents backflow. It does not have inlet or outlet shutoff valves and usually does not have test cocks. Inline testing is not possible for devices without test cocks. The devices are not approved by the University of Southern California Foundation for Cross-Connection Control and Hydraulic Research. Backflow devices generally are governed by plumbing code when a building is constructed or upfitted. Elimination —\xa0Eliminating the cross-connection is necessary when the hazard has been improperly installed and plumbed or when the hazard is no longer in use. Common Backflow Prevention Assemblies and Uses Double Check Valve Assembly Non-health hazards Back pressure/back siphonage Fire sprinkler system with no booster pumps, most commercial establishments Double Check Valve Detector Assembly Non-health hazards Back pressure/back siphonage Fire sprinkler systems that are classified as non-health hazards Reduced Pressure Detector Assembly Health and non-health hazards Back pressure/back siphonage Fire sprinkler systems that are classified as health hazards Reduced Pressure Principal Assembly (preferred assembly) Health and non-health hazards Hospitals, car washes, lawn irrigation, mortuaries Common Backflow Prevention Devices and Uses Pressure Vacuum Breaker Back siphonage only Multi-zone irrigation systems Hose Connection Vacuum Breaker (not testable) Back siphonage only Garden hose connections Protecting Devices in Winter Protection All backflow devices are required to be in a class 1 or 2 enclosure that meets the American Society of Sanitary Engineering’s 1060 standard. These enclosures help protect against frost and freezing. Steps to protect backflow devices in winter include: Adding pipe insulation, an insulated cover or a heat lamp and heat trace tape. The heat tape should be warm when touched if working properly. Wrapping the protective cover of the device with a blanket or other type of insulator. Dripping water from an interior faucet to have continuous movement of water through the assembly. Backflow devices for irrigation can be removed for winterization. They must be retested when reinstalled in the spring. Repair or Replacement For repair or replacement of a device that has frozen and burst, contact a business certified to repair or replace backflow preventers and then have the device tested. For a list of licensed, registered testing agencies, visit the Backflow Solutions website at BSI Online . If the preventer is replaced, contact Utilities Infrastructure Protection Supervisor Troy Miller to update your account information. Recommendations for Pool and Hot Tub Owners Due to the risk for backflow, the town recommends that customers filling swimming pools, hot tubs, ponds or other bodies of water: Use the air gap method. See the section on backflow prevention methods. Avoid times when hydrants are opened for flushing. This occurs in March and occasionally in the fall. Water System Flushing Lower Water Pressure —\xa0When hydrants are flushed, pressure in the water system drops. Water could backflow into the public water supply system if water main pressure is lower and a hose becomes submerged in a body of water being filled. Chemical Change —\xa0During the annual March flushing, the town’s water is disinfected by chlorination instead of chloramines (ammonia and chlorine). The state requires this change in disinfection for one month each year. The change could upset the balance of a pool, pond or spa. Contact Information Adron F. Thompson Water/Sewer Facility,\xa0715 Dimmocks Mill Road 919-296-9650 Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Water and Sewer Emergencies — Report immediately by calling 919-732-3621. Tampering or Theft —\xa0Call 911 and then use the contact utilities form. Contact Utilities Staff Title Phone Email Miller, Troy Utilities Infrastructure Protection Supervisor 919-296-9653 Email"]

Name: 12_budget_fiscal_financial_year
Representative_Docs: ["Staff from the Budget Division and Financial Services Department manage the budget and finances for the town. Two volunteer boards also have annual budgets to handle funds they oversee: the Tourism Board and Tourism Development Authority . Nonprofit Partnerships Stormwater Fees Tax Rates and Compliance Water and Sewer Rates Budget Budget Budget The budget is a financial plan for the fiscal year (July 1-June 30).\xa0Hillsborough uses a three‐year format to better see any fiscal or operational challenges.  The budget's first year is legally binding. Years 2 and 3 show needs to plan for. The budget contains: An estimate of proposed expenditures. The proposed means of financing the expenditures. Goals and objectives of the expenditures. The Budget Division is responsible for preparing the town's budget.\xa0When possible, the town applies for federal, state and other grants to cover some of the cost of town projects. Manager's Recommended Budget Fiscal Year 2027 Budget and Financial Plan Fiscal Year 2027 Budget Message Fiscal Year 2027 Budget at a Glance Current Budget Fiscal Year 2026 Budget Ordinance Fiscal Year 2026 Budget and Financial Plan Fiscal Year 2026 Budget at a Glance Fiscal Year 2026 Fees and Charges Schedule Fiscal Year 2026 Budget Message Budget Process The budget process takes nearly eight months. November — Town staff begin working on budget proposals for the next fiscal year, which starts July 1. January-March — The mayor and commissioners meet with key staff members in a retreat during the winter to plan for the budget. The retreat gives staff guidance on the priorities and direction for the budget. May — The town manager presents a recommended budget to the town board. The community is invited to review and share comments on the budget at a public hearing later in the month. The board also conducts a work session to discuss the budget at the same meeting. June —\xa0A second work session occurs in June if needed. The board votes on the budget at a regularly scheduled meeting in June. July — The approved budget goes into effect on July 1. Funds The town’s three budgetary funds are the General Fund, Stormwater Fund, and Water and Sewer Fund. General Fund — Primarily funded with property and sales tax revenues and pays for services like police protection, residential garbage collection and building and maintenance of recreation facilities. Stormwater and water and sewer funds — Enterprise funds that rely on user fees to pay for their specific services. No profits are made in enterprise funds, and the revenue is used for no other purpose. Strategic Plan The town uses a strategic plan to align available resources with priorities. The plan is used in coordination with the budget and the Comprehensive Sustainability Plan. Strategic Plan Comprehensive Sustainability Plan Financial Report Annual Financial Report The Annual Comprehensive Financial Report is a complete set of the town's financial statements for the previous fiscal year (July 1-June 30). The Financial Services Department oversees preparation of the annual report. The town's most recent annual financial report Past reports in the town's archives Volunteer\xa0Boards Budgets and Financial Statements Two of the town's appointed boards have activities supported by tax proceeds. Tourism Board activities are supported\xa0from a 1% tax on prepared food and beverages. Tourism Development Authority activities are supported from a 3% room occupancy tax. Name: 13_stormwater_runoff_pollution_illegal
Representative_Docs: ["About Stormwater Stormwater is rainfall or snow melt that flows across surfaces into waterways. It is North Carolina’s top water-quality problem. Stormwater is not treated and often picks up pollutants as it moves across surfaces, like exposed soil, pavement and rooftops. Pollutants include: Chemicals, oil and grease Excess fertilizer and pet waste Sediment and trash The pollution can harm aquatic ecosystems and drinking water supplies. Hillsborough's drinking water comes from the Eno River. Documents Climate Impact — Rainfall intensity's effect on flooding Do You Like to Fish — Stormwater runoff pollution and its effect on fish State and Federal Regulations Hillsborough must adhere to stormwater runoff regulations from the federal and state governments. Federal Regulation Stormwater is regulated under the 1972 Federal Water Pollution Control Act, commonly called the Clean Water Act. The Environmental Protection Agency regulates discharges through the National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System . Discharges include stormwater. State Regulation Within the state, the N.C. Department of Environmental Quality operates the\xa0stormwater program for the National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System. Under Phase II of the rules, small municipalities that own and operate separate storm sewers must obtain a permit and  reduce stormwater pollutants from entering their systems through six minimum measures. The minimum measures for Hillsborough are: Public education and outreach on stormwater impacts. Public involvement and participation. Illicit discharge detection and elimination. Construction site stormwater runoff control (erosion control). Post-construction stormwater management for new development and redevelopment. Pollution prevention and good housekeeping for municipal operations. Local Regulations Hillsborough is required to regulate runoff within town limits. See the effect of stormwater runoff pollution on businesses in the document Don't Go Down the Drain . See the Public Infrastructure Permits page for: Construction and post-construction stormwater management requirements and applications. Information on riparian (stream) buffers and stream identification requests. Town Stormwater Plans Bellevue Branch Watershed Improvement Plan Stormwater Management Plan Illegal Dumping and Connections Report any suspected stormwater pollution or illegal dumping into the town's stormwater system. It is illegal to: Place anything other than stormwater into any part of the town’s stormwater system, including storm drains and ditches. Connect pipes to the town’s stormwater system without approval. Illegal Dumping Signs of illegal dumping: Excessive algae Floating debris Oil sheen Strong pungent or musty odor Storm pipes flowing in dry weather Surface scum or foam Unusual color or cloudiness Examples of illegal dumping: Chlorinated backwash or the draining of swimming pools Contaminated foundation drains Oil, antifreeze, chemicals, garbage, paint or cleaning fluids Sewer and septic tank discharges Untreated animal waste Wash waters from commercial and industrial activities Washing machine discharges Required Detection The town is required to find and eliminate stormwater pollution. If an illegal connection or discharge is found, the property owner will be notified. The owner could face civil penalties up to $5,000 if the issue is not corrected.", "Events No results found. News No results found. The Stormwater and Environmental Services Division reduces stormwater runoff pollution from reaching local water bodies by: Complying with the town’s National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System permit. Regulating development practices. Educating the public about ways to reduce stormwater runoff impacts. Stormwater staff frequently make site visits to assess stormwater runoff issues in the community. It is North Carolina’s top water-quality problem. Stormwater is not treated and often picks up pollutants as it moves across surfaces, like exposed soil, pavement and rooftops. Pollutants include: Chemicals, oil and grease Excess fertilizer and pet waste Sediment and trash The pollution can harm aquatic ecosystems and drinking water supplies. Hillsborough's drinking water comes from the Eno River. Documents Climate Impact — Rainfall intensity's effect on flooding Do You Like to Fish — Stormwater runoff pollution and its effect on fish State and Federal Regulations Hillsborough must adhere to stormwater runoff regulations from the federal and state governments. Federal Regulation Stormwater is regulated under the 1972 Federal Water Pollution Control Act, commonly called the Clean Water Act. The Environmental Protection Agency regulates discharges through the National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System . Discharges include stormwater. State Regulation Within the state, the N.C. Department of Environmental Quality operates the\xa0stormwater program for the National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System. Under Phase II of the rules, small municipalities that own and operate separate storm sewers must obtain a permit and  reduce stormwater pollutants from entering their systems through six minimum measures. The minimum measures for Hillsborough are: Public education and outreach on stormwater impacts. Public involvement and participation. Illicit discharge detection and elimination. Construction site stormwater runoff control (erosion control). Post-construction stormwater management for new development and redevelopment. Pollution prevention and good housekeeping for municipal operations. Local Regulations Hillsborough is required to regulate runoff within town limits. See the effect of stormwater runoff pollution on businesses in the document Don't Go Down the Drain . See the Public Infrastructure Permits page for: Construction and post-construction stormwater management requirements and applications. Information on riparian (stream) buffers and stream identification requests. Town Stormwater Plans Bellevue Branch Watershed Improvement Plan Stormwater Management Plan Illegal Dumping and Connections Report any suspected stormwater pollution or illegal dumping into the town's stormwater system. It is illegal to: Place anything other than stormwater into any part of the town’s stormwater system, including storm drains and ditches. Connect pipes to the town’s stormwater system without approval. Illegal Dumping Signs of illegal dumping: Excessive algae Floating debris Oil sheen Strong pungent or musty odor Storm pipes flowing in dry weather Surface scum or foam Unusual color or cloudiness Examples of illegal dumping: Chlorinated backwash or the draining of swimming pools Contaminated foundation drains Oil, antifreeze, chemicals, garbage, paint or cleaning fluids Sewer and septic tank discharges Untreated animal waste Wash waters from commercial and industrial activities Washing machine discharges Required Detection The town is required to find and eliminate stormwater pollution. If an illegal connection or discharge is found, the property owner will be notified. The owner could face civil penalties up to $5,000 if the issue is not corrected. Stormwater and Your Property Rights and Reasonable Use In North Carolina, common law governs stormwater runoff and drainage. Basically, a downhill property has to receive water from uphill. Water has a right to go where it wants to go ― downhill. Components of this common law: The easement of an uphill owner includes the right to accelerate and increase the natural flow of water. Natural flow means the uphill owner cannot divert or cause water to flow onto the lower land in a different manner or place than it would naturally. The downhill owner may not obstruct natural flow from above. Property owners have the legal right to make reasonable use of their property, even if development alters water flow and affects downstream owners. The uphill owner can incur liability when the change in runoff is unreasonable and causes substantial damage. Documents Stormwater Runoff Conveyances Water Flows Downhill Maintenance Responsibilities Property owners are responsible for the stormwater control measures on their private property. Owners include homeowners associations. Drainage Ditches Along Streets Property owners are required to mow drainage ditches along town streets. The town ensures water flow is not restricted in the drainage ditches along town streets. Contact the Public Works Division to request maintenance of drainage ditches. Wet and Dry Ponds Property owners are required to have  wet and dry ponds inspected annually by a private licensed inspector. The inspection report must be submitted to the Stormwater and Environmental Services Division. The third-party annual inspections may result in recommendations for maintenance or improvements. Before making a costly repair, contact town staff to check that the work is necessary. See the Ponds and Geese section if a recommendation involves addressing geese. Ponds and Geese The Stormwater and Environmental Services Division requires a 6-foot vegetated area around ponds used as stormwater control measures. In this area, non-woody vegetation must be allowed to grow to a natural height during the growing season. When this area is newly planted, Canada geese may eat or pull out the vegetation before it is fully established. Goose management can be used to ensure this vegetation is established and maintained. Once grown to a natural height, this vegetation can also help deter geese from accessing the pond. This deterrence method works best if the vegetated area is maintained consistently around the entire pond. Canada geese are protected under the Migratory Bird Treaty Act . They are attracted to ponds, including those used as stormwater control measures. Several geese living by a pond can hurt water quality. They eat young grass shoots that help keep pollution from reaching a pond, and they drop fecal matter that can run into the water. Lack of vegetation can cause shoreline erosion, and both erosion and excessive fecal matter can lead to odor problems, algal blooms and fish kills in the pond and downstream of the pond. To improve water quality, pond management companies may suggest ways to manage Canada geese. The Stormwater and Environmental Services Division does not require property owners to deter or chase away geese. Deterring Geese Hazing Canada geese is a legal option in North Carolina for deterring geese. See more information about hazing on the North Carolina Wildlife Resources Commission's website. Some additional methods for deterring geese include planting tall vegetation and installing temporary or permanent fencing. Often, pond owners with the most success are those who hire a management company to help them and who use multiple methods simultaneously to deter geese. Stream Identification and Riparian Buffers The town enforces a 50-foot buffer of plantings along streams and the Eno River. Prior approval is required to clear, build or make other impacts within a stream or river buffer, also called a riparian buffer. See the Public Infrastructure Permits page for additional information and to request stream identification services. See Riparian (Stream) Buffers . About Stream and River Buffers Buffers along water bodies are undisturbed, naturally forested areas that: Provide wildlife food and habitat. Slow stormwater runoff by allowing it to soak into the ground. Buffers help protect water quality by: Controlling erosion and keeping stream banks from collapsing. Reducing flooding and pollution of waterways. Moderating stream temperature through the amount and quality of sunlight allowed on a waterbody. Woody debris in waterways also protect water quality and provide habitat for aquatic organisms. See the document Woody Debris in Streams and Rivers . Resources The division offers materials to help property owners manage stormwater and protect the environment. Contact staff to request a site visit and advice. Documents Compost Blankets Designing A Rain Garden Don't Go Down the Drain — effect of stormwater runoff pollution on businesses Lawn Care For Water Quality Stormwater Bioswales Stormwater Runoff Conveyances Water Flows Downhill Woody Debris In Streams And Rivers Websites These sites were created in partnership with\xa0Elon University students: Enhance Your Yard with Green Infrastructure — Focusing on water management that works with the natural water cycle. Climate Impact in Residential Areas — Spotlighting the effects of climate change on the local area and actions community members can take. Videos Rain Garden More Information Programs and Volunteer Opportunities Annual Herbicide Treatment Typically, the state treats the Eno River annually to reduce the invasive plant hydrilla. The town helps fund the treatment through stormwater fees. See the About Water page. Educational Programs The town partners with Orange County to provide educational programs and presentations.\xa0Topics include: Preventing stormwater pollution Maintaining stormwater ponds Other best practices Programs typically are provided for civic,\xa0school and local watershed groups, elected officials, developers, contractors and other groups interested in learning more about stormwater issues. Contact stormwater staff to request a program or presentation. Other Partnerships and Opportunities Clean Water Education Partnership —\xa0The town is a supporting member of this partnership to protect water quality in the Tar-Pamlico, Neuse and Cape Fear river basins. Hillsborough's watershed is part of the Upper Neuse River Basin. The partnership is a cooperative effort among local governments, state agencies and nonprofit organizations. It uses education and outreach to protect water quality. Eno River Association —\xa0The town partners with the association\xa0in preserving and conserving lands along the Eno River. Community members can join the association's stream monitoring program to help protect the local watershed. Volunteer Workdays\xa0— Stormwater staff offer\xa0volunteer workdays at parks, wetlands and other areas around town. Subscribe to town news releases . Publications and Reports Annual Reports Falls Lake Nutrient Sensitive Water Report\xa0—\xa0The report summarizes efforts related to new and existing developments in the previous fiscal year (July 1-June 30). It is required by the N.C. Department of Environmental Quality\xa0under the Falls Lake Stormwater Rule for New Development. Falls Lake Nutrient Sensitive Water Annual Report National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System Phase II Report — The report summarizes activities of the town’s stormwater management program for the previous fiscal year (July 1-June 30). It\xa0is required under the federal Clean Water Act and state regulations. National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System Phase II Annual Report Stormwater Almanac The division periodically publishes the Stormwater Almanac with news and information on the division's recent projects. Stormwater Almanac Awards Awards received by the division include: Green Infrastructure Project of the Year ― For the Odie Street bioswales project from the\xa0Stormwater Management Division of the American Public Works Association North Carolina Chapter in 2023. Contact Information Town Hall, 101 E. Orange St. 919-296-9621 Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Use the contact form or staff directory to report a stormwater pollution concern or to request an educational program. Name: 14_officer_force_resistance_reasonably
Representative_Docs: ["After exhausting reasonable attempts to gain voluntary compliance, when it is objectively reasonable under the circumstances, and in accordance with North Carolina General Statute 15A-401(d), officers of the Hillsborough Police Department are: Authorized to use force upon another person when and to the extent they believe it is reasonably necessary: To effect the arrest or prevent the escape from custody of a person the officer reasonably believes has committed a criminal offense, unless the officer knows the arrest is unauthorized. To defend the officer or a third party from what the officer reasonably believes to be the use or imminent use of physical force while effecting or attempting to effect an arrest or while preventing or attempting to prevent an escape. Authorized to use deadly physical force upon another person only when that force is, or appears to be, reasonably necessary to the officer: To defend the officer or a third party from what the officer reasonably believes to be the use or imminent use of deadly physical force. To effect the arrest or prevent the escape from custody of a person who the officer reasonably believes is attempting to escape by means of a deadly weapon or who, by the person's conduct or any other means, indicates an imminent threat of death or serious physical injury to others unless apprehended without delay. Forbidden from using deadly force to prevent the escape of a person from custody imposed upon the person as a result of conviction for a felony as provided by North Carolina General Statute 15A-401(d)(2)(c), unless one of the elements described in the second legal authority section above is present.", 'Officers must continually assess the situation and escalate or deescalate their use of force based on the circumstances they are facing. The level of resistance being faced and the safety of the officer and subject should considered when selecting a use of force option. Force options exist in a continuum. The available options often overlap, and multiple techniques may be used in response to resistance exhibited by a suspect. Every situation can be unique. Verbal Commands — During any encounter, unless an imminent or immediate threat makes it impractical to do so, some form of verbal commands or directions shall be given to a citizen who is exhibiting resistance. Soft Hand Guidance — This is physically touching a subject to provide guidance, hold in place, apply handcuffs or search. As a matter of safety, soft hand guidance is reasonably anticipated to occur in any detention situation, regardless of how compliant a subject is. Physical Intervention and Compliance — This is contact intended to force compliance. The choice of technique or tool shall be made based on the resistance being faced and intended to minimize the possibility of serious injury to the officer or the citizen. Control Techniques — This is pulling, pushing or dragging a citizen, taking a citizen to the ground, using pressure points, arm bars or other control tactics. Less Lethal — This refers to conducted energy weapons (Taser) or aerosol spray. Hard Hands — This refers to physical strikes with a closed fist or some other part of the body that could be reasonably anticipated to cause and injury. Impact Weapons — This refers to baton or other device (such as a radio or flashlight) used to strike a subject. Deadly Force — This refers to a firearm and/or any use of force option or tool that is intended or can reasonably be anticipated to cause death or serious bodily injury through its use. Officers must choose the most appropriate level of force based on the situation they are facing and their training and experience. The force option selected must be reasonable, appropriate and proportional to the level of resistance an officer is encountering or reasonably believes imminent. The graphic is a guide to help officers understand the selection of the appropriate level of force in response to resistance and aggression.', 'Use of Force Continuum Officers must continually assess the situation and escalate or deescalate their use of force based on the circumstances they are facing. The level of resistance being faced and the safety of the officer and subject should considered when selecting a use of force option. Force options exist in a continuum. The available options often overlap, and multiple techniques may be used in response to resistance exhibited by a suspect. Every situation can be unique. Verbal Commands — During any encounter, unless an imminent or immediate threat makes it impractical to do so, some form of verbal commands or directions shall be given to a citizen who is exhibiting resistance. Soft Hand Guidance — This is physically touching a subject to provide guidance, hold in place, apply handcuffs or search. As a matter of safety, soft hand guidance is reasonably anticipated to occur in any detention situation, regardless of how compliant a subject is. Physical Intervention and Compliance — This is contact intended to force compliance. The choice of technique or tool shall be made based on the resistance being faced and intended to minimize the possibility of serious injury to the officer or the citizen. Control Techniques — This is pulling, pushing or dragging a citizen, taking a citizen to the ground, using pressure points, arm bars or other control tactics. Less Lethal — This refers to conducted energy weapons (Taser) or aerosol spray. Hard Hands — This refers to physical strikes with a closed fist or some other part of the body that could be reasonably anticipated to cause and injury. Impact Weapons — This refers to baton or other device (such as a radio or flashlight) used to strike a subject. Deadly Force — This refers to a firearm and/or any use of force option or tool that is intended or can reasonably be anticipated to cause death or serious bodily injury through its use. Officers must choose the most appropriate level of force based on the situation they are facing and their training and experience. The force option selected must be reasonable, appropriate and proportional to the level of resistance an officer is encountering or reasonably believes imminent. The graphic is a guide to help officers understand the selection of the appropriate level of force in response to resistance and aggression.']
