Name: 16_usage_water_customers_minimum
Representative_Docs: ['The town is committed to finding ways to help relieve customers from the high cost of funding quality, reliable water and sewer service. Efforts include: Rates Study — State law prevents the town from charging different rates to customers based on income. A rate study to evaluate the town’s rate structures and to begin exploring other options to lower costs for customers was completed in Fiscal Year 2024. This study weighed the expected costs for operations and large projects against required revenue between fiscal years 2025 and 2040. It showed the need for increases in rates in Fiscal Year 2025 and in future years. The model helps the town plan for meeting needs and important financial metrics, like days of cash on hand and debt coverage ratio. Lower Minimum Usage — Since 2013, t he town has reduced the monthly minimum number of gallons that customers are charged for water and sewer use from 3,000 to 2,000 gallons. Spreading the reduction over a number of years minimized the effect  of losing revenue. Water Assistance Program — The town started the Water Assistance Program in 2006 to help eligible customers pay water and sewer bills. Candidates are recommended by the Orange County Department of Social Services or a local church. Innovation — Town employees regularly increase efficiency of the water and sewer system through innovation, defer lesser priority projects, and perform much work in house. The United States Environmental Protection Agency has included the town’s wastewater treatment plant in a case study on innovation efforts for the treatment of wastewater. Pump Station Reduction — The town is working to reduce the number of pump stations it operates. The pumps keep sewage flowing in low areas, but they are expensive to maintain and the town has more than a typical municipality. Since 2005, two pump stations have been eliminated.', 'Efforts to Provide Water and Sewer Rates Relief The town is committed to finding ways to help relieve customers from the high cost of funding quality, reliable water and sewer service. Efforts include: Rates Study — State law prevents the town from charging different rates to customers based on income. A rate study to evaluate the town’s rate structures and to begin exploring other options to lower costs for customers was completed in Fiscal Year 2024. This study weighed the expected costs for operations and large projects against required revenue between fiscal years 2025 and 2040. It showed the need for increases in rates in Fiscal Year 2025 and in future years. The model helps the town plan for meeting needs and important financial metrics, like days of cash on hand and debt coverage ratio. Lower Minimum Usage — Since 2013, t he town has reduced the monthly minimum number of gallons that customers are charged for water and sewer use from 3,000 to 2,000 gallons. Spreading the reduction over a number of years minimized the effect  of losing revenue. Water Assistance Program — The town started the Water Assistance Program in 2006 to help eligible customers pay water and sewer bills. Candidates are recommended by the Orange County Department of Social Services or a local church. Innovation — Town employees regularly increase efficiency of the water and sewer system through innovation, defer lesser priority projects, and perform much work in house. The United States Environmental Protection Agency has included the town’s wastewater treatment plant in a case study on innovation efforts for the treatment of wastewater. Pump Station Reduction — The town is working to reduce the number of pump stations it operates. The pumps keep sewage flowing in low areas, but they are expensive to maintain and the town has more than a typical municipality. Since 2005, two pump stations have been eliminated.', "Minimum Usage Charge The town charges for a minimum amount of water and sewer use each month. Additional use for both is billed per 1,000 gallons. Providing the availability of water and sewer service costs the town money even if a customer uses no water that month. The costs include: Managing and protecting Hillsborough's water source. Treating, storing and distributing drinking water. Collecting, treating and returning used water to the environment. Managing water and sewer billing, including meter reading, billing software, and bill printing and mailing. These operations also have costs for qualified workers, facilities, vehicles, equipment, chemicals, electricity, gas, compliance with regulations, and administrative needs. Minimum Fee Versus Base Fee All water and sewer systems charge a base fee or a minimum usage fee to ensure they can provide service. Base fee — No water is provided  with the monthly charge. Minimum usage fee — Water is provided with the monthly charge up to the minimum number of gallons set. In-Town Versus Out-of-Town Rates The most recent published annual research in North Carolina shows a higher outside rate is charged by 98% of water systems and 95% of sewer systems that offer service outside their geographical limits. The annual study is provided by the UNC School of Government's Environmental Finance Center and the North Carolina League of Municipalities. Out-of-town water and sewer fees are higher than in-town fees due to: Cost of Service — The operations, maintenance, pumping of water and sewer, and reading of meters can be more costly in areas outside of town, mainly because of greater distance and travel time from the town’s water or wastewater treatment plants. Fewer customers in the area to spread the cost can make the service more expensive. Growth Management — P roviding water and sewer service to developments outside of town can cause the town to lose some ability to attract development that would improve its tax base, help improve the economies of scale in providing services, and potentially ease the tax burden on residents.  Lower in-town fees also can make annexation more desirable in areas that have water and sewer service. Investment Risk — In-town customers bear more of the investment risks of owning and operating the water and sewer system. When revenue bonds are issued to finance large projects, like the reservoir, the town pledges its full  rate setting authority for water and sewer as collateral to ensure the debt will be repaid. Equity — In-town customers bear more of the burden of financing and facilitating the water and sewer system’s operations. In addition to water and sewer payments, their municipal property taxes help pay for positions and resources that support the water and sewer operations. They also subsidize the use of Hillsborough services by out-of-town customers who live near the town’s corporate limits, including: Streets Police protection when inside town limits Recreation facilities, such as Gold Park, Kings Highway Park and the Riverwalk greenway Understanding the Cost of Providing Service Every community has different conditions that affect the ability to provide safe and reliable water and sewer service. Below are primary reasons that Hillsborough’s water and sewer rates are higher than those of some surrounding communities. Small System —\xa0Hillsborough operates a small water and sewer system. A larger system can spread the costs of operations among more customers. Small Water Supply —\xa0The Eno River does not provide a large volume of water. The town is  near the river's beginning, and the drainage basin that feeds into Hillsborough’s waterways is relatively small. Partnering with a neighboring system is likely not feasible. The Carrboro-Chapel Hill area\xa0is in a different river basin, and a past evaluation found\xa0costs for partnering with Durham to be too expensive. The town built a reservoir in the late 1990s and completed an expansion of it in 2021 to address water capacity needs because of expected growth and a history of droughts. Debt Management —\xa0A portion of  water and sewer bill payments helps pay the debt on projects to provide clean and reliable water and sewer service, including: Wastewater Treatment Plant upgrades —\xa0$17.8 million financed with payments until 2034. Reservoir expansion —\xa0$10.2 million financed with payments until 2042. Roads project for reservoir expansion —\xa0$3.3 million financed with payments until 2030. By law, the town must pass a balanced budget each year. Planned expenses cannot fall below expected revenue, and debt payments cannot create a deficit. State Rules —\xa0Hillsborough must treat its wastewater at higher and more expensive standards than other sewer systems because its watershed drains into Falls Lake. The lake has been deemed impaired by the Environmental Protection Agency and nutrient-sensitive by the North Carolina Division of Water Resources. The state's Falls Lake Rules to protect and restore the lake’s water quality\xa0are said to be the nation’s strictest limits on treated wastewater. In 2014, Hillsborough completed upgrades to its wastewater treatment plant in part to meet the Stage 1 wastewater rule that went into effect in 2016. A large portion of customers' sewer payments helps pay debt on the plant upgrades. Stage 2 rules, expected in 2036,\xa0likely will require more upgrades. Expensive and Older Infrastructure —\xa0Hillsborough has more sewage pump stations than a typical municipality because of its terrain and likely because of low upfront costs.\xa0Gravity is the most cost-effective way to transport water and wastewater. Pump stations are expensive to operate, require a large amount of staff time and can result in overflows and state fines when they fail.\xa0The town operates 25 stations in about 5 square miles. Most were built by developers, are old and were not built to today's standards. The town also must budget to replace other infrastructure. Some were installed well before the town's treatment plants were built. Maintenance Commitment —\xa0Water and sewer services are expensive to provide. The United States  poorly maintains its drinking and wastewater infrastructure, earning grades of C- and D+ in the 2025 Report Card for America's Infrastructure from the American Society of Civil Engineers. Across North Carolina , $20 billion is needed to maintain and improve drinking water infrastructure and $12.6 billion is needed for wastewater infrastructure. Hillsborough is committed to taking care of its operations. Your water and sewer bill reflects your contribution to the labor and the physical infrastructure required to have clean water available and to have wastewater removed and safely returned to the environment. The town does not profit from providing water and sewer service. It uses water and sewer revenue only for the operation, maintenance and improvement of the town’s water and sewer system. Videos Full Cost Pricing of Water Water: What You Pay For Understanding Enterprise Funds The Water and Sewer Fund is one of two enterprise funds operated by the town. The other is the Stormwater Utility Fund. Enterprise funds account for the business-like activities of the government. These services are funded with revenues generated from fees and charges rather than taxes.\xa0No profits are made in enterprise funds, and the revenue is used for no other purpose. The Hillsborough Water and Sewer Fund pays for providing water and sewer service. The revenue for the fund comes\xa0primarily from water and sewer charges. Efforts to Provide Water and Sewer Rates Relief The town is committed to finding ways to help relieve customers from the high cost of funding quality, reliable water and sewer service. Efforts include: Rates Study — State law prevents the town from charging different rates to customers based on income. A rate study to evaluate the town’s rate structures and to begin exploring other options to lower costs for customers was completed in Fiscal Year 2024. This study weighed the expected costs for operations and large projects against required revenue between fiscal years 2025 and 2040. It showed the need for increases in rates in Fiscal Year 2025 and in future years. The model helps the town plan for meeting needs and important financial metrics, like days of cash on hand and debt coverage ratio. Lower Minimum Usage — Since 2013, t he town has reduced the monthly minimum number of gallons that customers are charged for water and sewer use from 3,000 to 2,000 gallons. Spreading the reduction over a number of years minimized the effect  of losing revenue. Water Assistance Program — The town started the Water Assistance Program in 2006 to help eligible customers pay water and sewer bills. Candidates are recommended by the Orange County Department of Social Services or a local church. Innovation — Town employees regularly increase efficiency of the water and sewer system through innovation, defer lesser priority projects, and perform much work in house. The United States Environmental Protection Agency has included the town’s wastewater treatment plant in a case study on innovation efforts for the treatment of wastewater. Pump Station Reduction — The town is working to reduce the number of pump stations it operates. The pumps keep sewage flowing in low areas, but they are expensive to maintain and the town has more than a typical municipality. Since 2005, two pump stations have been eliminated."]

Name: 17_treatment_plant_wastewater_tours
Representative_Docs: ['Wastewater Treatment Plant employees are highly certified and operate and maintain the plant to ensure compliance with state and federal permits and regulations. The plant can treat up to 3 million gallons of wastewater per day.\xa0It was built in 1977, with upgrades in 2001 and\xa02007 to improve solids management, nutrient removal and\xa0treatment capabilities. The most recent expansion and upgrade was completed in 2014, allowing the plant to meet state\xa0requirements to protect\xa0Falls Lake, where Hillsborough’s watershed drains. This upgrade Hillsborough’s discharged water\xa0quality among the best in the nation. Awards In previous years, the Wastewater Treatment Plant and its staff have won awards for: Professionalism in the Operation of a Wastewater Treatment Plant Solutions to Operational Problems Wastewater Treatment Plant Operations and Maintenance Wastewater Treatment Plant Operator Tours See what it takes to treat used water for return to the Eno River. Public tours are offered at 1 p.m. on the second Friday of the month in March and September. Private tours can be scheduled separately for special groups. All tours are open to a maximum of 10 people with participants at least 10 years old. All tours require a completed request form. Request a Tour', 'Wastewater Treatment Plant and Tours Wastewater Treatment Plant employees are highly certified and operate and maintain the plant to ensure compliance with state and federal permits and regulations. The plant can treat up to 3 million gallons of wastewater per day.\xa0It was built in 1977, with upgrades in 2001 and\xa02007 to improve solids management, nutrient removal and\xa0treatment capabilities. The most recent expansion and upgrade was completed in 2014, allowing the plant to meet state\xa0requirements to protect\xa0Falls Lake, where Hillsborough’s watershed drains. This upgrade Hillsborough’s discharged water\xa0quality among the best in the nation. Awards In previous years, the Wastewater Treatment Plant and its staff have won awards for: Professionalism in the Operation of a Wastewater Treatment Plant Solutions to Operational Problems Wastewater Treatment Plant Operations and Maintenance Wastewater Treatment Plant Operator Tours See what it takes to treat used water for return to the Eno River. Public tours are offered at 1 p.m. on the second Friday of the month in March and September. Private tours can be scheduled separately for special groups. All tours are open to a maximum of 10 people with participants at least 10 years old. All tours require a completed request form. Request a Tour', "Our Treated Wastewater Quality The town's wastewater treatment must meet extremely high standards set by the town, state and federal governments.\xa0Hillsborough routinely tests for more than\xa0220\xa0substances in the town's wastewater and solids and meets or exceeds the standards.\xa0See the annual Wastewater Quality Report . Testing Staff monitor and evaluate water 24 hours a day.\xa0The plant has a\xa0laboratory to perform frequent checks on the progress of wastewater treatment. Staff also test various points in the Eno River.  Tests occur: Upstream of the water intake Before and after wastewater discharge Downstream of wastewater discharge Our Wastewater Treatment Process The plant uses an enhanced biological nutrient removal process to treat wastewater.\xa0Naturally occurring nutrients can cause algae growth, depleted oxygen, and fish kills. The nutrients\xa0— like nitrogen and\xa0phosphorus — must be\xa0brought to safe levels before treated wastewater can be discharged into the Eno River. Treatment Steps The Town of Hillsborough's wastewater treatment plant Large item removal — Bar screens remove trash, wood and rocks, which then are compacted for disposal. Grit removal — Sand and dirt are removed for disposal. Secondary treatment — Organic materials and nutrients are removed through a series of aeration tanks and sent to large tanks called digesters (see 4). The remaining wastewater moves to the clarifiers (see 5). Solids treatment — Organic materials and nutrients are treated in digesters and then thickened and trucked to a composting facility to be turned into a soil amendment. Clarification — Rakes slowly skim the wastewater surface for scum like grease, oils, plastics and soap. The scum is pumped to digesters (see 4). Solids settle and are removed for disposal. Sand filtration — The remaining water moves through 6 feet of sand, which removes additional solids. Disinfection, final treatment — Chlorine is added to kill bacteria. Chemicals are added to neutralize the chlorine. Aeration — The water is re-aerated before discharge to remove dissolved gases and to oxidize dissolved metals. Discharge — The treated wastewater is returned to the Eno River at an outfall. Wastewater Treatment Plant and Tours Wastewater Treatment Plant employees are highly certified and operate and maintain the plant to ensure compliance with state and federal permits and regulations. The plant can treat up to 3 million gallons of wastewater per day.\xa0It was built in 1977, with upgrades in 2001 and\xa02007 to improve solids management, nutrient removal and\xa0treatment capabilities. The most recent expansion and upgrade was completed in 2014, allowing the plant to meet state\xa0requirements to protect\xa0Falls Lake, where Hillsborough’s watershed drains. This upgrade Hillsborough’s discharged water\xa0quality among the best in the nation. Awards In previous years, the Wastewater Treatment Plant and its staff have won awards for: Professionalism in the Operation of a Wastewater Treatment Plant Solutions to Operational Problems Wastewater Treatment Plant Operations and Maintenance Wastewater Treatment Plant Operator Tours See what it takes to treat used water for return to the Eno River. Public tours are offered at 1 p.m. on the second Friday of the month in March and September. Private tours can be scheduled separately for special groups. All tours are open to a maximum of 10 people with participants at least 10 years old. All tours require a completed request form. Request a Tour"]

Name: 18_lead_copper_rule_drinking
Representative_Docs: ["Safe Drinking Water Act In 1974, the U.S. Congress passed the Safe Drinking Water Act, which requires the Environmental Protection Agency to determine the safe levels of contaminants in drinking water.\xa0The safe level of lead in drinking water is zero. Congress later amended the act\xa0— effective after June 1986\xa0— to prohibit using pipes, solder or flux that contain lead in public water systems or in plumbing in facilities that provide water for human consumption. Federal Lead and Copper Rule In 1991, the Environmental Protection Agency\xa0published the lead and copper rule to minimize these heavy metals in drinking water.\xa0The rule established a maximum contaminant level goal of zero for lead in drinking water and a treatment technique to reduce corrosion of lead and copper pipe materials within water distribution systems. Rule Revisions In 2021, the Environmental Protection Agency revised the Lead and Copper Rule to improve protection of public health. The revisions\xa0focus on: Identifying and removing lead service lines. Providing increased levels of protection from lead exposure in drinking water to children at schools and childcare facilities. Providing information about lead in drinking water to the community. The revised rules required water systems to develop a publicly available inventory of public and private water service lines and their makeup by Oct. 16, 2024. The inventory must be updated annually. Any unknown materials must be identified within 10 years of the initial inventory compliance date. Resources For more information, see these resources from the Environmental Protection Agency: Safe Drinking Water Act page Lead and Copper Rule page Infographic\xa0— Improving Lead Sampling to Better Protect Public Health News Release\xa0— EPA Finalizes Historic Action to Better Protect Children's Health", "Regulations Safe Drinking Water Act In 1974, the U.S. Congress passed the Safe Drinking Water Act, which requires the Environmental Protection Agency to determine the safe levels of contaminants in drinking water.\xa0The safe level of lead in drinking water is zero. Congress later amended the act\xa0— effective after June 1986\xa0— to prohibit using pipes, solder or flux that contain lead in public water systems or in plumbing in facilities that provide water for human consumption. Federal Lead and Copper Rule In 1991, the Environmental Protection Agency\xa0published the lead and copper rule to minimize these heavy metals in drinking water.\xa0The rule established a maximum contaminant level goal of zero for lead in drinking water and a treatment technique to reduce corrosion of lead and copper pipe materials within water distribution systems. Rule Revisions In 2021, the Environmental Protection Agency revised the Lead and Copper Rule to improve protection of public health. The revisions\xa0focus on: Identifying and removing lead service lines. Providing increased levels of protection from lead exposure in drinking water to children at schools and childcare facilities. Providing information about lead in drinking water to the community. The revised rules required water systems to develop a publicly available inventory of public and private water service lines and their makeup by Oct. 16, 2024. The inventory must be updated annually. Any unknown materials must be identified within 10 years of the initial inventory compliance date. Resources For more information, see these resources from the Environmental Protection Agency: Safe Drinking Water Act page Lead and Copper Rule page Infographic\xa0— Improving Lead Sampling to Better Protect Public Health News Release\xa0— EPA Finalizes Historic Action to Better Protect Children's Health Harmful Effects Harmful Effects Lead in drinking water has the following negative effects, according to the Environmental Protection Agency: Children Even low levels of lead in the blood of children can result in: Behavior and learning problems Lower IQ and hyperactivity Slowed growth Hearing problems Anemia In rare cases, ingestion of lead can cause seizures, coma and death. Pregnant Women Lead can accumulate in our bodies over time, where it is stored in bones along with calcium. During pregnancy, lead is released from bones as maternal calcium and is used to help form the bones of the fetus. This is particularly true if a woman does not have enough dietary calcium. Lead also can cross the placental barrier exposing the fetus to lead. This can result in serious effects to the mother and her developing fetus, including: Reduced growth of the fetus Premature birth All Adults Adults exposed to lead can suffer from: Cardiovascular effects, increased blood pressure, and hypertension Decreased kidney function Reproductive problems (in both men and women) Resources For more information, see these resources from the Environmental Protection Agency: Learn About Lead webpage", "Lead is not typically found in raw water sources, such as lakes, but it can enter drinking water from corrosion of plumbing materials that contain lead. The town adds the corrosion prohibitor orthophosphate into drinking water to prevent corrosion in plumbing materials. Identify Your Service Line Material Water Service Line Material Form Water Service Line Map About Lead in Water Across the country, service lines commonly were made from lead pipe from the late 1800s, throughout much of the 20th century, and in some cities as late as the mid-1980s. After June 1986, installation of new lead service lines was prohibited. Regulations Safe Drinking Water Act In 1974, the U.S. Congress passed the Safe Drinking Water Act, which requires the Environmental Protection Agency to determine the safe levels of contaminants in drinking water.\xa0The safe level of lead in drinking water is zero. Congress later amended the act\xa0— effective after June 1986\xa0— to prohibit using pipes, solder or flux that contain lead in public water systems or in plumbing in facilities that provide water for human consumption. Federal Lead and Copper Rule In 1991, the Environmental Protection Agency\xa0published the lead and copper rule to minimize these heavy metals in drinking water.\xa0The rule established a maximum contaminant level goal of zero for lead in drinking water and a treatment technique to reduce corrosion of lead and copper pipe materials within water distribution systems. Rule Revisions In 2021, the Environmental Protection Agency revised the Lead and Copper Rule to improve protection of public health. The revisions\xa0focus on: Identifying and removing lead service lines. Providing increased levels of protection from lead exposure in drinking water to children at schools and childcare facilities. Providing information about lead in drinking water to the community. The revised rules required water systems to develop a publicly available inventory of public and private water service lines and their makeup by Oct. 16, 2024. The inventory must be updated annually. Any unknown materials must be identified within 10 years of the initial inventory compliance date. Resources For more information, see these resources from the Environmental Protection Agency: Safe Drinking Water Act page Lead and Copper Rule page Infographic\xa0— Improving Lead Sampling to Better Protect Public Health News Release\xa0— EPA Finalizes Historic Action to Better Protect Children's Health Harmful Effects Harmful Effects Lead in drinking water has the following negative effects, according to the Environmental Protection Agency: Children Even low levels of lead in the blood of children can result in: Behavior and learning problems Lower IQ and hyperactivity Slowed growth Hearing problems Anemia In rare cases, ingestion of lead can cause seizures, coma and death. Pregnant Women Lead can accumulate in our bodies over time, where it is stored in bones along with calcium. During pregnancy, lead is released from bones as maternal calcium and is used to help form the bones of the fetus. This is particularly true if a woman does not have enough dietary calcium. Lead also can cross the placental barrier exposing the fetus to lead. This can result in serious effects to the mother and her developing fetus, including: Reduced growth of the fetus Premature birth All Adults Adults exposed to lead can suffer from: Cardiovascular effects, increased blood pressure, and hypertension Decreased kidney function Reproductive problems (in both men and women) Resources For more information, see these resources from the Environmental Protection Agency: Learn About Lead webpage Inventory in Hillsborough Lead in Hillsborough Town records dating to the mid-1930s call for copper or plastic water service lines and not lead. Lead service lines have not come up in staff discussions with local plumbers familiar with the area and have not been found by staff when working throughout the system. Homes and buildings built before 1987 may have lead service lines. Inventory The Hillsborough Utilities Department has developed an inventory of water service line materials in the community to help identify any lead service lines. The Environmental Protection Agency required the initial inventory by Oct. 16, 2024, under revised requirements to the Lead and Copper Rule. Updates must be made each year, and unknown materials must be identified within 10 years. Community Help For some properties, town staff continue to need community members’ help in identifying or correcting the materials of their private water service lines. Private lines are those between the building and water meter. In this inventory, the service line is under 2 inches in diameter. Please check the town’s Water Service Line Map to see if your building’s service line is marked as unknown. If the town does not have information about the customer side of your water service line, please determine the material by checking inspection reports for your home or building or by performing simple tests on the Identify Your Service Line Material page. Once you have identified your pipe material, complete the Water Service Line Material Form . Attach documentation of your service line material, such as a photo, and any repairs made to the service line after 1986 for homes or buildings built before 1987. Water Service Line Map Instructions Use the Water Service Line Map to see if\xa0the service line material at your property has been identified. Enter your address in the search field at the top of the map. Your property will become highlighted on the map. A circle at the property will display materials for both sides of the service line\xa0— utility and customer sides. See the map’s legend\xa0for the color coding of your circle. The circle’s left side represents\xa0the water service line owned by the town. The circle’s right side represents the water service line owned by the customer. If the material is unknown or incorrect, the town needs your help in identifying the material or reporting the correct material. A circle with\xa0gray on the right side means the town does not have information on the water service line for that property. Precautions Precautions the Town Takes Corrosion Inhibitor The town adds a blended phosphate corrosion inhibitor to its water. This additive greatly reduces the risk of pipes leaching lead and copper into water on the public and private side of water lines. A building’s supply lines and plumbing are not owned by the town. Periodic Testing The town performs periodic testing of customers’ water, primarily conducting tests in houses built during the years that lead-based solder was in use. Use of lead-based solder was a major reason the Environmental Protection Agency instituted the Lead and Copper Rule in 1991. The Lead and Copper Rule requires that the town check the amount of lead and copper in water after the water has passed through a customer’s service line and building plumbing. The town tests 30 samples every three years. The Environmental Protection Agency has reduced the testing required of the town due to its long history of compliance with the Lead and Copper Rule, which the agency has revised several times. The town has remained fully compliant since the rule was created. Pipe Materials Replacement Lead particles can attach to galvanized pipes, which were commonly used for water mains several decades ago. The town has a program to replace galvanized water mains on the public side of the water meter with newer standard materials when galvanized pipes are found. Precautions Customers Can Take If your home or building was built before 1987, you may have a lead service line. To determine your service line material, check inspection reports for your structure or v isit the Identify Your Service Line Material page for guidance on testing your line. See other precautions below. Internal Water Testing Homes and buildings may have internal plumbing materials that contain lead. Testing can determine whether lead is in your drinking water. The town offers free water testing to determine if lead is in your drinking water. For testing, utilities staff must be given access to the interior of your building. Before requesting a water test, please check the Water Service Line Map to see if the material of your property’s water service line has been identified. Testing performed by commercial laboratories generally costs between $20 and $100. For more information, see these resources from the state and Environmental Protection Agency: Certified Drinking Water Commercial Laboratories Home Water Testing Protect Your Tap: A Quick Check for Lead Tips to Limit Exposure Customers who suspect they have lead pipes or copper pipes with lead-based solder are encouraged to take the following additional precautions to limit exposure: Flush water lines after a period of stagnation. For guidance, see the Stale Water section on the About Water page. To minimize waste, consider using the released water to flush toilets or water plants. Purchase a filtering water pitcher certified to remove lead. Purchase a whole house filtering or treatment device certified to remove lead. Make sure it is properly installed and maintained. Note that a filter prevents the town from conducting bacteriological tests of a property’s water lines should a water quality issue occur. This is because the water will have undergone secondary treatment, and test results would not provide data on the water Hillsborough delivers. Regularly clean the screen or aerator on faucets. Sediment, debris and lead particles can collect. Contact Information Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. 919-296-9630 Office Hours — 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. Monday-Friday Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Contact Utilities Staff Title Phone Email Strandwitz, Marie Utilities Director 919-296-9631 Email"]

Name: 20_riverwalk_park_greenway_gold
Representative_Docs: ["Building in Flood-Prone Areas Undeveloped floodplains often serve the need for public parks and recreation. The Riverwalk greenway and Gold Park were built in their locations to provide recreation in areas that otherwise would not be developed. These recreation facilities require cleanup after a severe weather event to remove hazards. They are open for use many more days throughout the year than they are closed for weather and maintenance. Built to Withstand Flooding Riverwalk is located partially within Gold Park and was built along a sewer easement by the Eno River. Both recreation facilities were designed to withstand flooding. Gold Park’s dog park was built with a chain-link fence to allow floodwaters through. When flooding is expected, the fencing is left open to allow water carrying debris to flow through without damaging the fence. Severe Weather Parks and greenways are closed at times for the safety of the community and emergency workers. Hazards can exist even after a storm with high winds or snow has passed or after floodwaters have receded. Parks and greenways: Will remain closed following severe weather until the town can inspect the areas and remove any hazards. Are not a priority for snow and ice removal. They will be closed in deteriorating wintry conditions until staff determines they are safe. During Closures Heed any closure signs or barriers for parks and trails. Those who choose to use the town’s parks and greenways do so at their own risk. By entering closed areas and by moving or removing closure signs and barriers, you also: Slow cleanup efforts and delay the reopening of parks and greenways for everyone. Force public works and public safety staff away from other duties to reinstall closure signs and barriers. Force emergency workers away from other duties for potential rescues. Risk the safety of others. Subscribe to town news and follow @HillsboroughGov social media to stay informed\xa0about closures and reopenings. Shade at Parks Town staff and appointed boards are working to provide shade options at parks. Fast-growing shade trees have been planted around the perimeter of Cates Creek and Gold parks. Trees are planted in available spaces around the play areas but not inside due to spacing regulations for the play equipment. Because of the play equipment's current height and spacing regulations, sunshades in the play areas would be installed at a height that would provide little sun blockage during the day and that would make the shades a safety risk in high winds. Water Fountains Water is turned off at exterior drinking fountains at parks when the first freezing temperatures of winter are forecasted. Water service then is restored in the spring when the danger of freezing temperatures has passed. This helps avoid water line breaks, which can be costly to repair and disruptive to the use of recreational areas. Make a Recommendation The Tree Board makes planting decisions for town spaces. Plants typically are added between November and March to improve survival due to less heat and more precipitation. The Parks and Recreation Board advises the town on recreation improvements and implements recommendations in recreation master plans.", 'Building in Flood-Prone Areas Undeveloped floodplains often serve the need for public parks and recreation. Name: 21_incident_commander_shall_involved
Representative_Docs: ['Supervisory Review Anytime a reportable use of force occurs, the on-duty supervisor will conduct a review of the incident and forward all the documentation and their findings to the commander by the end of the supervisor’s duty day unless otherwise directed by that commander or a higher authority. The supervisor’s actions, conclusions and findings shall be documented in a memorandum to the supervisor’s commander. At minimum, the supervisor’s review shall include: The reviewer’s own observations and information collected at the incident scene including any statements from witnesses or others involved. A visual inspection of the suspect and officers for injuries. An interview with the involved officers. A review of all written documentation. A review of any audio or video recordings available. The supervisor shall immediately contact and notify the commander if the supervisor finds at any time during the incident or the review that there may be a policy violation, material inconsistency or excessive use of force. Another member of the command staff shall be notified if the commander is unavailable.', 'Supervisory Review Anytime a reportable use of force occurs, the on-duty supervisor will conduct a review of the incident and forward all the documentation and their findings to the commander by the end of the supervisor’s duty day unless otherwise directed by that commander or a higher authority. The supervisor’s actions, conclusions and findings shall be documented in a memorandum to the supervisor’s commander. At minimum, the supervisor’s review shall include: The reviewer’s own observations and information collected at the incident scene including any statements from witnesses or others involved. A visual inspection of the suspect and officers for injuries. An interview with the involved officers. A review of all written documentation. A review of any audio or video recordings available. The supervisor shall immediately contact and notify the commander if the supervisor finds at any time during the incident or the review that there may be a policy violation, material inconsistency or excessive use of force. Another member of the command staff shall be notified if the commander is unavailable. Command Review The division commander will conduct a review of the incident to ensure a complete and accurate picture of the incident has been established. The division commander will submit findings and all the documentation of the incident to the chief of police for final review. The division commander’s review shall include: Reviewing all written documentation. Collecting and reviewing any video or audio recordings of the event. Conducting additional and follow-up interviews as needed. Providing a review of the officer’s past performance to ensure no trends exist that would activate the Personnel Early Warning System. The commander’s actions, conclusions and findings shall be documented in a memorandum to the chief of police. The commander’s review must be completed and the entire use of force file submitted to the police chief: Within five working days from the date of the incident for any incident that does not involved a significant injury. Within 48 hours from the occurrence of the incident if it involves a significant injury. The police chief may grant additional time based on a written request from the division commander. Excessive Force/Policy Violations The chief of police shall be notified immediately any time a commander believes a use of force may be deemed excessive and/or outside of policy. Depending on the nature of the potential violation and/or the extent of any injuries to a citizen involved, the chief of police may direct transferring the investigation to internal affairs or an outside agency.', 'Anytime a reportable use of force occurs, the on-duty supervisor will conduct a review of the incident and forward all the documentation and their findings to the commander by the end of the supervisor’s duty day unless otherwise directed by that commander or a higher authority. The supervisor shall immediately contact and notify the commander if the supervisor finds at any time during the incident or the review that there may be a policy violation, material inconsistency or excessive use of force. Another member of the command staff shall be notified if the commander is unavailable.']

Representative_Docs: ['Preparation When weather conditions worsen, please stay off roads and town trails. Doing otherwise is risky and creates more emergency work. Resources Emergency Information See emergency information on the Ready Hillsborough page, including including key local contacts, how to report problems and how to subscribe to emergency notifications. Emergency Kits Basic readiness tips include having adequate supplies of food and water to last several days and putting together a disaster supply kit, including a flashlight, batteries, cash, first aid supplies and copies of\xa0critical documents. See the Ready Hillsborough page. Equipment and Vehicles Prepare your equipment and vehicles by ensuring they are fully fueled. Additionally, vehicles may be equipped with tire chains\xa0to improve traction and prevent slipping on snowy or icy roads. Stay Informed Please stay informed and share information with neighbors. Use the Town of Hillsborough’s various communication channels: Website\xa0— hillsboroughnc.gov News releases\xa0— Get News page to receive town news by email Facebook\xa0— @HillsboroughGov Instagram\xa0— @HillsboroughGov Nextdoor\xa0— www.nextdoor.com/pages/town-of-hillsborough-2 Bluesky — @hillsboroughnc.gov More Information Weather Conditions and Forecasts — National Weather Service Food Safety After Power Outages — U.S. Department of Health and Human Services Disaster Response — American Red Cross in English or Español Local Flooding Flooding frequently occurs on some high-traffic roads in town during heavy rains. The state owns and maintains several of these roads. Please heed closure signs and barriers. Town Roads Eno Street between: West Hill Avenue and Murray Street South Bellvue Street and Dimmocks Mill Road Faribault Lane East Queen Street between North Cameron and Mitchell streets East Tryon Street between North Cameron and North Churton streets North Wake Street between West Tryon and West Queen streets State Roads Churton Street at the intersection with: Daniel Boone Street Orange Grove Road West Tryon Street Orange Grove Road at the Exchange Club Lane intersection North Nash Street between Hayes and West Corbin streets', "Below is information on how the town prepares for severe storms and how you can too. Many people have a Hillsborough mailing address but do not live in town limits. See the Services for Your Property page. When it is safe to do so, crews from the town's public works division work to clear the roads of debris. Town Activities Preparations Hillsborough crews prepare equipment and resources before forecasted storms, including: Ensuring vehicles and generators are fully fueled. Ensuring any equipment that may be needed is ready to go. Clearing as many storm drains and cross pipes as possible. Operations Below is information on how the town operates during severe weather events. Curbside Collections Hillsborough will stop curbside collections of trash, brush, bulk items and loose leaves if weather conditions prevent collection trucks from operating safely. Garbage collection will be given priority when collections resume. Orange County provides collection of recyclables. See the Orange County website for changes in its services. Parks and Trails Before and during severe weather, staff will close parks and greenways as needed to keep the community and emergency workers safe. When flooding is expected, the dog park fencing at Gold Park will be placed in an open position. This prevents damage to the fencing by allowing water carrying debris to flow through the open gates. After severe weather, parks and greenways will remain closed until the town can inspect them and remove any hazards. Please heed all safety signage and barricades and do not enter closed areas.\xa0Those who choose to use the town’s parks and greenways do so at their own risk.\xa0Entering closed areas or moving signs and barriers also puts others at risk and pulls emergency workers away from other duties. Recreation in Floodplains Undeveloped floodplains often serve as public parks and recreation. The Riverwalk greenway and Gold Park were built in their locations to provide recreation in areas that otherwise would not be developed. Riverwalk was built along a sewer easement by the Eno River. It is partially located within Gold Park.\xa0Both recreation facilities were designed to withstand flooding. Community Preparation When weather conditions worsen, please stay off roads and town trails. Doing otherwise is risky and creates more emergency work. Resources Emergency Information See emergency information on the Ready Hillsborough page, including including key local contacts, how to report problems and how to subscribe to emergency notifications. Emergency Kits Basic readiness tips include having adequate supplies of food and water to last several days and putting together a disaster supply kit, including a flashlight, batteries, cash, first aid supplies and copies of\xa0critical documents. See the Ready Hillsborough page. Equipment and Vehicles Prepare your equipment and vehicles by ensuring they are fully fueled. Additionally, vehicles may be equipped with tire chains\xa0to improve traction and prevent slipping on snowy or icy roads. Stay Informed Please stay informed and share information with neighbors. Use the Town of Hillsborough’s various communication channels: Website\xa0— hillsboroughnc.gov News releases\xa0— Get News page to receive town news by email Facebook\xa0— @HillsboroughGov Instagram\xa0— @HillsboroughGov Nextdoor\xa0— www.nextdoor.com/pages/town-of-hillsborough-2 Bluesky — @hillsboroughnc.gov More Information Weather Conditions and Forecasts — National Weather Service Food Safety After Power Outages — U.S. Department of Health and Human Services Disaster Response — American Red Cross in English or Español Local Flooding Flooding frequently occurs on some high-traffic roads in town during heavy rains. The state owns and maintains several of these roads. Please heed closure signs and barriers. Town Roads Eno Street between: West Hill Avenue and Murray Street South Bellvue Street and Dimmocks Mill Road Faribault Lane East Queen Street between North Cameron and Mitchell streets East Tryon Street between North Cameron and North Churton streets North Wake Street between West Tryon and West Queen streets State Roads Churton Street at the intersection with: Daniel Boone Street Orange Grove Road West Tryon Street Orange Grove Road at the Exchange Club Lane intersection North Nash Street between Hayes and West Corbin streets", "Below is information on how the town prepares for winter weather and how you can too. Many people have a Hillsborough mailing address but do not live in town limits. See the Services for Your Property page. Town Activities Preparations In advance of predicted winter weather, Hillsborough crews prepare equipment and resources, including: Outfitting trucks with plows and tire chains. Ensuring vehicles and generators are fully fueled. Ensuring any equipment that may be needed is ready to go. Pretreating some roadways with brine if needed, including Rainey Avenue; Beckett’s Ridge, Lafayette, Lakeshore, Meadowlands and Waterstone drives; East Margaret Lane; College Park Road; and Cameron Street. Operations Snow and Ice Removal Visit the Street Maintenance page to view plowing guidelines and the town's responsibilities for winter weather. Curbside Collections Hillsborough will stop curbside collections of trash, brush, bulk items and loose leaves if weather conditions prevent collection trucks from operating safely. Garbage collection will be given priority when collections resume. Orange County provides collection of recyclables. See the Orange County website for changes in its services. Parks and Trails Parks and greenways are not a priority for snow removal. When weather conditions deteriorate, staff will close parks and greenways until they determine the areas are safe. Please heed all safety signage and barricades and do not enter closed areas. Those who choose to use the town’s parks and greenways do so at their own risk. Entering closed areas or moving signs and barriers also puts others at risk and pulls emergency workers away from other duties. Community Responsibilities Preparation When weather conditions worsen, please: Stay off roads, sidewalks and town trails. Do not park vehicles on streets because this affects plowing. Do not pass vehicles plowing snow or spreading salt or brine to avoid bodily injury or damage to vehicle paint. Remember that sidewalks and roads may continue to have snow and ice and to be hazardous after a storm, particularly in shaded areas. Traveling on roads, sidewalks and trails during severe weather is risky, creates more emergency work, and slows efforts to keep roadways cleared for emergency response. Resources Emergency Information See emergency information on the Ready Hillsborough page, including key local contacts, how to report problems and how to subscribe to emergency notifications. Emergency Kits Basic readiness tips include having adequate supplies of food and water to last several days and putting together a disaster supply kit, including a flashlight, batteries, cash, first aid supplies and copies of\xa0critical documents. See the Ready Hillsborough page. Equipment and Vehicles Prepare your equipment and vehicles by ensuring they are fully fueled. Additionally, vehicles may be equipped with tire chains\xa0to improve traction and prevent slipping on snowy or icy roads. Stay Informed Please stay informed and share information with neighbors. Use the Town of Hillsborough’s various communication channels: Website\xa0— hillsboroughnc.gov News releases\xa0— Get News page to receive town news by email Facebook\xa0— @HillsboroughGov Instagram\xa0— @HillsboroughGov Nextdoor\xa0— www.nextdoor.com/pages/town-of-hillsborough-2 Bluesky — @hillsboroughnc.gov Safety Tips Low temperatures can bring power outages due to high electrical demand. Stay safe with these tips: Heater Safety If heat is out, go to a neighbor or family member’s home if possible. Do not use generators, grills, camp stoves or other gasoline or charcoal-burning devices inside\xa0the home, basement or garage or near a window. These devices create carbon monoxide gas,\xa0which is poisonous. Check heating units and carbon monoxide detectors. Poorly operating or damaged heating units\xa0can release carbon monoxide gas. Caring for Others Don't leave children, elderly people or pets alone in vehicles. Temperatures in vehicles can drop rapidly.\xa0Pets can be greatly affected by the cold and should not be exposed long. Remember that older adults, people in fragile health and small children are affected more by\xa0the cold than the average adult. Check on loved ones and neighbors, preferably by telephone. Caring for Yourself Create a warm room by sealing off the warmest room in the home where everyone gathers. Close off additional rooms, cover doorways and windows, and block drafts with towels, blankets or clothes. Avoid unnecessarily opening doors and windows. Keep body heat in by layering loose but warm clothing, wearing a hat, using blankets, and huddling with people or pets. Eat and drink regularly to help stay warm. Avoid alcohol and caffeine because they cause the body to lose heat faster. Wear appropriate clothing that will adequately insulate from the cold when outdoors. The\xa0National Weather Service advises wearing a hat and gloves if outdoors during a wind chill\xa0advisory. Avoid\xa0overexertion and be aware of the amount and intensity of physical activity indoors and outdoors. Cold weather strains the body, especially the heart. Resources Personal Safety in Extreme Cold and Power Outages — Centers for Disease Control and Prevention Fire Safety Tips — National Fire Protection Association Food Safety After Power Outages — U.S. Department of Health and Human Services Disaster Response — American Red Cross in English or Español State Road Information — 511 Information Line Pipes and Cold Weather Frozen pipes can burst and cause substantial damage and water loss. This includes fire sprinkler systems. See the Backflow Prevention page for information on protecting backflow prevention devices in winter. Preventing Frozen and Burst Pipes Use these tips to protect water pipes during freezing temperatures. Unheated Areas Pipes in unheated areas have the greatest chance of freezing. Unheated areas include attics, basements and crawl spaces. Insulate pipes ― Pipe insulation is available in fiberglass or foam sleeves.\xa0Home centers and hardware stores have sleeves providing one-eighth to five-eighths inches of insulation. Specialty dealers have products that provide up to 2 inches of insulation. The extra thickness can save a pipe. Heat pipes ―\xa0Heating cables and tapes are effective. Select a heating cable with the UL label and a built-in thermostat that turns\xa0the heat on when needed. Follow the manufacturer’s instructions closely. If you have it, turn on an incandescent\xa0light bulb in your crawl space for extra warmth. Seal openings and air leaks ― This includes access doors, air vents in the foundation, and cracks.\xa0Especially keep cold wind away from pipes, which speeds the freezing process. Close and seal cracks and other openings with insulation, cardboard, plastic or newspaper. Foundation air vents may have hinged or sliding covers to easily close the vents. Ensure your water meter box cover is closed ― The cover prevents cold air from freezing water inside the meter. If the cover is broken or missing and you are a Hillsborough water customer, call the Town of Hillsborough at 919-732-3621. Heated Areas Leave cabinet doors open under kitchen and bathroom sinks ― This\xa0allows warmer room air to\xa0circulate around pipes. Let water drip slowly from the highest faucet in the building ― Use this tip if you have difficulty protecting pipes in an unheated area or if you believe your pipes may freeze for other reasons. An open faucet allows water to escape before pressure builds to a point where a pipe can burst. If dripping stops, ice may be blocking the pipe. Keep the\xa0faucet open because the pipe still needs pressure relief. Frozen and Burst Pipes If Your Pipes Are Frozen If nothing comes out of your faucets: Leave the faucets open and call a plumber. Try to thaw the pipe with warm air from a hair dryer ― Start by warming the pipe as close to the faucet as possible. Then work toward the coldest section of pipe. Do not use electrical appliances in areas of standing water. You could be electrocuted. Do not use a torch or other open flame. This could cause a fire hazard. If Your Pipes Have Burst Turn off water at the main shutoff valve and leave faucets open\xa0― Make sure everyone knows where the master valve is for your plumbing system and how to open and close it. The valve may be in your basement, crawl space or closet where water pipes enter the house or building. If you do not have a master shutoff valve,\xa0consider installing one. Drain the pipes in your plumbing system ― Open the highest and lowest cold-water faucets in your house until the water is depleted. Before draining pipes\xa0― Turn off the water heater to ensure the lack of water does not burn out the\xa0heating elements. After draining pipes ―\xa0Before turning on the water heater again, ensure water service is restored and the water tank is refilled. If you fix a leak, you may qualify for an adjustment to your utility bill. See the Billing and Adjustments page."]

Name: 23_tourism_occupancy_tax_room
Representative_Docs: ['The two Hillsborough appointed boards that oversee proceeds from taxes also reinvest the funds into tourism. Tourism Board — Reinvests funds from a 1% sales tax on prepared foods and beverages\xa0into: Hillsborough Visitors Center Grants, special projects and partnerships Contract services with local organizations and historic sites Tourism Development Authority — Reinvests funds from a 3% hotel and motel room occupancy tax\xa0to encourage visitors to stay in local lodging facilities.', 'About Tourism Funding The two Hillsborough appointed boards that oversee proceeds from taxes also reinvest the funds into tourism. Name: 24_leaves_bags_brush_collection
Representative_Docs: ['Brush The town collects woody material from trees and shrubs weekly as part of its brush collection. Most homes receive collection on their regular garbage collection days. Homes on the\xa0following streets receive collection on Wednesday: Lydia and East Margaret lanes Streets including Caine, North and South Cameron, East Corbin, Huddle, East King, Mitchell, East Orange, East Queen, East Tryon, and East Union. Requirements Keep trash and loose leaves out of brush. Ensure brush is no larger than 6 feet in length and 6 inches in diameter. Place brush within 4 feet of the curb, 3 feet away from obstructions and not below trees or power lines. Stack limbs in one pile, with all limbs in the same direction. Place small amounts of brush in biodegradable paper bags. Or tie small amounts into bundles weighing less than 30 pounds. Use jute or sisal twine for the bundles. Do not use plastic or cotton twine. Allow a bagful or several armfuls of sticks to accumulate before placing at the curb. Christmas trees can be collected with brush with restrictions. Name: 25_master_tree_park_trees
Representative_Docs: ['Park Master Plans The Parks and Recreation Board has approved an individual master plan for each town-maintained park. Cates Creek Park Master Plan Gold Park Master Plan Hillsborough Heights Park Master Plan Kings Highway Park Master Plan Murray Street Park Master Plan Riverwalk Master Plan Turnip Patch Park Master Plan', "The Tree Board is responsible for: The trees and landscaping in town parks and on town property and rights of way, including pollinator gardens. Contributing to requirements for tree plantings in private development. All meetings are open to the public. The Tree Board recognizes Arbor Day by planting trees on town property. Agendas and Minutes Bee City USA Treasure Trees Programs and Resources Invasive Plants Removal The invasive species removal team meets weekly to remove invasive plants from Hillsborough’s public spaces as part of a program of the Hillsborough Tree Board. The team gathers on Wednesdays for two hours in the afternoon in the cooler months and in the morning during warmer months. If it’s removal season, they bring loppers and shears to remove plants. If it’s planting season, they bring shovels and trowels. Trees Are Good The International Society of Arboriculture manages the educational website Trees Are Good, which offers quality tree care information for homeowners and other tree owners. The site helps increase awareness of the benefits of trees and provides resources to help sustain trees in an urban environment. Check out the directory to find an arborist and see the Tree Owner Resources for information, including why to hire an arborist and why topping hurts trees. Tree City USA The town has been a part of the Arbor Day Foundation's Tree City USA program since 2003. To qualify for the program, the town is required to have: A tree board or department. A tree care ordinance. A community forestry program with an annual budget of at least $2 per resident. An annual Arbor Day observance and proclamation. Name: 26_project_construction_funding_transportation
Representative_Docs: ['Funding Funding The multimodal corridor study costs $200,000. Name: 28_project_86_sewer_facility
Representative_Docs: ["Improvements are planned to the sewer system within the Lawndale Basin in northern Hillsborough. The project will involve: Cleaning and inspecting sewers. Performing small dig and repair projects on the main sewer line and on select sewer services\xa0within the right of way. Moving or installing some sewer cleanouts. Lining sewer pipes to prevent groundwater and rainwater from entering the sewer system. The town maintains a part of the sewer service line — from within the road right of way to the sewer main. A sewer cleanout is typically on the line between the property's edge and the public right of way. Site Project area The Lawndale Basin is in northern Hillsborough. The project area includes: An easement between Rainey Avenue and Torain Street. Parts of Lawndale and Rainey avenues and parts of Cornelius, Hill and Torain streets. Funding Funding The project is estimated to cost about $1.5 million. The town will receive a low-interest loan of about $1.1 million from the Clean Water State Revolving Fund, which includes loan forgiveness for up to $500,000 of the funding. The remaining part of the project will be paid for from reserve funds and should have no impact on future customer rates. Current Phase The rehabilitation and replacement project is expected to begin in November 2025. Project Details About the Project Orange County installed the sewers in this area under a grant program in the 1980s before the town assumed ownership. The sewers now need repair or replacement. A hired contractor studied the condition of the sewers and the sewer service connections from homes in the Lawndale Basin. The study included monitoring flow in the sewers during different times of day and storm events. Part of the project will involve installing a sewer cleanout pipe where one may be missing.\xa0The pipe allows access to the sewer line to remove blockages. It helps specify between public and private ownership. The town maintains the public portion of sewer lines. Property owners are responsible for the section of sewer service lines that connects to their structure from the right of way or easement. Lining a Sewer Roots and soil can enter a pipe and clog it. Cracks in the pipe also can let sewage out. Water that enters sewer pipes must be treated by the town. The blue coloring shows a relined pipe. When a sewer is relined, the service connection from a home or building is blocked temporarily. The relining process reduces disturbance to roads and yards because the work is performed by machines underground. To line a sewer: A flexible resin inverted “sock” is inserted into the sewer main through manholes. The sock is cured or hardened to the pipe's shape. Crews use hot steam or water or sometimes ultraviolet light for the curing process. A machine cuts openings in the relined pipe for any sewer services connected to the sewer main. Project Contact Julie Taylor Taylor Engineering & Consulting 336-339-6883 Contact Taylor Engineering What to Expect Sewer Service Issues When workers reline the sewer pipes: The sewer service connection from your home to the sewer line will be blocked. Pressure in the sewer line can force water out of toilets and drains. The contractor will communicate directly with residents when service disruption will occur. Service connections will be reopened at the end of the noted workday. Avoid a Sewer Backup On the day your service is disrupted: Do not do laundry, dishwashing, bathing or other activities that use large amounts of water. Keep toilets flushed after use. Place a towel on the rim of your toilet bowl and keep the seat and lid closed. Cover any basement floor drains. Odor The curing process for lining a sewer may produce an odor, like glue or a permanent marker. The odor may enter your home through the pipe that connects your home to the public sewer main. The odor is caused by styrene, a naturally occurring chemical, used in the resin of the liners. The airborne concentration levels are minimal and do not pose a health risk. During short-term exposure, the chemical can cause eye irritation, headache and discomfort. The odor will disappear once the pipe lining is fully hardened. Avoid Potential Odor To Avoid the Odor — Run water into sinks, bathtubs and drains to fill the P-trap fitting on pipes, creating a water barrier. About a gallon of water will fill the U-shaped portion of the pipe, which keeps sewer gas and other vapors out of your home or building. If Odor Is Inside — Open windows or use fans to refresh and circulate your home’s air. If Odor Remains — If the odor remains despite the steps above, contact a plumber about fixing a potential open air connection to the sewer. That problem is a health risk from sewer gas entering your home. Additional Items Timeline and Hours — The project is expected to start in November 2025. Once the project moves forward, it is expected to take about six months to complete. Construction activities will be 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. Monday-Friday unless approved in writing by the town. Construction — Noise and traffic disruptions will occur throughout the project. Contractors may need to dig in the road or rights of way to make repairs. They will use manholes or sewer cleanouts in the right of way to reline sewers. One lane of traffic may be closed. If repairs are needed on private property, the contractor will coordinate with the resident. Markings — Workers will place paint markings or flags on the ground to identify underground utilities where digging may occur. Paint markings wash off with time. Please do not disrupt the project markings. Equipment — If equipment or materials are stored on private property, the contractor should have a written contract with the property owner. Post-construction — The contractor will restore areas disturbed by the project. Contact Information Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. 919-296-9631 Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Contact Utilities Staff Title Phone Email Strandwitz, Marie Utilities Director 919-296-9631 Email", "Location and Background The booster pumping station is to be built off Old N.C. 86 near Starfield Circle, south of Hillsborough. The station will improve a connection that already exists between the\xa0town's water supply and Orange Water and Sewer Authority's supply on the south side of Hillsborough. The improved connection is expected to meet the water demands of either utility during a drought or an emergency. The station would benefit the entire town and specifically help serve the UNC Hospitals Hillsborough Campus and Orange County/Hillsborough Economic Development District on the town's south side in an emergency due to the planned location near receiving pipes. Funding The estimated cost of the project is\xa0$2.2 million. Grant Funding The town received a\xa0Building Resilient Infrastructure and Communities grant of $1.01 million from the Federal Emergency Management Agency in August 2023. As a part of the grant, the town would pay for 30% of the project. In April 2025, the federal program was ended and all applications from fiscal years 2020-2023 were canceled. This canceled $831,418 in federal funds for the building of the booster pumping station. North Carolina and 19 other states sued FEMA in July 2025 over its decision to cancel the BRIC program. In December 2025, a U.S. district court judge ruled the program was unlawfully ended. On March 18, 2026, FEMA issued an advisory notifying states that the agency plans to resume programming support. As of June 2026, the BRIC program has been reestablished, and the town has received reimbursements from FEMA for expended design fees. The state plans to transfer the project to its hazard mitigation grant program. Moving the project to the state's program would give the town more time to complete the project and include increased costs. Current Phase The project is  in the design phase. Additionally, permitting and easement acquisition are not complete.", "The town is working to add a booster pump to improve the interconnection between the town's water supply and the Orange Water and Sewer Authority's supply in southern Orange County. Location and Background The booster pumping station is to be built off Old N.C. 86 near Starfield Circle, south of Hillsborough. The station will improve a connection that already exists between the\xa0town's water supply and Orange Water and Sewer Authority's supply on the south side of Hillsborough. The improved connection is expected to meet the water demands of either utility during a drought or an emergency. The station would benefit the entire town and specifically help serve the UNC Hospitals Hillsborough Campus and Orange County/Hillsborough Economic Development District on the town's south side in an emergency due to the planned location near receiving pipes. Funding The estimated cost of the project is\xa0$2.2 million. Grant Funding The town received a\xa0Building Resilient Infrastructure and Communities grant of $1.01 million from the Federal Emergency Management Agency in August 2023. As a part of the grant, the town would pay for 30% of the project. In April 2025, the federal program was ended and all applications from fiscal years 2020-2023 were canceled. This canceled $831,418 in federal funds for the building of the booster pumping station. North Carolina and 19 other states sued FEMA in July 2025 over its decision to cancel the BRIC program. In December 2025, a U.S. district court judge ruled the program was unlawfully ended. On March 18, 2026, FEMA issued an advisory notifying states that the agency plans to resume programming support. As of June 2026, the BRIC program has been reestablished, and the town has received reimbursements from FEMA for expended design fees. The state plans to transfer the project to its hazard mitigation grant program. Moving the project to the state's program would give the town more time to complete the project and include increased costs. Current Phase The project is  in the design phase. Additionally, permitting and easement acquisition are not complete. Staff Title Phone Email Strandwitz, Marie Utilities Director 919-296-9631 Email"]

Name: 29_drivers_collisions_campaigns_bikes
Representative_Docs: ['The town provides annual driver training to its officers. In addition, the town manager and police chief help provide driver training to other police departments in the state through a course modeled after the Hillsborough "Slower Is Faster" program and sponsored by the\xa0N.C. League of Municipalities Risk Management Services Division. Claims from police vehicle collisions are one of the largest liabilities and costs that the league\'s insurance pool must pay for each year. Vehicle-related accidents also are the leading cause of police line-of-duty deaths and cause many deaths within communities. Tactics to Reduce Collisions Town Manager Eric Peterson competes in motorsports and coordinates the annual training in Hillsborough, which emphasizes key tactics to perform while driving to reduce collisions: Looking ahead and scanning surroundings to avoid surprises. Braking early. Slowing down to leave a margin for error for the unexpected. Using self-talk to better perform under pressure. Breathing to remain calm. The tactics help officers to keep their vehicles and cognitive functions under control. Adrenaline can impair driving by causing tunnel vision, loss of hearing, slowed reaction time, decreased motor skills, and a slowing of forebrain functions and cognitive processing. Training includes classroom instruction and practice behind the wheel that is focused on improving decision-making through various scenarios, including the sudden appearance of objects in the roadway. An instructor rides along to provide coaching.', 'Slower Is Faster Driver Training The town provides annual driver training to its officers. Training includes classroom instruction and practice behind the wheel that is focused on improving decision-making through various scenarios, including the sudden appearance of objects in the roadway. An instructor rides along to provide coaching.', 'Alcohol Law Enforcement The department has an alcohol law enforcement response team. The team c hecks for compliance throughout the year with: Operations that\xa0target sales to underage buyers at local businesses, including bars, restaurants and stores . Inspections of Alcohol Beverage Control permits at local businesses. Patrols dedicated to looking for impaired drivers. Traffic Enforcement Officers focus primarily on educating drivers. They place emphasize moving violations over nonmoving violations, such as those related to vehicle equipment or registration. Submit a Traffic Concern Safety Campaigns The Police Department participates in campaigns directed by the\xa0North Carolina Governor Highway Safety Program to reduce collisions, including campaigns on: Drinking and driving Seatbelts Speeding Speeding The department has digital signs for placement by roads to show drivers their current speed. One sign is moved around the town regularly.']
