Name: 31_waste_composting_recycling_food
Representative_Docs: ['Composting food waste reduces disposal fees the town must pay and benefits the environment. Drop-off Sites Food waste can be dropped off for free at 11 sites in Orange County, including: Eno River Farmers Market in downtown Hillsborough. Orange County 24-hour recycling drop-off site in Hampton Pointe. Orange County waste and recycling center on Walnut Grove Church Road, north of Hillsborough. The North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality adds public and private composting sites to the NC Food Donation and Compost Resource Map as they become available. Acceptable Items Items that can be dropped off for composting at these sites includes: Food scraps. Food-related paper, including napkins, greasy pizza boxes and staple-free tea bags. Flowers and trimmings from small indoor plants. For more information on the drop-off sites and a comprehensive list of acceptable items, see the Orange County food waste page. Composting at Home Composting can be done at home and can reduce fertilizer, herbicide and water needs for plants. Learn how to get started on the Orange County Cooperative Extension website . For those without the space, conditions or desire for backyard composting, countertop composters can be purchased to provide a soil additive with less effort. Countertop composters may use a combination of grinders and heat to reduce food volume and create compost.', 'Food Waste Composting food waste reduces disposal fees the town must pay and benefits the environment. Drop-off Sites Food waste can be dropped off for free at 11 sites in Orange County, including: Eno River Farmers Market in downtown Hillsborough. Orange County 24-hour recycling drop-off site in Hampton Pointe. Orange County waste and recycling center on Walnut Grove Church Road, north of Hillsborough. The North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality adds public and private composting sites to the NC Food Donation and Compost Resource Map as they become available. Acceptable Items Items that can be dropped off for composting at these sites includes: Food scraps. Food-related paper, including napkins, greasy pizza boxes and staple-free tea bags. Flowers and trimmings from small indoor plants. For more information on the drop-off sites and a comprehensive list of acceptable items, see the Orange County food waste page. Composting at Home Composting can be done at home and can reduce fertilizer, herbicide and water needs for plants. Learn how to get started on the Orange County Cooperative Extension website . For those without the space, conditions or desire for backyard composting, countertop composters can be purchased to provide a soil additive with less effort. Countertop composters may use a combination of grinders and heat to reduce food volume and create compost.', "Commercial Waste Independent vendors provide trash collection to multi-family housing communities, businesses, churches and nonprofits. Businesses may use any vendor they choose if town standards are met. See the regulations and an application for a dumpster exemption . Food Waste Composting food waste reduces disposal fees the town must pay and benefits the environment. Drop-off Sites Food waste can be dropped off for free at 11 sites in Orange County, including: Eno River Farmers Market in downtown Hillsborough. Orange County 24-hour recycling drop-off site in Hampton Pointe. Orange County waste and recycling center on Walnut Grove Church Road, north of Hillsborough. The North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality adds public and private composting sites to the NC Food Donation and Compost Resource Map as they become available. Acceptable Items Items that can be dropped off for composting at these sites includes: Food scraps. Food-related paper, including napkins, greasy pizza boxes and staple-free tea bags. Flowers and trimmings from small indoor plants. For more information on the drop-off sites and a comprehensive list of acceptable items, see the Orange County food waste page. Composting at Home Composting can be done at home and can reduce fertilizer, herbicide and water needs for plants. Learn how to get started on the Orange County Cooperative Extension website . For those without the space, conditions or desire for backyard composting, countertop composters can be purchased to provide a soil additive with less effort. Countertop composters may use a combination of grinders and heat to reduce food volume and create compost. Fees and Violations Fees can be paid several ways.\xa0Contact the administrative support specialist for more information. Cash or check at the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. Credit or debit card through the town's payments and donations website . Violations If requirements for collection are not followed, the garbage or bulk items will not be collected. An employee will leave information on the town's policy for the resident. If trash carts and uncollected garbage are not removed from the street as required, a notice of violation will be delivered to the resident by mail or by town staff.\xa0A fee of $25 per day will be assessed if the resident does not comply with the ordinance within five days of the notice date. Recycling and Other Solid Waste Services Orange County collects recycling at curbs on Wednesdays in Hillsborough. See the county's Urban and Town Curbside (Weekly Collection) page for requirements. The county also provides other trash services to Hillsborough residents.\xa0Contact Orange County Solid Waste Management for these services, including: Recycling drop-off sites. Household hazardous waste collection. Construction and demolition waste collection. Solid waste convenience centers. Rollout Carts For collection and safety, carts must be: Placed at the curb between 4 p.m. the day before collection and 6:30 a.m. the day of collection. Placed within 3 feet of the curb, with the handle facing away from the street. Placed at least 4 feet from parked cars, trees or other obstructions. Removed from the street by 6 a.m. the day after collection. See our video Position Your Solid Waste and Recycling Carts Properly . Rollout Cart Delivery and Repair Residents in single-family homes with active utility accounts may receive one free trash rollout cart after account activation if they live in town limits on a publicly owned road. See the Services for Your Property page to determine whether a property is in town limits. A fee is charged for a second cart. Payment must be received before delivery is scheduled. See the fees section for how to pay the fee. Place a work order or call the administrative support specialist to request: Cart delivery or repair Second cart"]

Name: 32_station_future_train_site
Representative_Docs: ["Town Projects Future Train Station —\xa0The town is working with the state to reestablish passenger rail service and provide for economic development in Hillsborough. It will use 20 acres that the town owns by the North Carolina Railroad corridor. See the Future Train Station project page. Lawndale Basin Sewer Rehabilitation —\xa0Sewer lines will be repaired and relined in the Lawndale Basin in northern Hillsborough. See the Lawndale Basin Sewer Rehabilitation project page. Parking Study —\xa0The town is comprehensively studying its parking supply and the demand for parking in the downtown area and West Hillsborough to address existing conditions and future needs. See the Parking Study page. Highway 86 North Facility — The facility will be renovated and storage outbuildings will be constructed to allow use by multiple town operations. See the Highway 86 North Facility project page. Ridgewalk Greenway —\xa0A feasibility study has been conducted on a future north-south greenway that would provide a bike/pedestrian route from the future train station to the downtown. See the Ridgewalk Greenway project page. River Pumping Station Relocation — The town is working to replace the 1970s-era River Pumping Station, moving it from the Eno River floodplain and increasing sewer capacity in the area. See the River Pumping Station Relocation project page. South Churton Street Multimodal Corridor Study —\xa0South Churton Street is being studied to develop design concepts that contribute to the town's character and meet the goals of a state-funded project to widen the road from Interstate 40 to the Eno River. See the South Churton Street Multimodal Corridor Study project page. Starfield Booster Pumping Station —\xa0The town is working to add a booster pump to improve the interconnection between the town's water supply and the Orange Water and Sewer Authority's supply in southern Orange County. See the Starfield Booster Pumping Station project page. Unified Development Ordinance Rewrite —\xa0The town is updating its unified development ordinance to ensure it aligns with the Hillsborough Comprehensive Sustainability Plan. See the Unified Development Ordinance Rewrite project page. Water and Sewer Facility Renovation and Expansion —\xa0The Adron F. Thompson Water/Sewer Facility will be renovated and expanded. See the Water and Sewer Facility Renovation and Expansion project page.", "About the Project A train station building and passenger platform will be built. The building will be about 7,000 square feet and will provide space for offices and passenger service areas. The station primarily will be used for town office and meeting space until future expansion and construction of the station’s platform. The project included the development of three concept designs. One was elected to be carried through to the construction-document level design and engineering estimates. Station Site Station Site The town bought 20 acres next to the North Carolina Railroad corridor in 2008. It has a conceptual plan for a portion of the site. The plan will inform environmental screening and preliminary planning for the future station. Future Train Station Conceptual Site Layout — June 2016 Future Train Station Presentation Packet — November 2022 Future Train Station Site Map Funding Funding The future train station was funded in the 2016-2025 State Transportation Improvement Program. In 2020, the town entered into a funding agreement with the N.C. Department of Transportation and the public transportation agency GoTriangle to facilitate the design and construction of the station building and site. Future Train Station Funding Agreement — March 2020 Current Phase Current Phase The project's current phase includes planning, design and construction document-level engineering of: The train station building and a covered platform. Parking and site circulation. Connectivity to the site and o ther related and necessary improvements. A request for proposals for design of the future station was issued in September 2021. In December 2021, the Hillsborough Board of Commissioners authorized staff to negotiate a contract up to $800,000 with Clearscapes for design and engineering of the future train station and a greenway connection to downtown Hillsborough. Clearscapes gave a presentation to the Board of Commissioners in November 2022. The site plan and architecturals are still in development and subject to change. Videos Future Train Station Presentation Transportation Priorities Presentation", "The town is working with the state, North Carolina Railroad and Amtrak to reestablish passenger rail service and provide for economic development in Hillsborough. A train station will be built on 20 acres the town owns next to the North Carolina Railroad corridor. The train station will be located south of downtown Hillsborough. About the Project A train station building and passenger platform will be built. The building will be about 7,000 square feet and will provide space for offices and passenger service areas. The station primarily will be used for town office and meeting space until future expansion and construction of the station’s platform. The project included the development of three concept designs. One was elected to be carried through to the construction-document level design and engineering estimates. The plan will inform environmental screening and preliminary planning for the future station. In 2020, the town entered into a funding agreement with the N.C. Department of Transportation and the public transportation agency GoTriangle to facilitate the design and construction of the station building and site. Future Train Station Funding Agreement — March 2020 Current Phase Current Phase The project's current phase includes planning, design and construction document-level engineering of: The train station building and a covered platform. Parking and site circulation. Connectivity to the site and o ther related and necessary improvements. A request for proposals for design of the future station was issued in September 2021. In December 2021, the Hillsborough Board of Commissioners authorized staff to negotiate a contract up to $800,000 with Clearscapes for design and engineering of the future train station and a greenway connection to downtown Hillsborough. Name: 34_line_material_lead_service
Representative_Docs: ['The Hillsborough Utilities Department has developed an inventory of water service line materials in the community to help identify any lead service lines. The Environmental Protection Agency required the initial inventory by Oct. 16, 2024, under revised requirements to the Lead and Copper Rule. Updates must be made each year, and unknown materials must be identified within 10 years. Community Help For some properties, town staff continue to need community members’ help in identifying or correcting the materials of their private water service lines. Private lines are those between the building and water meter. In this inventory, the service line is under 2 inches in diameter. Please check the town’s Water Service Line Map to see if your building’s service line is marked as unknown. If the town does not have information about the customer side of your water service line, please determine the material by checking inspection reports for your home or building or by performing simple tests on the Identify Your Service Line Material page. Once you have identified your pipe material, complete the Water Service Line Material Form . Attach documentation of your service line material, such as a photo, and any repairs made to the service line after 1986 for homes or buildings built before 1987.', "News Town Continues Inventory of Materials in Water Service Lines The town may need you to provide information. January 02, 2026 8:00 a.m. More News Lead is not typically found in raw water sources, such as lakes, but it can enter drinking water from corrosion of plumbing materials that contain lead. The town adds the corrosion prohibitor orthophosphate into drinking water to prevent corrosion in plumbing materials. Identify Your Service Line Material Water Service Line Material Form Water Service Line Map About Lead in Water Across the country, service lines commonly were made from lead pipe from the late 1800s, throughout much of the 20th century, and in some cities as late as the mid-1980s. After June 1986, installation of new lead service lines was prohibited. Regulations Safe Drinking Water Act In 1974, the U.S. Congress passed the Safe Drinking Water Act, which requires the Environmental Protection Agency to determine the safe levels of contaminants in drinking water.\xa0The safe level of lead in drinking water is zero. Congress later amended the act\xa0— effective after June 1986\xa0— to prohibit using pipes, solder or flux that contain lead in public water systems or in plumbing in facilities that provide water for human consumption. Federal Lead and Copper Rule In 1991, the Environmental Protection Agency\xa0published the lead and copper rule to minimize these heavy metals in drinking water.\xa0The rule established a maximum contaminant level goal of zero for lead in drinking water and a treatment technique to reduce corrosion of lead and copper pipe materials within water distribution systems. Rule Revisions In 2021, the Environmental Protection Agency revised the Lead and Copper Rule to improve protection of public health. The revisions\xa0focus on: Identifying and removing lead service lines. Providing increased levels of protection from lead exposure in drinking water to children at schools and childcare facilities. Providing information about lead in drinking water to the community. The revised rules required water systems to develop a publicly available inventory of public and private water service lines and their makeup by Oct. 16, 2024. The inventory must be updated annually. Any unknown materials must be identified within 10 years of the initial inventory compliance date. Resources For more information, see these resources from the Environmental Protection Agency: Safe Drinking Water Act page Lead and Copper Rule page Infographic\xa0— Improving Lead Sampling to Better Protect Public Health News Release\xa0— EPA Finalizes Historic Action to Better Protect Children's Health Harmful Effects Harmful Effects Lead in drinking water has the following negative effects, according to the Environmental Protection Agency: Children Even low levels of lead in the blood of children can result in: Behavior and learning problems Lower IQ and hyperactivity Slowed growth Hearing problems Anemia In rare cases, ingestion of lead can cause seizures, coma and death. Pregnant Women Lead can accumulate in our bodies over time, where it is stored in bones along with calcium. During pregnancy, lead is released from bones as maternal calcium and is used to help form the bones of the fetus. This is particularly true if a woman does not have enough dietary calcium. Lead also can cross the placental barrier exposing the fetus to lead. This can result in serious effects to the mother and her developing fetus, including: Reduced growth of the fetus Premature birth All Adults Adults exposed to lead can suffer from: Cardiovascular effects, increased blood pressure, and hypertension Decreased kidney function Reproductive problems (in both men and women) Resources For more information, see these resources from the Environmental Protection Agency: Learn About Lead webpage Inventory in Hillsborough Lead in Hillsborough Town records dating to the mid-1930s call for copper or plastic water service lines and not lead. Lead service lines have not come up in staff discussions with local plumbers familiar with the area and have not been found by staff when working throughout the system. Homes and buildings built before 1987 may have lead service lines. Inventory The Hillsborough Utilities Department has developed an inventory of water service line materials in the community to help identify any lead service lines. The Environmental Protection Agency required the initial inventory by Oct. 16, 2024, under revised requirements to the Lead and Copper Rule. Updates must be made each year, and unknown materials must be identified within 10 years. Community Help For some properties, town staff continue to need community members’ help in identifying or correcting the materials of their private water service lines. Private lines are those between the building and water meter. In this inventory, the service line is under 2 inches in diameter. Please check the town’s Water Service Line Map to see if your building’s service line is marked as unknown. If the town does not have information about the customer side of your water service line, please determine the material by checking inspection reports for your home or building or by performing simple tests on the Identify Your Service Line Material page. Once you have identified your pipe material, complete the Water Service Line Material Form . Attach documentation of your service line material, such as a photo, and any repairs made to the service line after 1986 for homes or buildings built before 1987. Water Service Line Map Instructions Use the Water Service Line Map to see if\xa0the service line material at your property has been identified. Enter your address in the search field at the top of the map. Your property will become highlighted on the map. A circle at the property will display materials for both sides of the service line\xa0— utility and customer sides. See the map’s legend\xa0for the color coding of your circle. The circle’s left side represents\xa0the water service line owned by the town. The circle’s right side represents the water service line owned by the customer. If the material is unknown or incorrect, the town needs your help in identifying the material or reporting the correct material. A circle with\xa0gray on the right side means the town does not have information on the water service line for that property. Precautions Precautions the Town Takes Corrosion Inhibitor The town adds a blended phosphate corrosion inhibitor to its water. This additive greatly reduces the risk of pipes leaching lead and copper into water on the public and private side of water lines. A building’s supply lines and plumbing are not owned by the town. Periodic Testing The town performs periodic testing of customers’ water, primarily conducting tests in houses built during the years that lead-based solder was in use. Use of lead-based solder was a major reason the Environmental Protection Agency instituted the Lead and Copper Rule in 1991. The Lead and Copper Rule requires that the town check the amount of lead and copper in water after the water has passed through a customer’s service line and building plumbing. The town tests 30 samples every three years. The Environmental Protection Agency has reduced the testing required of the town due to its long history of compliance with the Lead and Copper Rule, which the agency has revised several times. The town has remained fully compliant since the rule was created. Pipe Materials Replacement Lead particles can attach to galvanized pipes, which were commonly used for water mains several decades ago. The town has a program to replace galvanized water mains on the public side of the water meter with newer standard materials when galvanized pipes are found. Precautions Customers Can Take If your home or building was built before 1987, you may have a lead service line. To determine your service line material, check inspection reports for your structure or v isit the Identify Your Service Line Material page for guidance on testing your line. See other precautions below. Internal Water Testing Homes and buildings may have internal plumbing materials that contain lead. Testing can determine whether lead is in your drinking water. The town offers free water testing to determine if lead is in your drinking water. For testing, utilities staff must be given access to the interior of your building. Before requesting a water test, please check the Water Service Line Map to see if the material of your property’s water service line has been identified. Testing performed by commercial laboratories generally costs between $20 and $100. For more information, see these resources from the state and Environmental Protection Agency: Certified Drinking Water Commercial Laboratories Home Water Testing Protect Your Tap: A Quick Check for Lead Tips to Limit Exposure Customers who suspect they have lead pipes or copper pipes with lead-based solder are encouraged to take the following additional precautions to limit exposure: Flush water lines after a period of stagnation. For guidance, see the Stale Water section on the About Water page. To minimize waste, consider using the released water to flush toilets or water plants. Purchase a filtering water pitcher certified to remove lead. Purchase a whole house filtering or treatment device certified to remove lead. Make sure it is properly installed and maintained. Note that a filter prevents the town from conducting bacteriological tests of a property’s water lines should a water quality issue occur. This is because the water will have undergone secondary treatment, and test results would not provide data on the water Hillsborough delivers. Regularly clean the screen or aerator on faucets. Sediment, debris and lead particles can collect. Contact Information Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. 919-296-9630 Office Hours — 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. Monday-Friday Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Contact Utilities Staff Title Phone Email Strandwitz, Marie Utilities Director 919-296-9631 Email", 'Lead in Hillsborough Town records dating to the mid-1930s call for copper or plastic water service lines and not lead. Once you have identified your pipe material, complete the Water Service Line Material Form . Attach documentation of your service line material, such as a photo, and any repairs made to the service line after 1986 for homes or buildings built before 1987. Name: 36_vehicle_operating_employees_wireless
Representative_Docs: ['General Guidelines Even when not strictly prohibited below, employees are strongly encouraged to bring vehicles they are operating to a complete stop if they need to use electronic wireless communication devices or engage in any activity that may distract their driving. Employees may use electronic wireless communication devices any time a vehicle they are operating is stopped or when they are a passenger in a vehicle. Supervisors and employees shall refrain from communicating any critical information by text message or email to any employee who may be operating a vehicle. Police radios shall be used for communicating critical information. Critical information is information that is expected to be read or responded to in less than 30 minutes. Employees will not be penalized for reasonable delays in reading and/or responding to a text or email or in answering or returning phone calls they receive while operating a vehicle.', 'Even when not strictly prohibited below, employees are strongly encouraged to bring vehicles they are operating to a complete stop if they need to use electronic wireless communication devices or engage in any activity that may distract their driving. Employees may use electronic wireless communication devices any time a vehicle they are operating is stopped or when they are a passenger in a vehicle. Supervisors and employees shall refrain from communicating any critical information by text message or email to any employee who may be operating a vehicle. Police radios shall be used for communicating critical information. Critical information is information that is expected to be read or responded to in less than 30 minutes. Employees will not be penalized for reasonable delays in reading and/or responding to a text or email or in answering or returning phone calls they receive while operating a vehicle.', 'This policy establishes practices that reduce or eliminate distractions while Police Department employees are operating town-owned vehicles which are in motion. The department recognizes the inherent danger that exists when employees operate vehicles while distracted. The use of cellular phones, computers or other electronic devices while operating a vehicle can: Cause unnecessary distractions. Present a negative image to the public. Interfere with the safe operation of a vehicle. Impede response to calls for service. Comment on Policy A. Definitions Distracted Driving This is any activity that could divert a person’s attention from the primary task of driving. Employees who use a cell phone while driving expose themselves and others to a significant safety risk. Studies on cellular phones related vehicular accidents reveal that those individuals using cell phones while driving were significantly more likely to have an accident than those not using cell phones. Electronic Wireless Communication Devices These are any devices that can be used to send or receive information and/or communicate wirelessly. This includes cellular phones, smart phones, laptops, global positioning system (GPS) devices or tablets, regardless of whether they are provided by the town or personally owned. B. General Guidelines General Guidelines Even when not strictly prohibited below, employees are strongly encouraged to bring vehicles they are operating to a complete stop if they need to use electronic wireless communication devices or engage in any activity that may distract their driving. Employees may use electronic wireless communication devices any time a vehicle they are operating is stopped or when they are a passenger in a vehicle. Supervisors and employees shall refrain from communicating any critical information by text message or email to any employee who may be operating a vehicle. Police radios shall be used for communicating critical information. Critical information is information that is expected to be read or responded to in less than 30 minutes. Employees will not be penalized for reasonable delays in reading and/or responding to a text or email or in answering or returning phone calls they receive while operating a vehicle. C. Prohibitions While Operating a Vehicle Prohibitions While Operating a Vehicle While operating a vehicle in motion (including a town vehicle whether on or off duty and a town or personally owned vehicle for town business), employees shall not: Use a cellular phone in any mode other than hands-free nor for any purpose that is not strictly work related. View, search or otherwise try to manage body-worn or in-car videos. Eat, read printed material, perform personal grooming, smoke or engage in any other activity that significantly limits the use of one or both hands and is not directly work related and/or cannot be avoided until their vehicle comes to a complete stop. Engage in writing, sending, searching, replying, using an application (app) or reading any text-based communication on electronic wireless communication devices. Employees shall not alter, change or disengage any device designed to reduce distractions that is installed in a town vehicle. D. Exceptions Exceptions The following are exempt from these provisions: Operation and use of portable and vehicle-mounted radios and radio detection and ranging (RADAR) units. The activation and deactivation of in-car and body worn video cameras. Additionally: Employees may use a mobile data computer while in motion when one-button or one-touch responses are needed to activate an emergency button or to indicate an officer is en route or arriving on scene. Using a wireless communication device in a manner inconsistent with the prohibitions in Section C (Prohibitions While Operating a Vehicle) is permitted only when emergency or significant circumstances with a justification for immediacy exist and no reasonable alternative is available. Employees may not use any electronic wireless communication devices (other than a police radio and activation of in-car or body-worn cameras) when operating a vehicle that has lights and/or siren activated or when operating outside the normally expected rules of the road.']

Name: 37_police_department_community_enforcement
Representative_Docs: ['Mission The Hillsborough Police Department exists to make the Hillsborough vision a reality by providing exceptional service, protecting and enhancing our community, helping those in need, and exemplifying policing at its best through the most positive and professional law enforcement services possible. Vision The Hillsborough Police Department will be an example of the very best law enforcement service possible and will always strive to exceed our community’s expectations through our character, operations, interactions and personnel.', "Community Action Team Over the years, the Police Department has had problem-solving teams to work with communities in Hillsborough on a number of issues, such as: Car break-ins Littering Speeding Trespassing The Community Action Team: Continues to perform problem-solving work. Offers programming to build relationships with the community. If you have an issue in your Hillsborough community that you would like to discuss, submit a Police Department contact form or contact the police sergeant. See the Services for Your Property page to determine if your home is in Hillsborough's town limits. Community Programming Community Services Programs The Administrative and Support Division offers a variety of community programs and  nontraditional police services, including: Coffee with a Cop Community summits Community Watch Easter egg hunts for dogs Medication drop-off events National Night Out Safety presentations at schools, nursing homes, workplaces and similar venues For details about upcoming events: Subscribe to town news . Visit the Police Department Facebook page . To schedule an officer to appear at an event, submit a Police Department contact form or contact the police sergeant or senior administrative support specialist. Electric Bikes The Police Department has three electric bikes that it uses to: Connect with community members. Maneuver through crowds at community events and through wooded areas, fields and other places a patrol vehicle might not be able to access. Patrol areas  without drawing as much attention as a patrol vehicle. Search for missing people. The Pedego e-bikes are\xa0mountain terrain rated and have packs with safety and first-aid supplies. Community grant funds from the Orange County Alcoholic Beverage Control Board\xa0helped pay for their cost. The bikes can travel up to almost 30 miles per hour and have an estimated range of 38 to 76 miles on a full charge.", "The Hillsborough Police Department is always looking for individuals to join our team who have a high moral character and a genuine desire to serve and make a difference. The Town of Hillsborough is an equal opportunity employer that welcomes applicants from diverse backgrounds. All applicants will be treated in a fair and consistent manner. Selection for employment will be made based on the applicants' qualifications for the job. Opportunities may be available for: Individuals with no experience who are interested in law enforcement. Individuals looking to start a career who have completed basic law enforcement training. Lateral transfers from other agencies. Apply Police Information Recruitment Video Benefits Benefits of Joining HPD The Town of Hillsborough offers a competitive benefits package, including: Salary — The starting salary for an officer who has completed basic law enforcement training is $58,966. Hiring incentives also may be offered: 2% increase for an associate’s degree or 4% for a bachelor’s degree in a related field. Up to 3% increase for each year of comparable service, up to 25%. $1,500 for Spanish fluency. Shift differential of $4 per hour between midnight and 7 a.m. Officers may attain up to two specializations with 2.5% pay increase for each. Specializations include: Canine unit Collision investigation specialist Community engagement specialist Investigative specialist Tactical and medical specialist Traffic and drug enforcement specialist Pay scales are as follows: $58,966 to $94,345 for police officer $61,739 to $98,782 for police officer 1st class $64,679 to $103,487 for corporal Career progression — Hillsborough's two-step career progression system allows officers to advance their careers outside of regular performance evaluations and\xa0opportunities for promotion. Officers who complete certain training requirements can be eligible at 12 months of service for officer 1st class, the first step of the career progression. Each step up to corporal provides a 5% salary increase. Individually assigned vehicles — Each officer is assigned a vehicle. Officers living within 30 miles of Hillsborough may be eligible to take the vehicle home. Tuition assistance — The department prioritizes education. The town will provide up to $5,250 in tuition reimbursement each year. Training — The department also prioritizes developing and maintaining skills. Officers have an annual minimum goal of 40 hours of elective training (external training that officers choose to attend beyond their required training). Officers are allowed up to 120 hours of elective training per year. Workout time — Officers may work out for up to 1.5 hours on duty. Ask about other physical fitness incentives. Disqualifiers Disqualifiers Factors that will disqualify a candidate from joining the department include: Criminal history — Any felony or crime conviction that could have required more than two years of incarceration as punishment. Conviction of a Class B misdemeanor within the preceding five years. Conviction of multiple misdemeanor, non-traffic crimes within the preceding five years. Any history of domestic violence. A history of criminal charges that reflect poorly on the candidate’s character. Traffic violations — Having a driver's license suspended or revoked after the date of application or\xa0in the three years before. Any history of driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs. Multiple at-fault accidents or driving violations beyond minor speeding or right-of-way violations. Drug use —\xa0Illegal drug use beyond youthful experimentation. The department evaluates prior drug use for its extent and for how recent it was. Credit —\xa0Demonstrated history of financial irresponsibility. Other — Former military personnel discharged dishonorably and ineligible for reenlistment. Untruthfulness or intentional withholding of information from any application, interview or paperwork associated with the position. Deliberate inaccuracies or incomplete statements. Cheating on any examination or testing associated with the position. Actions or activities that call into question the candidate’s character, integrity or morality. Hiring Process Lateral Transfers and New Hire Information Police Officer candidates and non-sworn police department applicants must meet the minimum standards as required by the North Carolina Criminal Justice Standards Commission. Police Officer applicants must have completed Basic Law Enforcement Training (BLET) and be eligible for sworn status in the State of North Carolina before assuming the role of officer. Successful candidates must adhere to standards set forth in sub chapter 09B of the North Carolina Criminal Justice Employment: Education & Training. The department most frequently hires candidates who are: Completing or have completed a basic law enforcement training program. Sworn with other agencies and are seeking a change. However, the department occasionally hires and puts recruits through a basic law enforcement training program. Candidates who accept an offer to be hired as a recruit officer must agree to a two-year commitment to the Town of Hillsborough. Completing the hiring process can take several months. The process\xa0generally includes: Application — Hillsborough accepts applications through its employment website . Recruits will be required to submit all necessary documents, including: F-3 personal history statement Liability release Wri tten testing — Hillsborough uses a standardized, written, entry-level basic skills test\xa0in math, reading comprehension, grammar and incident report writing to help ensure candidates have the basic cognitive skills needed to perform the job. Physical assessment — Lateral candidates are asked to complete the\xa0Police Officer Physical Abilities Test\xa0within the state-mandated time. This requirement is waived if applicants can provide documentation that they have successfully completed the test within the past six months. Recruits will be required to complete a\xa0modified version of the Police Officer Physical Abilities Test to ensure candidates are adequately prepared to enter a basic law enforcement training program. Panel interview — Based on the results of written and physical testing, candidates will be invited to a panel interview. During this interview, three or four staff members will meet with the candidates and ask a series of questions. They will evaluate the candidates and provide a recommendation. Interview with the chief — Based on the results of the panel interview, the chief may conduct an additional interview with the candidate. This interview generally is conducted at the beginning of the process for lateral hires. Background investigation — Candidates who successfully complete the steps above will be moved as a group into the background investigation phase. The number referred varies depending on the number of openings and staff available to conduct background investigations. During the background investigation, an experienced investigator will meet with a candidate, verify all information provided and ensure no disqualifying factors or omissions exist. Conditional offer —\xa0The town will make a conditional job offer once the department is satisfied that a candidate: Has completed the hiring steps above. Is a person it wishes to hire. The conditional offer will outline salary, benefits and expectations.\xa0It is conditional upon the successful completion of some or all of the following: Drug screening Medical and psychological evaluations Polygraph testing where applicable Upon successfully completing the hiring process, a new candidate would be hired as a recruit officer and enrolled in a basic law enforcement training program at a nearby community college. The recruit officer would attend the program, and the department would provide all uniforms and materials for attending the program. Basic Law Enforcement Training We offer opportunities for interested people to enroll in and complete basic training through sponsorship or employment as a police recruit. About the Training Becoming a police officer in North Carolina requires successfully completing a basic law enforcement training program.\xa0The full-time program takes about 16-20 weeks to complete and concludes with a comprehensive written exam and skills testing. The program\xa0prepares entry-level individuals with the cognitive and physical skills needed to become certified law enforcement officers. It includes: 36 blocks of instruction with topics including: Arrest, search and seizure Driver training Firearms Motor vehicle law Practical exercises Extensive ethics training woven throughout the course. Minimum requirements to attend the basic training include being: A United States citizen. At least 20 years old. A high school graduate or having passed the General Educational Development tests. Being of good moral character. Basic law enforcement training programs are offered through many community colleges across the state. More information is available on the North Carolina Justice Academy website . Basic Training Sponsorship Individuals wishing to attended a BLET program can request that the Hillsborough Police Department sponsor them. Sponsorship allows the program’s $900 community college tuition to be waived. The individual is responsible for costs associated with books, uniforms and other supplies. Sponsorship is not an offer of employment, and neither the individual nor the agency is making a commitment. Those wishing to be considered for sponsorship must complete an application online through the town's employment website . All required documents must be submitted, including the F-3 personal history statement and the liability release. Those seeking to be considered for sponsorship must indicate this on the supplemental question that is part of the application. Sponsorship requests are considered on a case-by-case basis and generally do not involve a specific process and only minor background checks. Contact Information Police Station, 127 N. Churton St. 919-296-9500 919-732-2187 (fax) Office phones are answered 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. weekdays. Contact Police Department Staff Title Phone Email Chelenza, Nicholas Lieutenant 919-296-9523 Email Hooks, Richard Captain 919-296-9524 Email Simmons, Andy Assistant Chief of Police 919-296-9522 Email"]

Name: 38_content_media_social_comments
Representative_Docs: ['Comments Policy Comments Policy All content posted to the Town of Hillsborough’s social media sites by the public should be related to discussion of town-sponsored programs, services, projects, issues, events and activities. Please note that this is a moderated online discussion site and subject to North Carolina public records laws and electronic discovery laws and policies. All comments and content shared on our government-sponsored social media sites must pertain to items within the jurisdictional control or authority of our governmental unit. The Town of Hillsborough reserves the right to delete submissions that do not meet the purpose of this site. The following list is not intended to be all-inclusive. It includes examples of unacceptable social networking content and comments. Content or comments that are spam or not topically related to town-sponsored programs, services, projects, issues, events and activities or the particular post being commented on. This includes comments not related to the jurisdiction or to the authority of the jurisdiction. Profane or obscene language or content. Such language or content is subject to being blocked or removed by the social media platform’s filtering functions. This includes sexual content or links to sexual content. Content that promotes, fosters or perpetuates discrimination on the basis of race; creed; color; age; religion; gender; gender identity; gender expression; marital status; status with regard to public assistance; national origin; physical or mental disability; sexual orientation; or any other protected class. Content that advocates violence or illegal activity or compromises the safety and security of the public or public system. Confidential or nonpublic information, including any personally identifiable medical information or content that violates the legal rights of the owner and said content. Promotions or content related to political campaigns or organizations. Solicitations of commerce, including content that contains gratuitous links (links or references to other relevant governmental webpages or official government sites are acceptable). Infringement on copyrights or trademarks. Duplicate posts by the same author (the original post will be left intact assuming it follows the guidelines of acceptable comments or content). The Town of Hillsborough also reserves the right to disallow comments altogether. This policy is subject to amendment or modification at any time.', 'Disclaimer Statement Disclaimer Statement Representatives of the Town of Hillsborough government communicate via town social media sites. Consequently, any communication on our social media site is subject to North Carolina public records law. Any comment, feedback or other method of communication made by someone other than an official representative of the Town of Hillsborough is solely the view and opinion of the author. It does not represent an official communication from the Town of Hillsborough or any member of its staff. The Town of Hillsborough does not endorse any advertisement or marketing effort that may be seen on our social media sites. Name: 40_south_churton_street_study
Representative_Docs: ["South Churton Street Multimodal Corridor Study —\xa0South Churton Street is being studied to develop design concepts that contribute to the town's character and meet the goals of a state-funded project to widen the road from Interstate 40 to the Eno River. See the South Churton Street Multimodal Corridor Study project page.", "South Churton Street is being studied to develop design concepts that contribute to the town's character and meet the goals of a state-funded project to widen the road from Interstate 40 to the Eno River. Funding Funding The multimodal corridor study costs $200,000. The town contributed $50,000 in Fiscal Year 2023 to match $150,000 in funding from the Metropolitan Planning Organization. Construction is currently funded by the North Carolina Department of Transportation. Schedule Schedule The planning study was completed in 2024 and endorsed by the Board of Commissioners on June 10, 2024. Project findings and recommended improvements will be shared with the North Carolina Department of Transportation to inform the future construction project. The construction project is scheduled in the draft State Transportation Improvement Program for Fiscal Year 2033. Background South Churton Street serves as the primary entrance into town from interstates 40 and 85 and is one of the more heavily traveled roads in Hillsborough. It carries between 20,000 and 25,000 annual average daily trips, many of which are generated by commuters from beyond the town limits. State Plans The State Transportation Improvement Program includes a project to widen South Churton Street from Interstate 40 to the Eno River to relieve traffic congestion and provide north-south pedestrian and bicycle connectivity. In 2019, the North Carolina Department of Transportation developed concepts for the widening project. No preferred alternative or cross section was designated by the town at that time. Town Study Town staff and leaders recognize the importance of a well-designed South Churton Street. A quality design that enhances safety, improves flow and presents a beautiful entryway to town will benefit the local community and the wider network. The town has initiated this study to develop alternatives that consider the needs of the town and all users of the corridor. Name: 42_roads_maintained_streets_repaving

Name: 43_security_home_police_checks
Representative_Docs: ["On request and at no cost, officers will: Assess a home's security and offer suggestions for safety improvements related to lighting and landscaping. Provide security checks of homes in town limits when residents are not home. Home Security Checks Officers will: Check your home when you are away to ensure that doors and windows appear secure and untouched. Look for irregularities, such as damage to property and leaking pipes. They cannot guarantee that all irregularities will be found. Officers will not: Enter a home without signs that the home is unsecured. Water or cut grass. Feed animals. To request a house check, submit a Police Department contact form or contact the police sergeant or senior administrative support specialist. See the Services for Your Property page to determine whether your home is in town limits.", "Home Security Assessments and Checks On request and at no cost, officers will: Assess a home's security and offer suggestions for safety improvements related to lighting and landscaping. Provide security checks of homes in town limits when residents are not home. Home Security Checks Officers will: Check your home when you are away to ensure that doors and windows appear secure and untouched. Look for irregularities, such as damage to property and leaking pipes. They cannot guarantee that all irregularities will be found. Officers will not: Enter a home without signs that the home is unsecured. Water or cut grass. Feed animals. To request a house check, submit a Police Department contact form or contact the police sergeant or senior administrative support specialist. See the Services for Your Property page to determine whether your home is in town limits.", 'Subscribe to news releases or sign up for emergency alerts . Police Conduct Home Security Checks Hillsborough police officers provide free home security checks and advice Post Date: May 27, 2026 8:30 a.m. Police Officer Jordan McDonald demonstrates a security check at a Hillsborough home. Whether you’re away for the day or for several weeks, the Hillsborough Police Department can help you make your home more secure. When requested, Hillsborough police officers will check on your home when you’re away and provide advice on how to make your property more secure before you go. “We want to do everything we can to help our residents,” said Police Sgt. Scott Foster, who leads the department’s community action team. “Our mission is to protect people and property.” According to a study by the home security company ADT, 65% of home break-ins occur during the day and more home burglaries occur in the summer than in other seasons. Asking the Police Department for help or following officers’ general tips may help save you the headache of a home break-in. Security and Property Checks When requested, an officer will visit your property as time permits and walk around the outside of your home. The time of the visit will be logged, and the visit will include visually checking that your windows and doors remain secure. For security assistance beyond two weeks, the Police Department offers a property check, which involves driving by your home as time permits but not necessarily walking on your property. In addition to giving you peace of mind, the frequent visits may deter someone from breaking in. They also provide a time frame for a break-in, should one occur. To schedule a security or property check, contact Senior Administrative Support Specialist Danielle King at least two business days before you plan to be away  at 919-296-9500 or by contact form . These services are secondary to primary emergency services but are still taken very seriously. A date, time or frequency cannot be guaranteed, but the Hillsborough Police Department does its best to maintain a high standard of customer service for these requests. Security Consultation Police officers also are available to meet with you to evaluate the security of your house and property and make suggestions. This consultation includes outside security considerations, such as fences, lighting and landscaping as well as doors, windows and locks. It also includes interior security, such as alarms, safes and how to best protect property inside the home. To request a security consultation, contact Foster by phone at 919-296-9563 or by contact form. General Security Tips Consider these simple tips to help secure your home. Outside: Lock up ladders — An unsecured ladder may be used to break into your home. Avoid hiding a key outside. Leave a key with a neighbor if you’re worried about locking yourself out. If you can, park your car in your garage while you’re away rather than in the driveway. Inside your home: Use a timer so lights come on at night, but don’t have only the hallway light on timer. Place a timer on a radio. Close blinds or drapes, or remove valuables from areas that can be seen through windows. In your garage: Lock garage doors. If your garage is attached to your house, lock the house door in your garage. Remove garage door opener remotes from any cars remaining on your property while you’re away. Other: Don’t post on social media that you’re on vacation. Test smoke alarms before going on vacation. Ask a neighbor to pick up your mail and packages. Return to full list >> Subscribe to News Subscribe to Emergency Alerts']

Name: 45_investigation_bureau_internal_attorney
Representative_Docs: ['The police chief shall request that the State Bureau of Investigation conduct an independent criminal investigation of any incident involving the use of deadly force by a department member that results in death or serious physical injury to any person. The bureau’s role is to obtain all the facts for the district attorney to make a determination as to any criminal liability on the part of the involved law enforcement officers.', 'Criminal Investigation The police chief shall request that the State Bureau of Investigation conduct an independent criminal investigation of any incident involving the use of deadly force by a department member that results in death or serious physical injury to any person. The bureau’s role is to obtain all the facts for the district attorney to make a determination as to any criminal liability on the part of the involved law enforcement officers. The State Bureau of Investigation has established procedures for investigation of such incidents to help ensure the public’s confidence in an investigation’s objectivity and integrity. Procedures to be expected include: A team of bureau agents will immediately respond to handle the initial aspects of the investigation and assume overall responsibility for conducting the investigation. Initial aspects of the investigation include crime scene processing and interviewing of witnesses.) The Police Department will turn over all aspects of the criminal investigation to the bureau. The Police Department, as standard practice, will release the name of the involved officers to the news media and confirm that the State Bureau of Investigation has been asked to investigate the matter. Involved officers interviewed by the bureau will be advised that the investigation is criminal in nature and of their constitutional rights. Unless an officer specifically requests an attorney and/or someone else to be present during the interview, no one other than bureau personnel will participate in the interview. Upon completing the investigation, the State Bureau of Investigation will submit a written report to the district attorney for review and a decision on whether further investigation or prosecution is warranted. Officers should remain aware that submission of the bureau’s final report to the district attorney might be delayed pending receipt of laboratory reports and/or medical examiner findings.', 'The police chief shall request that the State Bureau of Investigation conduct an independent criminal investigation of any incident involving the use of deadly force by a department member that results in death or serious physical injury to any person. Name: 46_weather_snow_plowing_collections
Representative_Docs: ['Operations Below is information on how the town operates during severe weather events. Curbside Collections Hillsborough will stop curbside collections of trash, brush, bulk items and loose leaves if weather conditions prevent collection trucks from operating safely. Garbage collection will be given priority when collections resume. Orange County provides collection of recyclables. See the Orange County website for changes in its services. Parks and Trails Before and during severe weather, staff will close parks and greenways as needed to keep the community and emergency workers safe. When flooding is expected, the dog park fencing at Gold Park will be placed in an open position. This prevents damage to the fencing by allowing water carrying debris to flow through the open gates. After severe weather, parks and greenways will remain closed until the town can inspect them and remove any hazards. Please heed all safety signage and barricades and do not enter closed areas.\xa0Those who choose to use the town’s parks and greenways do so at their own risk.\xa0Entering closed areas or moving signs and barriers also puts others at risk and pulls emergency workers away from other duties. Recreation in Floodplains Undeveloped floodplains often serve as public parks and recreation. The Riverwalk greenway and Gold Park were built in their locations to provide recreation in areas that otherwise would not be developed. Riverwalk was built along a sewer easement by the Eno River. Name: 47_sponsorship_funding_nonprofit_partnerships
Representative_Docs: ["Basic Training Sponsorship Individuals wishing to attended a BLET program can request that the Hillsborough Police Department sponsor them. Sponsorship allows the program’s $900 community college tuition to be waived. The individual is responsible for costs associated with books, uniforms and other supplies. Sponsorship is not an offer of employment, and neither the individual nor the agency is making a commitment. Those wishing to be considered for sponsorship must complete an application online through the town's employment website . All required documents must be submitted, including the F-3 personal history statement and the liability release. Those seeking to be considered for sponsorship must indicate this on the supplemental question that is part of the application. Sponsorship requests are considered on a case-by-case basis and generally do not involve a specific process and only minor background checks.", "Individuals wishing to attended a BLET program can request that the Hillsborough Police Department sponsor them. Additionally, nonprofit agencies may seek funding from the two volunteer boards charged with overseeing the town's prepared food and beverage tax and its room occupancy tax. Town of Hillsborough Funding Through nonprofit partnerships, the town provides funding for a limited time to qualified agencies that offer services the town can legally fund. This program is a part of the annual budget cycle. Application Period: Jan. 9-March 31 Funding Policy Read-only Application Apply for Nonprofit Partnership Funds Tourism Funding About Tourism Funding The two Hillsborough appointed boards that oversee proceeds from taxes also reinvest the funds into tourism. Tourism Board — Reinvests funds from a 1% sales tax on prepared foods and beverages\xa0into: Hillsborough Visitors Center Grants, special projects and partnerships Contract services with local organizations and historic sites Tourism Development Authority — Reinvests funds from a 3% hotel and motel room occupancy tax\xa0to encourage visitors to stay in local lodging facilities. Special Projects and Partnerships Both the Tourism Board and Tourism Development Authority provide funds for special projects and partnerships. The projects and partnerships are ge nerally small in scope and ne eded only once. Requests must demonstrate or explain how the project or partnership will: Expand tourism if funded by either board. Increase Hillsborough lodging occupancies if funded by\xa0the Tourism Development Authority. During its March-June grant cycle, the Tourism Board generally does not accept requests for special projects and partnerships. Contact Staff Support Tourism Board Grants The Tourism Board accepts grant applications for a wide range of special events and projects designed to increase tourism to Hillsborough. February-March — The application period is open. April — Applications are competitively scored. May —\xa0Awards are announced at the Tourism Board meeting. June —\xa0Applicants receive official notification of funding status. The board requires a final report and the return of unused grant funds after a project has concluded. Name: 49_pump_station_stations_pumps
Representative_Docs: ["A pump station is a collection chamber that uses pumps to lift wastewater from a lower elevation to a higher elevation, where the wastewater can flow by gravity. It pumps into a pressurized main called a force main, which ends in a sewer manhole. There, wastewater can flow by gravity to the Wastewater Treatment Plant. The pump stations require frequent maintenance and weekly inspections. Improperly disposed rags, wipes or other trash can cause expensive repairs. Failures can result in larger sanitary sewer overflows than from a gravity sewer, with subsequent fines from the state. Why Pump Stations? Hillsborough has more pump stations per customer than surrounding water and sewer systems because of the town's terrain. Also, the systems were likely preferred decades ago due to the low upfront costs. Some of the pump stations were built by developers and turned over to the town to own and operate.", "The town is working to replace the 1970s-era River Pumping Station, moving it from the Eno River floodplain and increasing sewer capacity in the area. Flooding during Tropical Storm Chantal in July 2025 reached the base of the fence on top of the station's concrete structure. Location and Background The River Pumping Station transports 75% of the sewage of Hillsborough's customers to the Wastewater Treatment Plant. It was built in the 1970s near the Eno River by the Wastewater Treatment Plant on Elizabeth Brady Road. The project would relocate the station to higher ground out of the Eno River's 500-year floodplain. The pumping station has a history of flooding in severe weather, including during Hurricane Fran in 1996 (right photo), a storm in 1998 (left photo), and Tropical Storm Chantal in 2025. Flooding from Tropical Storm Chantal left the station's three pumps inoperable.\xa0The town used three bypass pumps through late December 2025 to prevent sewage spills while new pumps, motors, and electrical and control systems were purchased and installed. The station was restored to service in late December. Funding The estimated cost of the project is more than $10.2 million. Grant Funding The town received a\xa0Building Resilient Infrastructure and Communities grant of $5.8 million from the Federal Emergency Management Agency in August 2023. As a part of the grant, the town would pay for 30% of the project. In April 2025, the federal\xa0program was ended and all applications from fiscal years 2020-2023 were canceled. This affected Phase 2 of the River Pumping Station project, which would have used over $5.1 million in federal funds to build the new station. North Carolina and 19 other states sued FEMA in July 2025 over its decision to cancel the BRIC program. In December 2025, a U.S. district court judge ruled the program was unlawfully ended. On March 18, 2026, FEMA issued an advisory notifying states that the agency plans to resume programming support. As of June 2026, the BRIC program has been reestablished, and the town has received reimbursements from FEMA for expended design fees. The state  plans to transfer the project to its hazard mitigation grant program. Moving the project to the state's program would give the town more time to complete the project and include increased costs. Current Phase The project is  in the design phase. Had federal funding not been revoked, construction would have likely begun by the end of 2025. Name: 50_fiber_internet_agreement_google
Representative_Docs: ["Subcontractors Subcontractors are performing the fiber installations for all internet service providers. The subcontractors should have proper identification with them at all times while performing work. Their vehicles should be marked with a legible magnetic decal or similar marking that identifies the vehicles as a subcontractor for the companies and provides a contact phone number. Markings Excavation requires notification to North Carolina 811 to allow utilities to mark underground lines for safety. The work may proceed if utilities have not been marked within the prescribed time period and if no visible indications of underground utilities exist at the site. Several weeks before construction, paint markings will be sprayed on the ground. The paint typically disappears in a few weeks. For more information regarding markings, see the North Carolina 811 website. Color Codes The uniform color codes mandated by the American Public Works Association for temporarily marking utility lines are: Red — Electric power lines, cables, conduit and lighting cables Orange — Telecommunication, alarm or signal lines, cables or conduit Yellow — Natural gas, oil, steam, petroleum or other flammable Green — Sewers and drain lines Blue — Potable drinking water Purple — Reclaimed water, irrigation and slurry lines Pink — Temporary survey markings, unknown/unidentified facilities White — Proposed excavation limits or routes Construction Construction Type and Additions Trenching and horizontal boring are being used to excavate in rights of way. The trenching involves digging a shallower and narrower trench than traditional excavation methods. It can help in avoiding tree roots and existing underground utilities. Horizontal boring machines are being used for tight rights of way, deeper installation needs or the crossing of driveways. Periodic utility vaults are being installed in the right of way. Smaller plastic fiber vaults are being installed on property lines for individual service, similar in location to phone and cable provider boxes. The vaults are like water meter boxes in appearance. They are typically gray and are being placed at the same grade as the right of way. Construction Area This image is from Orange County's Geographic Information System website with the photography layer turned on. Town staff added the black and blue to highlight the street and right of way. A public right of way is designated between most streets and the adjacent private property. This is where service providers are allowed by law to place their fiber along with water, sewer and electrical lines. The right of way typically extends to the water meter box and sewer cleanout pipe on a property. Residents can review the approximate location of the rights of way for most streets on Orange County’s Geographic Information System website. Enter an address and select the photography button in the upper right corner to see property lines and streets. Contractors should receive permission from property owners before working on private property if permission is not already granted through an easement. This would include townhouse, condominium and apartment complexes. Construction Hours Work within town rights of way will typically occur from 7 a.m. to 7 p.m. weekdays. Permission to expand this schedule will be given sometimes on a case-by-case basis. Post-Construction In most cases and weather permitting, private property and public rights of way should be restored to original condition within three to five business days once construction in an area is complete. Companies are not required by law to coordinate their activities. Once the work of one company is complete, the construction process could start again by another company. Construction in your area does not mean that new or improved service will be available to you immediately. Residents should reach out to the internet service providers directly for availability timelines.", "Private utility companies are installing gigabit fiber-optic cables throughout Hillsborough. The installations should bring more services, capabilities and choices for those living and doing business in our community. Fiber-optic cable installation near Churton Street Project Ownership The Town of Hillsborough is not performing or paying for this work. The installations are private projects owned and operated by the utility companies and their contractors. The state regulates excavation on public and private property to prevent damage to underground utilities. See more information on the North Carolina Law page of the North Carolina 811 website. Internet Service Providers Brightspeed Brightspeed has a Certificate of Public Convenience from the North Carolina Public Utilities Commission. This agreement allows the town 30 days to review plans, which must be approved if state conditions are met. Google Fiber The town has a master encroachment agreement with Google Fiber. Lumos The town has a master encroachment agreement with Lumos. Lumos is under contract with Orange County to provide broadband internet to rural areas. Spectrum Spectrum\xa0has expressed interest in bringing its fiber internet service to Hillsborough. The company has a franchise agreement with the state to provide video programming services. This agreement will allow the town 30 days to review plans, which must be approved if state conditions are met. Work within Public Rights of Way The utility companies have the legal right to operate within rights of way of the town and of the North Carolina Department of Transportation. The town can impose additional conditions in its rights of way through encroachment agreements with utilities that do not already have special agreements with the state. For utilities that have special agreements with the state, the town only may require utilities to keep roads functional, follow existing laws and repair any damage caused in the rights of way. Work on Private Property Any activity on privately owned property requires approval of the property owner. Utility companies may have legal right to operate in some private areas through a utility easement managed by a homeowners association or other private owner. The town does not have authority to regulate work on private property. This includes final connections between the street and house. What to Expect All Fiber Companies Subcontractors Subcontractors are performing the fiber installations for all internet service providers. The subcontractors should have proper identification with them at all times while performing work. Their vehicles should be marked with a legible magnetic decal or similar marking that identifies the vehicles as a subcontractor for the companies and provides a contact phone number. Markings Excavation requires notification to North Carolina 811 to allow utilities to mark underground lines for safety. The work may proceed if utilities have not been marked within the prescribed time period and if no visible indications of underground utilities exist at the site. Several weeks before construction, paint markings will be sprayed on the ground. The paint typically disappears in a few weeks. For more information regarding markings, see the North Carolina 811 website. Color Codes The uniform color codes mandated by the American Public Works Association for temporarily marking utility lines are: Red — Electric power lines, cables, conduit and lighting cables Orange — Telecommunication, alarm or signal lines, cables or conduit Yellow — Natural gas, oil, steam, petroleum or other flammable Green — Sewers and drain lines Blue — Potable drinking water Purple — Reclaimed water, irrigation and slurry lines Pink — Temporary survey markings, unknown/unidentified facilities White — Proposed excavation limits or routes Construction Construction Type and Additions Trenching and horizontal boring are being used to excavate in rights of way. The trenching involves digging a shallower and narrower trench than traditional excavation methods. It can help in avoiding tree roots and existing underground utilities. Horizontal boring machines are being used for tight rights of way, deeper installation needs or the crossing of driveways. Periodic utility vaults are being installed in the right of way. Smaller plastic fiber vaults are being installed on property lines for individual service, similar in location to phone and cable provider boxes. The vaults are like water meter boxes in appearance. They are typically gray and are being placed at the same grade as the right of way. Construction Area This image is from Orange County's Geographic Information System website with the photography layer turned on. Town staff added the black and blue to highlight the street and right of way. A public right of way is designated between most streets and the adjacent private property. This is where service providers are allowed by law to place their fiber along with water, sewer and electrical lines. The right of way typically extends to the water meter box and sewer cleanout pipe on a property. Residents can review the approximate location of the rights of way for most streets on Orange County’s Geographic Information System website. Enter an address and select the photography button in the upper right corner to see property lines and streets. Contractors should receive permission from property owners before working on private property if permission is not already granted through an easement. This would include townhouse, condominium and apartment complexes. Construction Hours Work within town rights of way will typically occur from 7 a.m. to 7 p.m. weekdays. Permission to expand this schedule will be given sometimes on a case-by-case basis. Post-Construction In most cases and weather permitting, private property and public rights of way should be restored to original condition within three to five business days once construction in an area is complete. Companies are not required by law to coordinate their activities. Once the work of one company is complete, the construction process could start again by another company. Construction in your area does not mean that new or improved service will be available to you immediately. Residents should reach out to the internet service providers directly for availability timelines. Google Fiber In addition to the expectations for all internet service providers, the Google Fiber subcontractor should distribute informational door hangers in advance of construction in your neighborhood. Questions and Complaints Register formal complaints with the state by contacting the North Carolina Attorney General’s Office . Construction Concerns or Reporting Damages For questions or concerns about construction in your area, communicate with the crew working in the area. If additional discussion is needed, call the number on the subcontractor vehicle or door hanger or use the contact information below for assistance. If you are concerned for contractor safety, contact the Occupational Safety and Health Administration directly. Call 911 immediately if you see a danger threatening life or health.
