Name: 51_handlers_dogs_canine_jett
Representative_Docs: ['Training The police dogs are certified annually by an independent trainer. Each month, the canine officers and their handlers train for at least 16 hours in a variety of scenarios and in different locations and weather conditions. They also train with handlers and dogs from surrounding agencies.', 'Canine Training Police dogs are certified annually by an independent trainer. Each month, the canine officers and their handlers train for at least 16 hours in a variety of scenarios and in different locations and weather conditions. They also train with handlers and dogs from surrounding agencies.', 'Canine Officers Hillsborough currently has one police dog: Jett. Jett is a dual-purpose patrol dog in that she can perform patrol search duties in addition to protecting her partner and apprehending suspect. Jett joined the department in 2018. The German shepherd dog was named by students in Hillsborough. Police dogs typically retire between 6 and 10 years of age. They live with their handlers. Demonstrations The canine officer and handler demonstrate their work for local schools and community groups. To request a demonstration, submit a Police Department contact form or call the police sergeant or senior administrative support specialist. Training The police dogs are certified annually by an independent trainer. Each month, the canine officers and their handlers train for at least 16 hours in a variety of scenarios and in different locations and weather conditions. They also train with handlers and dogs from surrounding agencies.']

Name: 52_energy_conducted_aerosol_weapon
Representative_Docs: ['To reduce the risk of unintentional injury, the use of an aerosol spray should be avoided on the following groups of people unless urgent circumstances exist: People believed to be 65 years of age or older. Any person in control of a vehicle that is in gear or in motion. Any person in a position that creates the likelihood of significant additional injury other than those effects reasonably anticipated due to the use of the aerosol spray. Once a subject has been sprayed and subdued and the scene is safe, officers shall monitor the person’s breathing and consciousness. As soon as reasonable, officers shall decontaminate the effected prisoner by flushing the exposed area with fresh water and then exposing the area to fresh air. Emergency Medical Services shall be contacted if the subject who has been sprayed: Appears to be taking an excessive amount of time to recover. Exhibits any signs of respiratory or other distress. Complains of a preexisting respiratory condition. Was wearing contact lenses and/or complains of having a preexisting eye injury or condition. Requests medical treatment. Conducted energy weapons and aerosol sprays shall only be used in situations where the subject poses an immediate and continuing danger or to stop the flight of a subject trying to avoid arrest and presenting a risk to the safety of the community or others if not immediately apprehended. The officer must be reasonably able to articulate the risk of safety. Non-compliance and non-violent physical resistance do not necessarily create an immediate and continuing danger. Conducted energy weapons and aerosol sprays shall not be used on a subject who physically resists in a non-violent manner, unless the situation is such that an officer could reasonably conclude that the resistance presents some immediate danger, despite the subject’s non-violent character.', 'Aerosol Spray — Oleoresin Capsicum (Pepper) Spray To reduce the risk of unintentional injury, the use of an aerosol spray should be avoided on the following groups of people unless urgent circumstances exist: People believed to be 65 years of age or older. Any person in control of a vehicle that is in gear or in motion. Conducted energy weapons and aerosol sprays shall only be used in situations where the subject poses an immediate and continuing danger or to stop the flight of a subject trying to avoid arrest and presenting a risk to the safety of the community or others if not immediately apprehended. The officer must be reasonably able to articulate the risk of safety. Non-compliance and non-violent physical resistance do not necessarily create an immediate and continuing danger. Conducted energy weapons and aerosol sprays shall not be used on a subject who physically resists in a non-violent manner, unless the situation is such that an officer could reasonably conclude that the resistance presents some immediate danger, despite the subject’s non-violent character.', 'Conducted Energy Weapons (Taser) To reduce the risk of unintentional injury, use of a conducted energy weapon should be avoided on the following groups of people unless urgent circumstances exist: People believed to be 65 years of age or older. Women reasonably believed to be pregnant. Visibly frail people. Any person in control of a vehicle that is in gear or in motion. Any person’s head, neck or genitalia. Any person in a position that creates the likelihood of significant additional injury other than those effects reasonably anticipated due to the use of the conducted energy weapon. A conducted energy weapon will not be used under the following circumstances: On a person actively being sprayed with any aerosol weapon. In proximity to any known flammable or highly combustible substances that may be ignited by the conducted energy weapon, to include any person who has been exposed to known flammable or highly combustible substances such as gasoline. Removal of conducted energy weapon probes in non-sensitive areas may be done by officers according to probe-removal training guidelines. Officers, or other trained personnel, will provide first aid following the removal of the probes as needed. Medical personnel (such as Emergency Medical Services) shall be used to: Remove probes in sensitive areas. Remove probes that have broken off and are imbedded under the skin. In any situation where officers on the scene believe the use of medical personnel is appropriate. The officer must be reasonably able to articulate the risk of safety. Non-compliance and non-violent physical resistance do not necessarily create an immediate and continuing danger. Name: 53_greenway_collins_ridge_ridgewalk
Representative_Docs: ['This project was first introduced when Collins Ridge was seeking  approval for its Master Plan. Conditions of the approval require the developers to reserve and make available to the town land to accommodate pedestrian and bicycle connectivity and to work with the town to determine the location and specific design details for pedestrian and bicycle connectivity between the parcel south of I-85 and the public rights-of-way, sidewalks, greenways, and trails in Collins Ridge. This project was identified by the Board of Commissioners as a transportation priority in September 2021. Outside Groups Coordination with the North Carolina Department of Transportation will be required as the greenway must cross Interstate 85. The current plan is for a greenway and wildlife path underneath a bridge near Collins Ridge. The town must also work with the North Carolina Railroad Company to cross the rail corridor.', 'The proposed Ridgewalk greenway would be a paved, accessible urban greenway for pedestrians and bicyclists that connects downtown Hillsborough to the future train station site and Cates Creek Park in Waterstone. Background This project was first introduced when Collins Ridge was seeking  approval for its Master Plan. Conditions of the approval require the developers to reserve and make available to the town land to accommodate pedestrian and bicycle connectivity and to work with the town to determine the location and specific design details for pedestrian and bicycle connectivity between the parcel south of I-85 and the public rights-of-way, sidewalks, greenways, and trails in Collins Ridge. This project was identified by the Board of Commissioners as a transportation priority in September 2021. Outside Groups Coordination with the North Carolina Department of Transportation will be required as the greenway must cross Interstate 85. The current plan is for a greenway and wildlife path underneath a bridge near Collins Ridge. The town must also work with the North Carolina Railroad Company to cross the rail corridor. Location and Sections It is likely that the greenway project will be implemented in sections. The main section will be between downtown and the train station. The train station could be complete in Fiscal Year 2028, and the town plans to complete the Ridgewalk greenway connection around the same time. The second section will connect the train station to the section of greenway being built and dedicated to the town by the developers of Collins Ridge. The third section will connect Collins Ridge to Cates Creek Park. Current Phase Preliminary engineering and discussion with North Carolina Department of Transportation and the North Carolina Railroad Company have begun. More information will be provided as engineering on projects that must be finished first are completed. Documents Documents Ridgewalk Greenway Feasibility Study Phase 1 (2023) Ridgewalk Greenway Feasibility Study Phase 2 (2024) Ridgewalk Greenway Benefits Analysis Contact Information Town Hall, 101 E. Name: 54_theft_tampering_box_hydrant
Representative_Docs: ['Theft from Hydrants If you see a person or business taking water from a water hydrant without an orange meter box, report it immediately to 911 and to the utility system superintendent. To help the town prosecute the case: Discreetly take a photo if possible. Note the time, date, location, vehicle name and number, and license plate. Legally Obtained Water Contractors are able to purchase bulk water from the town. They can: Obtain water from the Water Treatment Plant by paying in advance at the Town Hall Annex. Rent water meters to legally obtain water from a fire hydrant. The meters\xa0are housed in an orange box with a backflow prevention device to safely obtain water. See the Start or Stop Service page.', "Customers fund the operation of the town's water and sewer system, including the infrastructure needed to treat, transport and protect water. Please immediately call 911 to report any tampering or theft. See Fees and Charges Schedule for fees and penalties. See Utilities: Prohibited Activities in the Code of Ordinances. Water can be legally and safely obtained from a fire hydrant with an orange box. Billing and Adjustments Start or Stop Service Tampering and Theft of Infrastructure Backflow Prevention Devices The town own some backflow prevention devices on town properties. Most are privately owned. If you see anyone tampering with a backflow prevention device or have experienced a theft of the device: Call 911 to have an officer dispatched. Contact the utilities infrastructure protection supervisor. The devices are housed in utility boxes and prevent contaminated water from entering potable water in a building. They can be targets for theft due to their metal content. Property owners who have had a backflow preventer stolen or damaged will need to employ the services of a plumber. Preventing Theft Options for preventing theft include: Employing community or neighborhood watch prevention, asking community members to report any suspicious behavior to police. Adding a lock to the backflow preventer box or installing a locking box, available from a plumber or from a plumbing warehouse. If locks are added, access must be given to the town. Cutting back any shrubs or other plantings to allow some view of the device and ensuring that an area of at least 5 feet around the utility is free of any growth. Water Meters Any tampering of a meter or service connection will result in the meter being locked and a tampering fee and civil penalty. Service will not be reconnected until all fees are paid in full. Repeated tampering will result in removal of the meter, additional fees to reset and reactivate it, and potential civil or criminal charges. Theft of Water Safety and Operational Concerns Water theft can cause concerns for the public water system including: Water hammer (form of hydraulic shock) in the system that breaks pipes and damages equipment. Lowered water pressure for customers during the time of theft. Contamination of the public water system. Costs associated with repairing damage to the system. Name: 55_closed_greenways_parks_barriers
Representative_Docs: ['Please heed all safety signage and barricades and do not enter closed areas.\xa0Those who choose to use the town’s parks and greenways do so at their own risk.\xa0Entering closed areas or moving signs and barriers also puts others at risk and pulls emergency workers away from other duties.', 'Parks and greenways are closed at times for the safety of the community and emergency workers. Hazards can exist even after a storm with high winds or snow has passed or after floodwaters have receded. Parks and greenways: Will remain closed following severe weather until the town can inspect the areas and remove any hazards. Are not a priority for snow and ice removal. They will be closed in deteriorating wintry conditions until staff determines they are safe. During Closures Heed any closure signs or barriers for parks and trails. Those who choose to use the town’s parks and greenways do so at their own risk. By entering closed areas and by moving or removing closure signs and barriers, you also: Slow cleanup efforts and delay the reopening of parks and greenways for everyone. Force public works and public safety staff away from other duties to reinstall closure signs and barriers. Force emergency workers away from other duties for potential rescues. Risk the safety of others. Subscribe to town news and follow @HillsboroughGov social media to stay informed\xa0about closures and reopenings.', 'Severe Weather Parks and greenways are closed at times for the safety of the community and emergency workers. Hazards can exist even after a storm with high winds or snow has passed or after floodwaters have receded. Parks and greenways: Will remain closed following severe weather until the town can inspect the areas and remove any hazards. Are not a priority for snow and ice removal. They will be closed in deteriorating wintry conditions until staff determines they are safe. During Closures Heed any closure signs or barriers for parks and trails. Name: 56_enterprise_fund_funds_sewer
Representative_Docs: ['Understanding Enterprise Funds The Water and Sewer Fund is one of two enterprise funds operated by the town. The other is the Stormwater Utility Fund. Enterprise funds account for the business-like activities of the government. These services are funded with revenues generated from fees and charges rather than taxes.\xa0No profits are made in enterprise funds, and the revenue is used for no other purpose. The Hillsborough Water and Sewer Fund pays for providing water and sewer service. The revenue for the fund comes\xa0primarily from water and sewer charges.', 'The Water and Sewer Fund is one of two enterprise funds operated by the town. The other is the Stormwater Utility Fund. Enterprise funds account for the business-like activities of the government. These services are funded with revenues generated from fees and charges rather than taxes.\xa0No profits are made in enterprise funds, and the revenue is used for no other purpose. The Hillsborough Water and Sewer Fund pays for providing water and sewer service. The revenue for the fund comes\xa0primarily from water and sewer charges.', "Water and sewer rates fund Hillsborough's water and sewer fund. This enterprise fund pays for providing water and sewer service, including operating, maintaining and improving the water and sewer system. The town's water and sewer rates are reviewed annually as part of the budget process. The rates schedule that is adopted with the budget becomes effective July 1 with the new fiscal year. West Fork Eno Reservoir (Photo by Schnabel Engineering) Board of Commissioners Budget and Finances Financial Services Utilities Department Current Rates In-Town Customers Water Rate $12.39 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Water Fee $24.78 for up to 2,000 gallons Sewer Rate $17.44 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Sewer Fee $34.88 for up to 2,000 gallons Out-of-Town Customers Water Rate $24.16 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Water Fee $48.32 for up to 2,000 gallons Sewer Rate $34.01 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Sewer Fee $68.02 for up to 2,000 gallons Proposed Rates In-Town Customers Water Rate $13.32 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Water Fee $26.64 for up to 2,000 gallons Sewer Rate $18.75 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Sewer Fee $37.50 for up to 2,000 gallons Out-of-Town Customers Water Rate $25.97 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Water Fee $51.94 for up to 2,000 gallons Sewer Rate $36.56 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Sewer Fee $73.12 for up to 2,000 gallons Each month, water and sewer customers who use the minimum amount charged or less spend a combined: 2.98 cents per gallon in town. 5.82 cents per gallon out of town. Residents used an average of about 3,440 gallons of water and sewer monthly in the last fiscal year —\xa0July 2024 through June 2025. Fees and Charges Schedule About the Rates Minimum Usage Charge The town charges for a minimum amount of water and sewer use each month. Additional use for both is billed per 1,000 gallons. Providing the availability of water and sewer service costs the town money even if a customer uses no water that month. The costs include: Managing and protecting Hillsborough's water source. Treating, storing and distributing drinking water. Collecting, treating and returning used water to the environment. Managing water and sewer billing, including meter reading, billing software, and bill printing and mailing. These operations also have costs for qualified workers, facilities, vehicles, equipment, chemicals, electricity, gas, compliance with regulations, and administrative needs. Minimum Fee Versus Base Fee All water and sewer systems charge a base fee or a minimum usage fee to ensure they can provide service. Base fee — No water is provided  with the monthly charge. Minimum usage fee — Water is provided with the monthly charge up to the minimum number of gallons set. In-Town Versus Out-of-Town Rates The most recent published annual research in North Carolina shows a higher outside rate is charged by 98% of water systems and 95% of sewer systems that offer service outside their geographical limits. The annual study is provided by the UNC School of Government's Environmental Finance Center and the North Carolina League of Municipalities. Out-of-town water and sewer fees are higher than in-town fees due to: Cost of Service — The operations, maintenance, pumping of water and sewer, and reading of meters can be more costly in areas outside of town, mainly because of greater distance and travel time from the town’s water or wastewater treatment plants. Fewer customers in the area to spread the cost can make the service more expensive. Growth Management — P roviding water and sewer service to developments outside of town can cause the town to lose some ability to attract development that would improve its tax base, help improve the economies of scale in providing services, and potentially ease the tax burden on residents.  Lower in-town fees also can make annexation more desirable in areas that have water and sewer service. Investment Risk — In-town customers bear more of the investment risks of owning and operating the water and sewer system. When revenue bonds are issued to finance large projects, like the reservoir, the town pledges its full  rate setting authority for water and sewer as collateral to ensure the debt will be repaid. Equity — In-town customers bear more of the burden of financing and facilitating the water and sewer system’s operations. In addition to water and sewer payments, their municipal property taxes help pay for positions and resources that support the water and sewer operations. They also subsidize the use of Hillsborough services by out-of-town customers who live near the town’s corporate limits, including: Streets Police protection when inside town limits Recreation facilities, such as Gold Park, Kings Highway Park and the Riverwalk greenway Understanding the Cost of Providing Service Every community has different conditions that affect the ability to provide safe and reliable water and sewer service. Below are primary reasons that Hillsborough’s water and sewer rates are higher than those of some surrounding communities. Small System —\xa0Hillsborough operates a small water and sewer system. A larger system can spread the costs of operations among more customers. Small Water Supply —\xa0The Eno River does not provide a large volume of water. The town is  near the river's beginning, and the drainage basin that feeds into Hillsborough’s waterways is relatively small. Partnering with a neighboring system is likely not feasible. The Carrboro-Chapel Hill area\xa0is in a different river basin, and a past evaluation found\xa0costs for partnering with Durham to be too expensive. The town built a reservoir in the late 1990s and completed an expansion of it in 2021 to address water capacity needs because of expected growth and a history of droughts. Debt Management —\xa0A portion of  water and sewer bill payments helps pay the debt on projects to provide clean and reliable water and sewer service, including: Wastewater Treatment Plant upgrades —\xa0$17.8 million financed with payments until 2034. Reservoir expansion —\xa0$10.2 million financed with payments until 2042. Roads project for reservoir expansion —\xa0$3.3 million financed with payments until 2030. By law, the town must pass a balanced budget each year. Planned expenses cannot fall below expected revenue, and debt payments cannot create a deficit. State Rules —\xa0Hillsborough must treat its wastewater at higher and more expensive standards than other sewer systems because its watershed drains into Falls Lake. The lake has been deemed impaired by the Environmental Protection Agency and nutrient-sensitive by the North Carolina Division of Water Resources. The state's Falls Lake Rules to protect and restore the lake’s water quality\xa0are said to be the nation’s strictest limits on treated wastewater. In 2014, Hillsborough completed upgrades to its wastewater treatment plant in part to meet the Stage 1 wastewater rule that went into effect in 2016. A large portion of customers' sewer payments helps pay debt on the plant upgrades. Stage 2 rules, expected in 2036,\xa0likely will require more upgrades. Expensive and Older Infrastructure —\xa0Hillsborough has more sewage pump stations than a typical municipality because of its terrain and likely because of low upfront costs.\xa0Gravity is the most cost-effective way to transport water and wastewater. Pump stations are expensive to operate, require a large amount of staff time and can result in overflows and state fines when they fail.\xa0The town operates 25 stations in about 5 square miles. Most were built by developers, are old and were not built to today's standards. The town also must budget to replace other infrastructure. Some were installed well before the town's treatment plants were built. Maintenance Commitment —\xa0Water and sewer services are expensive to provide. The United States  poorly maintains its drinking and wastewater infrastructure, earning grades of C- and D+ in the 2025 Report Card for America's Infrastructure from the American Society of Civil Engineers. Across North Carolina , $20 billion is needed to maintain and improve drinking water infrastructure and $12.6 billion is needed for wastewater infrastructure. Hillsborough is committed to taking care of its operations. Your water and sewer bill reflects your contribution to the labor and the physical infrastructure required to have clean water available and to have wastewater removed and safely returned to the environment. The town does not profit from providing water and sewer service. It uses water and sewer revenue only for the operation, maintenance and improvement of the town’s water and sewer system. Videos Full Cost Pricing of Water Water: What You Pay For Understanding Enterprise Funds The Water and Sewer Fund is one of two enterprise funds operated by the town. The other is the Stormwater Utility Fund. Enterprise funds account for the business-like activities of the government. These services are funded with revenues generated from fees and charges rather than taxes.\xa0No profits are made in enterprise funds, and the revenue is used for no other purpose. The Hillsborough Water and Sewer Fund pays for providing water and sewer service. The revenue for the fund comes\xa0primarily from water and sewer charges. Efforts to Provide Water and Sewer Rates Relief The town is committed to finding ways to help relieve customers from the high cost of funding quality, reliable water and sewer service. Efforts include: Rates Study — State law prevents the town from charging different rates to customers based on income. A rate study to evaluate the town’s rate structures and to begin exploring other options to lower costs for customers was completed in Fiscal Year 2024. This study weighed the expected costs for operations and large projects against required revenue between fiscal years 2025 and 2040. It showed the need for increases in rates in Fiscal Year 2025 and in future years. The model helps the town plan for meeting needs and important financial metrics, like days of cash on hand and debt coverage ratio. Lower Minimum Usage — Since 2013, t he town has reduced the monthly minimum number of gallons that customers are charged for water and sewer use from 3,000 to 2,000 gallons. Spreading the reduction over a number of years minimized the effect  of losing revenue. Water Assistance Program — The town started the Water Assistance Program in 2006 to help eligible customers pay water and sewer bills. Candidates are recommended by the Orange County Department of Social Services or a local church. Innovation — Town employees regularly increase efficiency of the water and sewer system through innovation, defer lesser priority projects, and perform much work in house. The United States Environmental Protection Agency has included the town’s wastewater treatment plant in a case study on innovation efforts for the treatment of wastewater. Pump Station Reduction — The town is working to reduce the number of pump stations it operates. The pumps keep sewage flowing in low areas, but they are expensive to maintain and the town has more than a typical municipality. Since 2005, two pump stations have been eliminated. Comparing Water and Sewer Rates Comparing rates is not simple, as the cost of service for each water and sewer system is based on a number of diverse factors. Customers should not expect a gallon of water to cost the same across water systems, notes an Environmental Finance Blog article . Annual Rates Research Annual rates research is conducted by the Environmental Finance Center at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill and the North Carolina League of Municipalities. See: North Carolina Water and Wastewater Rates Dashboard North Carolina Water and Wastewater Rates Report Characteristics for Hillsborough Characteristics for Hillsborough that affect rates include that it: Is in the Central Pines Council of Government region. Is within the Upper Neuse River watershed and\xa0the Falls Lake watershed. Is affected by wastewater treatment mandates under the Falls Lake Rules, which are state mandates to protect and restore the water quality of Falls Lake. Charges for a minimum usage each billing cycle. Additional use is billed per 1,000 gallons. Charges out-of-town customers a rate that is 1.95 times the rate for in-town customers. Serves about 6,910 accounts and an estimated 13,995 people as of the start of Fiscal Year 2026. Had average residential customer use of about 3,440 gallons of water per month in the last fiscal year — July 2024 through June 2025. Staff Title Phone Email Bradford, Emily Budget Director 919-296-9427 Email"]

Name: 57_district_historic_events_arts
Representative_Docs: ["About the Historic District The Hillsborough Historic District was created in 1973 through passage of a preservation ordinance. The district presents a visual history of Hillsborough's development and\xa0includes the town’s commercial core and surrounding residential neighborhoods. The district is\xa0listed on the National Register of Historic Places and\xa0contains more than 100 houses, churches and buildings from the 18th and 19th centuries. Included is development from Georgian- and Federal-style buildings dating to the late 1700s to contemporary construction. The town's historic district overlaps with the National Register of Historic Places district, but it is a separate designation. Historic District Inventory Historic District Map About the Historic District Commission Mission The commission’s mission is to identify, protect and preserve Hillsborough's architectural resources and to educate the public about those resources and preservation in general. It conducts an awards program for noteworthy historic preservation projects in town. Role The commission has a quasi-judicial role. The commission: Hears evidence. Determines relevant facts. Ensures the changes are\xa0not conflicting with the special character of the historic district or landmark. Regulation It is against the law to begin building, moving, demolishing, altering or restoring any structure or site within the Historic District before a\xa0Certificate of Appropriateness\xa0has been issued by the commission or approved by staff as a minor work or ordinary maintenance and repair.\xa0See the Design Standards section on this page. Design Standards Exterior improvements to a site or structure in the Historic District may: Require a Certificate of Appropriateness from the Historic District Commission. Be exempt from permitting. Be approvable as a minor works item by Planning and Economic Development Division staff. Before making any exterior changes to a site or structure, discuss the proposal with town staff to determine the process required for the project and to avoid potential after-the-fact permit penalties and enforcement. These standards and matrices guide the commission's decisions on Certificate of Appropriateness applications: Compatibility Matrix Historic District Design Standards (effective Sept. 3, 2021) Historic District Signage Matrix Membership Composition A majority of the commission’s members have specialized training or an interest in architecture, preservation, archaeology, history or building methods. Terms Members serve up to two consecutive three-year terms. One calendar year must pass before a member may be reappointed after serving two complete terms and any partial term. Members Membership Name Term Expires G Miller Dec. 31, 2028 Hannah Peele — Vice Chair March 31, 2028 Sara Riek Aug. 31, 2026 Will Senner — Chair Nov. 30, 2027 Daniel Widis Nov. 30, 2027 Vacant Vacant Appointed Boards Application Meeting Information Meetings are at 6:30 p.m. the first Wednesday of each month in the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Name: 58_adjustment_leak_leaks_adjustments
Representative_Docs: ['Leak Adjustment Customers may request a bill adjustment if a water line break has resulted in significantly greater charges for a billing period. One of the following is required: A plumber or maintenance person must provide sufficient documentation of a leak. Customers must provide receipts for items purchased for repairs. Customers must request leak adjustments no later than two months after the billing date for the adjustment. Customers are allowed one leak adjustment in a 12-month period. If an adjustment is approved, the customer will be charged an amount based on an average of the previous six months. Name: 59_street_streets_easements_sweeping
Representative_Docs: ['The Public Works Division cleans stormwater catch basins on town-maintained streets annually, using water pressure and a jet vacuum to remove debris from the\xa0curbside drainage systems. To ensure safety and effective sweeping: Maintain a safe distance from the street-sweeping truck during operation. Do not park vehicles on curb and gutter streets during sweeping. Do not place brush, leaves or other vegetation on streets with curbing. Report Issues When reporting an issue, please provide the specific location of the problem, such as the nearest street address or intersection. Representative_Docs: ['Parking Study —\xa0The town is comprehensively studying its parking supply and the demand for parking in the downtown area and West Hillsborough to address existing conditions and future needs. Goals for this project are to: Assess the current supply and demand of parking in downtown Hillsborough. Understand perceptions about parking in the area. Analyze parking needs to proactively address challenges in the downtown parking infrastructure. Identify opportunities to improve the accessibility, visibility and use of parking in the area. Develop strategies to manage existing parking resources with broader community needs and the evolving demands of downtown users. Align parking strategies with previous planning initiatives, including the Comprehensive Sustainability Plan. Create a long-lasting parking management plan that supports existing downtown improvement efforts.', 'Effective parking strategies are essential as the town continues to invest in revitalizing its downtown area, including West Hillsborough.\xa0A strategic parking management plan will allow Hillsborough to maximize the potential of its downtown for commercial, residential and tourism growth. Name: 64_sewer_pipe_relined_odor
Representative_Docs: ['When workers reline the sewer pipes: The sewer service connection from your home to the sewer line will be blocked. Pressure in the sewer line can force water out of toilets and drains. The contractor will communicate directly with residents when service disruption will occur. Service connections will be reopened at the end of the noted workday. Avoid a Sewer Backup On the day your service is disrupted: Do not do laundry, dishwashing, bathing or other activities that use large amounts of water. Keep toilets flushed after use. Place a towel on the rim of your toilet bowl and keep the seat and lid closed. Cover any basement floor drains.', 'Sewer Service Issues When workers reline the sewer pipes: The sewer service connection from your home to the sewer line will be blocked. Pressure in the sewer line can force water out of toilets and drains. The contractor will communicate directly with residents when service disruption will occur. Service connections will be reopened at the end of the noted workday. Avoid a Sewer Backup On the day your service is disrupted: Do not do laundry, dishwashing, bathing or other activities that use large amounts of water. Keep toilets flushed after use. Place a towel on the rim of your toilet bowl and keep the seat and lid closed. Cover any basement floor drains. Odor The curing process for lining a sewer may produce an odor, like glue or a permanent marker. The odor may enter your home through the pipe that connects your home to the public sewer main. The odor is caused by styrene, a naturally occurring chemical, used in the resin of the liners. The airborne concentration levels are minimal and do not pose a health risk. During short-term exposure, the chemical can cause eye irritation, headache and discomfort. The odor will disappear once the pipe lining is fully hardened. Avoid Potential Odor To Avoid the Odor — Run water into sinks, bathtubs and drains to fill the P-trap fitting on pipes, creating a water barrier. About a gallon of water will fill the U-shaped portion of the pipe, which keeps sewer gas and other vapors out of your home or building. If Odor Is Inside — Open windows or use fans to refresh and circulate your home’s air. If Odor Remains — If the odor remains despite the steps above, contact a plumber about fixing a potential open air connection to the sewer. That problem is a health risk from sewer gas entering your home. Additional Items Timeline and Hours — The project is expected to start in November 2025. Once the project moves forward, it is expected to take about six months to complete. Construction activities will be 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. Monday-Friday unless approved in writing by the town. Construction — Noise and traffic disruptions will occur throughout the project. Contractors may need to dig in the road or rights of way to make repairs. They will use manholes or sewer cleanouts in the right of way to reline sewers. One lane of traffic may be closed. If repairs are needed on private property, the contractor will coordinate with the resident. Markings — Workers will place paint markings or flags on the ground to identify underground utilities where digging may occur. Paint markings wash off with time. Please do not disrupt the project markings. Equipment — If equipment or materials are stored on private property, the contractor should have a written contract with the property owner. Post-construction — The contractor will restore areas disturbed by the project.', 'Sewer Service Issues When workers reline the sewer pipes: The sewer service connection from your home to the sewer line will be blocked. Name: 66_sewer_smoke_lines_blockages
Representative_Docs: ["Blocked Sewer Lines The town\xa0assesses sewer blockages to determine the cause and location — whether they are\xa0on the utility or property owner's side\xa0of a\xa0sewer line. Customers may be responsible for the cost of investigation and repairs if their\xa0 actions cause a blockage in the town’s sewer main. Responsibilities The town owns and maintains the sewer main or sewer service line in public rights of ways or easements. It will clear blockages of lines in public rights of ways or easements. The town may require property owners to install or relocate cleanouts to the town's specifications. The town may agree to perform the work but may require reimbursement and the granting of property access from the customer. Property owners are responsible for blockages in the sewer service line between the house or structure on their property and the public right of way or easement.\xa0The town can require installation or relocation of a cleanout at the right of way or easement if one is not present. Sewer Cleanout A sewer cleanout is a capped pipe that allows access to the sewer line for maintenance and inspections. Cleanouts typically indicate the ownership line when located at the\xa0edge of the public right of way or easement.", 'The town’s primary responsibility for the sewer system is to protect the environment by maintaining unobstructed wastewater flow in the collection system’s main lines. Crews routinely do preventative maintenance and repairs, remove roots and clean sewer lines with pressure washers. Every year, staff is required by state mandate to hydraulically clean a minimum of 10% of the gravity sewer lines in the system. Smoke Testing Crews use non-toxic, non-staining vapor resembling smoke to identify leaks in the sewer system. During testing, it is normal to see smoke escaping from roof vents and from sinks or bathtubs that are not used frequently. If smoke appears from the sink or bathtub, try running water down the drain to fill the dry trap. If you see smoke indoors that is not due to a dry trap or that appears to be arising from the ground in your yard, call the Hillsborough Water Distribution and Collection Division at 919-296-9650 to have the problem investigated. The town will notify customers of private connection issues that require repair. The town will address issues with public sewer lines. For town and customers responsibilities, see the town Code of Ordinances, sections 14-21 and 14-22 . For more information, contact the utility maintenance supervisor at 919-296-9652.', 'Sewer Maintenance The town’s primary responsibility for the sewer system is to protect the environment by maintaining unobstructed wastewater flow in the collection system’s main lines. Crews routinely do preventative maintenance and repairs, remove roots and clean sewer lines with pressure washers. Every year, staff is required by state mandate to hydraulically clean a minimum of 10% of the gravity sewer lines in the system. Smoke Testing Crews use non-toxic, non-staining vapor resembling smoke to identify leaks in the sewer system. During testing, it is normal to see smoke escaping from roof vents and from sinks or bathtubs that are not used frequently. If smoke appears from the sink or bathtub, try running water down the drain to fill the dry trap. If you see smoke indoors that is not due to a dry trap or that appears to be arising from the ground in your yard, call the Hillsborough Water Distribution and Collection Division at 919-296-9650 to have the problem investigated. The town will notify customers of private connection issues that require repair. The town will address issues with public sewer lines. For town and customers responsibilities, see the town Code of Ordinances, sections 14-21 and 14-22 . For more information, contact the utility maintenance supervisor at 919-296-9652.']

Name: 67_bwc_situation_adversarial_activate
Representative_Docs: ['Patrol personnel assigned a BWC shall activate their camera as follows: When dispatched to any call for service, officers shall activate their BWC as they begin to respond to the call; when taking any self-initiated activity that could be reasonably anticipated to result in an enforcement contact with a community member, the officer shall activate the camera when they begin the self-initiated activity; officers shall activate or reactivate their cameras immediately upon any situation escalating beyond a normal consensual cooperative contact, becoming adversarial in nature or in any situation where a citizen displays hostility, negative views, or dissatisfaction with police services; officers shall activate or reactivate their BWC any time a person in their custody shows any resistance, makes adversarial statements, or displays any bizarre or unusual behaviors; during any consensual search of a person, building, vehicle or other property (the BWC shall be activated prior to asking for the consent to search); and/or, when serving any type of commitment papers and/or providing short distance transportation to citizens.', 'Personnel assigned or utilizing a BWC are responsible for ensuring the camera is functioning and recording properly prior to and throughout their duty shift. If an employee who is required to use a camera realizes that their camera is non-functional, they must immediately notify their supervisor and secure a replacement for the non-functioning equipment. Patrol personnel assigned a BWC shall activate their camera as follows: When dispatched to any call for service, officers shall activate their BWC as they begin to respond to the call; when taking any self-initiated activity that could be reasonably anticipated to result in an enforcement contact with a community member, the officer shall activate the camera when they begin the self-initiated activity; officers shall activate or reactivate their cameras immediately upon any situation escalating beyond a normal consensual cooperative contact, becoming adversarial in nature or in any situation where a citizen displays hostility, negative views, or dissatisfaction with police services; officers shall activate or reactivate their BWC any time a person in their custody shows any resistance, makes adversarial statements, or displays any bizarre or unusual behaviors; during any consensual search of a person, building, vehicle or other property (the BWC shall be activated prior to asking for the consent to search); and/or, when serving any type of commitment papers and/or providing short distance transportation to citizens. All personnel, regardless of assignment, shall utilize a BWC during: service and execution of a search warrant at a physical location; any warrantless search of buildings, vehicles or other property unless some type of articulable exigency makes it impractical to wait for a camera to arrive on the scene; and/or any situation where the officer sets out with a reasonable expectation that an arrest or enforcement situation may occur, such as attempting to serve warrants. Officers may utilize their BWC in any situation that they feel it is appropriate to do so in order to properly document the events, capture the events for evidentiary or training purposes, or to protect themselves or the department from liability. Officers may deactivate their BWC: when officers have made the initial assessment of a call for service and reasonably believe there is no enforcement action to be taken, have encountered no resistance or adversarial behaviors, and have moved into an investigative phase and are gathering information for reporting purposes or are performing other non-enforcement related activities, such as directing traffic; when any situation in which an enforcement action was or could have been taken has ended and the citizen is no longer in the officer’s presence; upon completion of any traffic stop or citizen contact once the citizen is no longer in the officer’s presence; and/or, during periods of excessive waiting or processing of a cooperative subject in custody, such as waiting at the magistrate’s office. Officers may deactivate a BWC when inside private property and specifically requested to do so by the property owner or person in control of the property if the situation is non-adversarial in nature and there are multiple officers present. Officers may deactivate the BWC if the situation is non-adversarial in nature and they can specifically articulate that doing so was necessary to keep a situation from escalating or was necessary to gain citizen cooperation. Officers who deactivate a BWC in this situation must notify their supervisor about this deactivation immediately upon completion of the event.', 'Procedures Personnel assigned or utilizing a BWC are responsible for ensuring the camera is functioning and recording properly prior to and throughout their duty shift. If an employee who is required to use a camera realizes that their camera is non-functional, they must immediately notify their supervisor and secure a replacement for the non-functioning equipment. All personnel, regardless of assignment, shall utilize a BWC during: service and execution of a search warrant at a physical location; any warrantless search of buildings, vehicles or other property unless some type of articulable exigency makes it impractical to wait for a camera to arrive on the scene; and/or any situation where the officer sets out with a reasonable expectation that an arrest or enforcement situation may occur, such as attempting to serve warrants. Officers may utilize their BWC in any situation that they feel it is appropriate to do so in order to properly document the events, capture the events for evidentiary or training purposes, or to protect themselves or the department from liability. Officers may deactivate their BWC: when officers have made the initial assessment of a call for service and reasonably believe there is no enforcement action to be taken, have encountered no resistance or adversarial behaviors, and have moved into an investigative phase and are gathering information for reporting purposes or are performing other non-enforcement related activities, such as directing traffic; when any situation in which an enforcement action was or could have been taken has ended and the citizen is no longer in the officer’s presence; upon completion of any traffic stop or citizen contact once the citizen is no longer in the officer’s presence; and/or, during periods of excessive waiting or processing of a cooperative subject in custody, such as waiting at the magistrate’s office. Officers may deactivate a BWC when inside private property and specifically requested to do so by the property owner or person in control of the property if the situation is non-adversarial in nature and there are multiple officers present. Officers may deactivate the BWC if the situation is non-adversarial in nature and they can specifically articulate that doing so was necessary to keep a situation from escalating or was necessary to gain citizen cooperation. Officers who deactivate a BWC in this situation must notify their supervisor about this deactivation immediately upon completion of the event.']

Representative_Docs: ['L. Use of Force Review and Investigation', 'Deadly Force — This refers to a firearm and/or any use of force option or tool that is intended or can reasonably be anticipated to cause death or serious bodily injury through its use.', 'Use of force is an example of an incident that may trigger an internal review.\xa0Department\xa0policy requires that use of force is reported for the display of a\xa0weapon toward a person and for any action that goes beyond the reasonably expected use of soft hands for guidance when detaining a person.']

Name: 70_buffer_stream_riparian_buffers
Representative_Docs: ['Riparian Buffers The town enforces a 50-foot vegetated riparian (stream) buffer along streams and the Eno River\xa0to: Reduce erosion. Encourage runoff to soak into the ground. Protect water quality. Use or Impact Must Be Approved Riparian buffers should remain undisturbed if possible.\xa0Clearing and building within a stream buffer requires approval.\xa0Allowable activities are typically minor in impact but require submitting and receiving approval of an analysis showing that no practical alternative exists. Typically allowed are: Impact from roadways, driveways and utilities. Agricultural and forestry activities within a buffer if state guidelines are followed. Resources Hillsborough Unified Development Ordinance Section 6.20.17 .\xa0The town’s riparian buffer rules contain a list of uses categorized as allowable or allowable with mitigation. North Carolina Neuse River Basin Buffer Rule through the Riparian Buffer Protection Program page of the North Carolina Division of Water Resources. Riparian (Stream) Buffers', 'The town enforces a 50-foot buffer of plantings along streams and the Eno River. Prior approval is required to clear, build or make other impacts within a stream or river buffer, also called a riparian buffer. See the Public Infrastructure Permits page for additional information and to request stream identification services. See Riparian (Stream) Buffers . About Stream and River Buffers Buffers along water bodies are undisturbed, naturally forested areas that: Provide wildlife food and habitat. Slow stormwater runoff by allowing it to soak into the ground. Buffers help protect water quality by: Controlling erosion and keeping stream banks from collapsing. Reducing flooding and pollution of waterways. Moderating stream temperature through the amount and quality of sunlight allowed on a waterbody. Woody debris in waterways also protect water quality and provide habitat for aquatic organisms. See the document Woody Debris in Streams and Rivers .', 'Stream Identification and Riparian Buffers The town enforces a 50-foot buffer of plantings along streams and the Eno River. Prior approval is required to clear, build or make other impacts within a stream or river buffer, also called a riparian buffer. See the Public Infrastructure Permits page for additional information and to request stream identification services. See Riparian (Stream) Buffers . About Stream and River Buffers Buffers along water bodies are undisturbed, naturally forested areas that: Provide wildlife food and habitat. Slow stormwater runoff by allowing it to soak into the ground. Buffers help protect water quality by: Controlling erosion and keeping stream banks from collapsing. Reducing flooding and pollution of waterways. Moderating stream temperature through the amount and quality of sunlight allowed on a waterbody. Woody debris in waterways also protect water quality and provide habitat for aquatic organisms. See the document Woody Debris in Streams and Rivers .']
